author: thipperz
title: cacheComponents breaks when streamed content is big enough - What is the limit?
body: Hi, I'm trying to deploy a Next 16 app with cacheComponents enabled. Things seem to work fine in local and preview, but crashes when deployed with syntax errors in the minified nextjs code (unescaped quotes and things like that). I noticed that this error seems to occur when the streamed HTML is too big - but this error did not occur before with cacheComponents disabled. For example (I know this is not best practice) I fetch 1000 results in a page and them render them in a virtualized list of <Card /> components. The page uses 'use cache' and cacheLife. Like I said, it works fine with cacheComponents disabled, but throws these minification errors in prod. I just wanted to know what is the limit here ![Screenshot_5](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4cc78bb8-808f-4438-a2e9-cd80f6fceafa) ![Screenshot_1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9dcba42c-f278-4022-98f5-e7604d1005ce) ![Screenshot_2](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b02537e0-6aca-41ee-97b4-49f53c894ba4) ![Screenshot_3](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/03b74f74-feb6-4c8a-b65a-4a6c7b6890f6) ![Screenshot_4](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/8d210f72-13c4-4ebb-983a-2892eb6e7ad9)

author: immiProgrammer
title: [Turbopack] Windows `next build` fails in standalone mode with `EINVAL: invalid argument, copyfile .
body: ### Summary ### Summary When running `next build` with `output: "standalone"` on Windows using Turbopack (default in Next.js 16+ or explicitly enabled), the build fails during the file copying phase. It attempts to copy a traced file for the `node:inspector` module, but the destination filename contains a colon (`:`), which is invalid on Windows filesystems. This issue persists even in **Next.js 16.1.6**, despite PR #88273 (escape colon in output names) being merged. ### Reproduction Steps 1. Create a Next.js 16.1.6 project on Windows. 2. Enable `output: "standalone"` in `next.config.ts`. 4. Run `next build`. ### Error Log ``` > Build error occurred Error: EINVAL: invalid argument, copyfile 'D:\path\to\.next\server\chunks\[externals]_node:inspector_7a4283c6._.js' -> 'D:\path\to\.next\standalone\apps\web\.next\server\chunks\[externals]_node:inspector_7a4283c6._.js' at ignore-listed frames { errno: -4071, code: 'EINVAL', syscall: 'copyfile', path: 'D:\\path\\to\\.next\\server\\chunks\\[externals]_node:inspector_7a4283c6._.js', dest: 'D:\\path\\to\\.next\\standalone\\apps\\web\\.next\\server\\chunks\\[externals]_node:inspector_7a4283c6._.js' } ``` ### Environment - **OS**: Windows (10/11) - **Next.js**: 16.1.6 - **Package Manager**: pnpm - **Turbopack**: Enabled ### Additional information _No response_ ### Example _No response_
discussion_title: [Turbopack] Windows `next build` fails in standalone mode with `EINVAL: invalid argument, copyfile .

author: abhishekmardiya
title: [SEO] _next Folder Being Crawled by Search Engines
body: ### Summary I’ve noticed that the `_next` directory is being crawled by search engines. Since this folder contains build assets (JS, CSS, chunks, etc.), I believe it shouldn’t be indexed. From an SEO perspective, allowing _next to be crawled may: - Waste crawl budget - Expose unnecessary internal build files - Potentially create duplicate or irrelevant indexed URLs However, I asked AI about this and received the following explanation: > Search engines crawl the `/_next/` folder because your HTML pages reference those JavaScript files, and Google needs them to properly render your site. These files are static build assets, not actual web pages, so it is completely normal for them to appear as “Crawled – currently not indexed” in Search Console. They are required for rendering but are not meant to be indexed. **You should not block `/_next/` in your `robots.txt`**, as doing so could prevent Google from rendering your pages correctly and potentially harm SEO. This behavior is expected and not an issue. However, I’m not fully confident about this explanation and would like confirmation from the community. ### Additional information _No response_ ### Example _No response_
discussion_title: [SEO] _next Folder Being Crawled by Search Engines

author: icyJoseph
body: I guess, as long as these don't return HTML, then it should be fine. Like, the bot fetches that JS to run the page, but it doesn't go and index it. Reverse the question, can you search a page by using "actual code" included in that page - AFAIK, nope. Not a bad idea to use a robots.txt though, it has a use case of course. Keep _next/static reachable though. Some SEO gurú expert might overrule me here though.

author: redat00
title: Cannot create an alias that uses `cd` : `string not in pwd`
body: Hey! I wanted to create a simple function, that would allow me to navigate more easily inside my common folders, and subfolders. For example if I type `ts infra` it takes me to `/home/redat00/Documents/testing/infra`. Here is the code I came up with, that works flawlessly with bash, but not with oh-my-zsh. I think that oh-my-zsh might be interfering with it, preventing it from working. Do anyone have an idea on what the issue might be ? ```zsh function ts() { cd "$HOME/Documents/testing/$1" } ``` The error I get is : ``` ts infra cd: string not in pwd: /home/redat00/Documents/testing/ ``` Thanks in advance!
discussion_title: Cannot create an alias that uses `cd` : `string not in pwd`

author: mcornella
body: Probably `cd` is aliased or a function, but I don't think we do that in Oh My Zsh. Maybe it's a remnant of an old configuration in your zshrc file. You can check with ```sh which -a cd ```
discussion_title: Cannot create an alias that uses `cd` : `string not in pwd`

author: redat00
body: Yeah I think it's not related to oh-my-zsh, but to ZSH itself. The output tells me it's a "shell built-in command" ``` $ which -a cd cd: shell built-in command ```
discussion_title: Cannot create an alias that uses `cd` : `string not in pwd`

author: okapia
body: The exact error message implies that zsh's `cd` command is receiving two arguments. I'm not quite sure how that could be the case for the function you list. In zsh, you can, e.g. `cd 00 01` and it will do a substitution on the current directory, e.g. changing from `/home/redat00/Documents` to `/home/redat01/Documents`. If `cd` could be wrapped with a function or alias, try prefixing it with `builtin` or just quote it, `\cd`. Also try using something like `printf '>%s\n<'` instead of cd to print the arguments. I'd also suggest using a named directory instead of the `cd` wrapper: `hash -d ts=/home/redat00/Documents/testing`. Named directories have the advantage of working anywhere on the command-line and not just with cd, in this case `~ts`.
discussion_title: Cannot create an alias that uses `cd` : `string not in pwd`

body: Thanks for the suggestion! I have no idea why, but for some reason the next day, after a reboot, the function started working as expected. Really absolutely no idea what was going on. But quite happy that it works. But thanks for the suggestions ! I didn't know about named directory.

author: mcornella
body: It looks like the prompt function is exiting directly after running: ```sh __git_prompt_git rev-parse --git-dir ``` which is a call to ```sh GIT_OPTIONAL_LOCKS=0 command git rev-parse --git-dir ``` Try checking if that returns an exit status: ``` GIT_OPTIONAL_LOCKS=0 command git rev-parse --git-dir echo $? ```

I've set the variables for this in my `~/.zshrc` after the `source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh`, but this only loads them on a new terminal session, and if I change the Terraform workspace, the prompt doesn't get updated. author: mcornella
body: It seems like you used double quotes (`"`) instead of single quotes (`'`) to wrap the prompt function call, this is a common issue with shells. With double quotes, the function is called instantly when defining the prompt. With single quotes, the function is called every time the prompt is drawn.

author: mzannoni
title: Plugin for nrfutil completion needs explicit .zshrc sourcing
body: I want to create a plugin that enables `nrfutil` completion fucntionalities. For reference, `nrfutil` is a tool used with Nordic MCUs, and it comes with a completion aid for `zsh` specifically, as explained [here](https://docs.nordicsemi.com/bundle/nrfutil/page/nrfutil-completion/guides/autocompletion_setup.html). The specific instructions by the tool itself: ``` ❯ nrfutil completion install zsh Add the following to your .zshrc file: # From nrfutil completion install [[ -r "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh" ]] && . "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh" It should be added before the zsh completion system is initialized in your .zshrc as it adds an entry to the zsh 'fpath' variable. If the zsh completion system is not initialized in your .zshrc file, you need to add initialization code: # Initialize zsh completion system autoload -Uz compinit compinit ``` (I attach an archive with the `zsh` folder mentioned here just above: [nrfutil_zsh.tar.gz](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24345805/nrfutil_zsh.tar.gz)) Now, if I add the line: ``` [[ -r "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh" ]] && . "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh" ``` in the `.zshrc` before the `plugins=(...)` statement, it works as expected. However, I want to wrap this into a plugin, for better order and portability. I've made a `nrfutil` folder in `$ZSH_CUSTOM/plugins` and added a file `nrfutil.plugin.zsh` with content: ``` [[ -r "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh" ]] && . "${HOME}/.nrfutil/share/nrfutil-completion/scripts/zsh/setup.zsh" # Mark _nrfutil completion function for autoloading autoload -Uz _nrfutil ``` then added `nrfutil` to the `plugins=(...)` statement. This works, but only if I explicitly call `source .zshrc` after a `zsh` is started. What am I missing? How can I prevent having to explicitly source `.zshrc` itself again after `zsh` starts?
discussion_title: Plugin for nrfutil completion needs explicit .zshrc sourcing

author: emcrisostomo
body: I can't reproduce that, and actually, I've been using ZSH as default shell, default terminal in VSCode with Github Copilot (and Codex, and Claude), on at least 2 different macOS versions and 3 different Linux distros and never had any issue. The steps to reproduce are insufficient to reproduce. You mention `path.d`. IIRC that doesn't exist in macOS. macOS has the `path_helper` utility that reads `/etc/paths.d` and at least the latest macOS version apparently correctly treats path items with whitespaces on them. (you can test that by creating entries, then inspecting the output `path_helper -s` and/or inspecting the path entries (e.g.: `print -l ${path}`) > If I disable all the Copilot extensions, then there is no problem: I bet the underlying problem is still there, you just don't see the symptoms. As pointed out, in a stock macOS installation you definitely have `sed`, `tr` and `tail` in the path. Hence, you most likely did something else that changed your environment in a way that fundamentally made those tools inaccessible to the script being executed.

author: arakis
title: Is oh-my-zsh vulnerable to software side-channel attacks?
body: Does oh-my-zsh have any external dependencies? Is it susceptible to side-channel attacks? How strict is the review process for patches? The project has 2.4k+ contributors. How are PRs reviewed in practice? Regardless of the current process, is there a way to opt in to a delayed update mechanism? Disabling auto-update is one option, but if I update manually at an unlucky moment, it does not help. I could pin a specific commit, but one of oh-my-zsh’s strengths is its simple install and update flow. An opt-in delayed auto-update could reduce the risk of breakage. Apologies for the many questions. Open-source projects are currently prime targets for supply-chain attacks, and we are all human - often unpaid maintainers and contributors. oh-my-zsh is widely used by power users, sysadmins, and developers who handle highly sensitive credentials - the keys to critical infrastructure. That is why I’m asking.
discussion_title: Is oh-my-zsh vulnerable to software side-channel attacks?

author: carlosala
body: Every commit in Oh My Zsh is reviewed and merged by either @mcornella, @robbyrussell, or me. There are no other people with merge rights at the moment (2025-10-22), and there are no automated dependency upgrades or anything like that. We take security very seriously. We just participated in [GitHub Secure Open Source Fund](https://resources.github.com/github-secure-open-source-fund/) project and we are applying any finding we discovered during the course (non of them were anything close to critical 🎉) I hope this answers your concerns, feel free to answer if anything else is missing!
discussion_title: Is oh-my-zsh vulnerable to software side-channel attacks?

title: Fuzzy matching causes completion candidates to not display properly
body: Fuzzy matching causes completion candidates to not display properly using the latest (and versions since at least 1.5 years ago) Oh My Zsh on zsh 5.9 included with macOS 15.6.1 (& with earlier macOS versions) or installed from Homebrew core (I presume that all distributions of zsh 5.9, as well as older versions of zsh, suffer from the issue). See <https://github.com/apple/swift-argument-parser/issues/631#issuecomment-3287779901>. I don't know the exact requirements for the failure, but it seems like, if you try to complete after a single `-`, and if a short & long option designate the same option (it might occur for all shorts & longs being distinct, or for multiple shorts or multiple longs designating the same option, etc.), then the display of the candidates below the command line is visually messed up, either way each long, each short, and each description on its own line, repeated multiple times (I've seen 3 repeats, but maybe the repeat count differs under circumstances). Candidates seem to be displayed correctly if you try to complete after `--`. The problem goes away if you disable Oh My Zsh or all Oh My Zsh matchers for completions, e.g. via: ```shell zstyle ':completion:*' matcher-list '' ``` I've attached a completion script that suffers from this problem: [_mas.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/22309822/_mas.txt) (I had to append `.txt` to attach it). Is there any way that existing completion scripts can be made compatible with Oh My Zsh fuzzy matching? If so, how? Either way, is there a GitHub issue that tracks fixing this? Oh My Zsh should be robust enough to not mess up display of valid zsh completion candidates. If the issue cannot be fixed, then should fuzzy matching be removed? Either way, should fuzzy matching be disabled by default until the problem is fixed?
discussion_title: Fuzzy matching causes completion candidates to not display properly

author: livcher
title: robbyrussell theme relies on a weird ZSH formatting behaviour - is it normal?
body: So I've been on a modified robbyrussell theme with the arrow removed for years, and then an update changed it back to standard robbyrussell. No biggie; I went in the theme file and saw this line: `PROMPT="%(?:%{$fg_bold[green]%}%1{➜%} :%{$fg_bold[red]%}%1{➜%} ) %{$fg[cyan]%}%c%{$reset_color%}"` I didn't remember how I modified it last time, or the syntax used, but I decided to try truncating it to: `PROMPT="%{$fg[cyan]%}%c%{$reset_color%}"` This almost achieved the desired result, except the directory name wasn't in bold. I asked Copilot why is that the case and why was the directory name bold before I truncated the prompt and it turned out the bold "leaked" from the arrow. And indeed when I removed the reset_color thing from the original prompt instead, my commands would become cyan and bold. For some reason, fg_bold overrides fg but there's no way to do it in the opposite direction other than resetting the colour. To me this sounds like a hack and I had an urge to submit a PR to change it to `PROMPT="%(?:%{$fg_bold[green]%}%1{➜%} :%{$fg_bold[red]%}%1{➜%} ) %{$fg_bold[cyan]%}%c%{$reset_color%}"` instead, so that the bolding is explicitly defined. But I'm new to this and don't know if it's normal to use hacks like this in OMZ themes. If it is, I'll just leave it at that. But if not, I'd make a PR. So, is it normal to rely on the bold leaking in OMZ themes? Or is it a robbyrussell specific issue?
discussion_title: robbyrussell theme relies on a weird ZSH formatting behaviour - is it normal?

author: mcornella
title: Ideas for in-console notification of security or other high-impact events
body: This thread is going to be a brainstorm of UX features for notifying users within their CLI environment, if such a feature is going to be desirable. cc @carlosala
discussion_title: Ideas for in-console notification of security or other high-impact events

author: mcornella
body: One idea: 1. Create a function within the core codebase for displaying notifications, regardless of what the content is. This notification could be: 1. At startup 2. Deferred X prompts 3. At a specific time of console session 2. There should be some kind of API / request-driven design that gets when some event has happened. We should try to use the platform as much as possible, to avoid having to run our own infra. NOTES to take into consideration: 1. Users are very against any prompt that distracts them from their work ([case in point](https://x.com/vboykis/status/1818332263643336708)). 2. Some themes like powerlevel10k use something called instant prompt, and dislike when content is shown at startup. So at least this should be deferred.
discussion_title: Ideas for in-console notification of security or other high-impact events

author: livcher
body: Good idea, but we need to be careful and sanitise the inputs so that it doesn't become an RCE vulnerability in case the source of notifications is compromised. And make it possible to opt out in a config.
discussion_title: Ideas for in-console notification of security or other high-impact events

author: amirongit
title: why themes are using asterisk (*) to show git status instead of the dedicated emojis?
body: for example, if I have un committed changes in some git repo, robbyrussell theme will render its prompt like this: "PyEnumerable git:(feature/hashed-implementation*)" while it should be like this: "PyEnumerable git:(feature/hashed-implementation) ✗" and what's weird here is that after I do `antigen theme robbyrussell`, it is rendered with the emoji; but when I put it in my zshrc, the former happens! this is relevant parts of my zshrc ```zsh export LANG=en_US.UTF-8 export ZSH="$HOME/.oh-my-zsh" source $HOME/antigen.zsh antigen use oh-my-zsh antigen theme robbyrussell antigen bundle zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions antigen bundle zsh-users/zsh-syntax-highlighting antigen apply # ZSH_AUTOSUGGEST_HIGHLIGHT_STYLE="fg=#4e5165" DISABLE_MAGIC_FUNCTIONS=true DEFAULT_USER=amir # DISABLE_UNTRACKED_FILES_DIRTY="true" source $ZSH/oh-my-zsh.sh setopt no_share_history unsetopt share_history ```
discussion_title: why themes are using asterisk (*) to show git status instead of the dedicated emojis?

title: Theme:gallois Time is gone?!

Hi, i didn't changed here anything, it was running fine with theme gallois but my time at right prompt is gone. Does anybody has a hint? Here is the code: # Depends on the git plugin for work_in_progress() (( $+functions[work_in_progress] )) || work_in_progress() {} ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_PREFIX="%{$fg[cyan]%}[%{$fg[green]%}" ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_SUFFIX="%{$fg[cyan]%}]" ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_DIRTY="%{$fg[red]%}" ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_CLEAN="" # Customized git status, oh-my-zsh currently does not allow render dirty status before branch git_custom_status() { local branch=$(git_current_branch) [[ -n "$branch" ]] || return 0 print "%{${fg_bold[yellow]}%}$(work_in_progress)%{$reset_color%}\ ${ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_PREFIX}$(parse_git_dirty)${branch}\ ${ZSH_THEME_GIT_PROMPT_SUFFIX}" } autoload -U colors && colors #export VCS_PROMPT=hg_prompt_info export VCS_PROMPT=git_custom_status base_prompt="%{$fg[cyan]%}[%~% ]%(?.%{$fg[green]%}.%{$fg[red]%})%B$%b " custom_prompt="" last_run_time="" last_vcs_info="" function pipestatus_parse { PIPESTATUS="$pipestatus" ERROR=0 for i in "${(z)PIPESTATUS}"; do if [[ "$i" -ne 0 ]]; then ERROR=1 fi done if [[ "$ERROR" -ne 0 ]]; then print "[%{$fg[red]%}$PIPESTATUS%{$fg[cyan]%}]" fi } # Combine it all into a final right-side prompt PROMPT='%{$fg[cyan]%}[%~% ]%(?.%{$fg[green]%}.%{$fg[red]%})%B$%b ' function preexec() { last_run_time=$(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f
", time') } function duration() { local duration local now=$(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f
", time') local last=$1 local last_split=("${(@s/./)last}") local now_split=("${(@s/./)now}") local T=$((now_split[1] - last_split[1])) local D=$((T/60/60/24)) local H=$((T/60/60%24)) local M=$((T/60%60)) local S=$((T%60)) local s=$(((now_split[2] - last_split[2]) / 1000000000.)) local m=$(((now_split[2] - last_split[2]) / 1000000.)) (( $D > 0 )) && duration+="${D}d" (( $H > 0 )) && duration+="${H}h" (( $M > 0 )) && duration+="${M}m" if [[ $S -le 0 ]]; then printf "%ims" "$m" else if ! [[ -z $duration ]] && printf "%s" "$duration" local sec_milli=$((S + s)) printf "%.3fs" "$sec_milli" fi } function precmd() { RETVAL=$(pipestatus_parse) local info="" if [ ! -z "$last_run_time" ]; then local elapsed=$(duration $last_run_time) last_run_time=$(print $last_run_time | tr -d ".") if [ $(( $(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f
", time' | tr -d ".") - $last_run_time )) -gt $(( 120 * 1000 * 1000 * 1000 )) ]; then local elapsed_color="%{$fg[magenta]%}" elif [ $(( $(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f
", time' | tr -d ".") - $last_run_time )) -gt $(( 60 * 1000 * 1000 * 1000 )) ]; then local elapsed_color="%{$fg[red]%}" elif [ $(( $(perl -MTime::HiRes=time -e 'printf "%.9f
", time' | tr -d ".") - $last_run_time )) -gt $(( 10 * 1000 * 1000 * 1000 )) ]; then local elapsed_color="%{$fg[yellow]%}" else local elapsed_color="%{$fg[green]%}" fi info=$(printf "%s%s%s%s%s" "%{$fg[cyan]%}[" "$elapsed_color" "$elapsed" "%{$fg[cyan]%}]" "$RETVAL") unset last_run_time fi if [ -z "$info" -a ! -z "$last_vcs_info" ]; then custom_prompt="$last_vcs_info$base_prompt" return; fi if (( ${+VCS_PROMPT} )); then last_vcs_info=$($VCS_PROMPT) if [ ! -z "$last_vcs_info" ]; then [ -z "$info" ] && info=$last_vcs_info || info="$info$last_vcs_info" fi fi [ -z "$info" ] && custom_prompt="$base_prompt" || custom_prompt="$info$base_prompt" } function hg_prompt_info() { unset output info parts branch_parts branch local output="" if ! output="$(hg status 2> /dev/null)"; then return fi local info=$(hg log -l1 --template '{author}:{node|short}:{remotenames}:{phabdiff}') local parts=(${(@s/:/)info}) local branch_parts=(${(@s,/,)parts[3]}) local branch=${branch_parts[-1]} [ ! -z "${parts[3]}" ] && [[ "${parts[1]}" =~ "$USER@" ]] && branch=${parts[3]} [ -z "${parts[3]}" ] && branch=${parts[2]} if [[ ! -z "$output" ]]; then local color="%{$fg[red]%}" elif [[ "${branch}" == "master" || "${branch}" == "warm" ]]; then local color="%{$fg[yellow]%}" else local color="%{$fg[green]%}" fi print "%{$fg[cyan]%}[${color}${branch}%{$fg[cyan]%}]" } setopt PROMPT_SUBST PROMPT='$custom_prompt'

author: halilim
body: Could you edit your snippets? 1. The formatting was messed up. Some parts are in code blocks, others are not. 2. A minimal reproducible example, i.e., by removing irrelevant code. If there are many moving parts, comment everything except the theme and time options. In complicated cases, binary search might help: comment out an arbitrary (but logical) half of the code, try it, if it works, swap the commented halves; once found, rinse and repeat within that half.
discussion_title: Theme:gallois Time is gone?!

author: xieyuschen
title: eval: command not found issue in custom function
body: Hi, recently I tried to define a function to ease my effort to create a version upgrading commit in upstream repo. My problem is that once I execute `upstream engine`, the eval reports error: ``` (eval):1: command not found: go _z:39: command not found: date _z:64: command not found: env ``` And then the zsh plugin features got broken, so I have had to rerun `~/.zshrc` to restore it. Note that I found if I source `~/.zshrc` before eval it works, but it's very slow and i don't think that's a good way. I don't know why eval causes this problem, and what should I do to fix it. Thanks a lot. ```diff + source ~/.zshrc # 3. Run the output of gg script eval "$gg_script" || return 1 ``` #### `~/.zshrc` ```diff + source ~/.config/own_cfg/git.sh ``` #### new added function `upstream`. ``` #!/usr/bin/env zsh function module { remote=$(git remote get-url --push origin | sed 's/^gitlab@//; s/:/\//; s/\.git$//') echo "${remote}" } function revision { tag=$(git tag --points-at HEAD | head -n1) if [ -n "$tag" ]; then rev="$tag" else rev=$(git rev-parse HEAD) fi echo "$rev" } function gg { # Try to get the tag pointing to HEAD, otherwise use commit hash rev="$(revision)" echo "go get ${remote}@${rev}" | clipcopy } function project_name { remote=$(git remote get-url --push origin | sed 's/^gitlab@//; s/:/\//; s/\.git$//') basename=$(basename "$remote") echo "$basename" } function upstream { if [ -z "$1" ]; then echo "Error: upstream path required." return 1 fi module_name=$(module) rev=$(revision) # Correctly call the revision function gg_script="go get ${module_name}@${rev}" # 1. Use z to jump, select first, run gg to get script path=$(z "$1" -l | head -n1 | awk '{print $2}') if [ -z "$path" ]; then echo "No matching path found for '$1'" return 1 fi cd "$path" || return 1 # source ~/.zshrc # 3. Run the output of gg script eval "$gg_script" || return 1 # 4. git status, commit if changed if git diff --quiet && git diff --cached --quiet; then echo "No changes to commit." return 0 fi branch="upstream/$(basename "$module_name")/$rev" echo $PATH git checkout -b "$branch" git add . basename=$(basename "$module_name") git commit -S -m "upstream(${basename}): ${basename}@${rev}" } ```
discussion_title: eval: command not found issue in custom function

author: xieyuschen
body: Updated: My final solution is add my PATH setting up inside `~/.zshenv`, and `source ~/.zshenv` before `eval`. I still don't know why but it solves my problem.
discussion_title: eval: command not found issue in custom function

Please verify config.") is_git_clone_cmd = "git clone" in command_str.lower() should_skip_due_to_existence = False if is_git_clone_cmd: cmd_parts = shlex.split(command_str) target_dir_in_cmd = "" if len(cmd_parts) > 0: try: git_idx = cmd_parts.index("git") clone_idx = cmd_parts.index("clone", git_idx) if len(cmd_parts) > clone_idx + 2: potential_path = cmd_parts[clone_idx + 2] if not potential_path.startswith("-"): target_dir_in_cmd = potential_path except ValueError: pass if target_dir_in_cmd: check_dir_exists_cmd = f"test -d {shlex.quote(target_dir_in_cmd)}" try: proc = system_utils.run_command( check_dir_exists_cmd, run_as_user=target_user, shell=True, capture_output=True, check=False, print_fn_info=None, logger=app_logger ) if proc.returncode == 0: con.print_info(f"Destination for '{item_name}' ('{target_dir_in_cmd}') seems to exist. Skipping.") should_skip_due_to_existence = True elif proc.returncode != 1: con.print_warning(f"Could not check existence for '{item_name}' due to test command error (code {proc.returncode}). Will attempt command.") app_logger.warning(f"test -d for '{item_name}' errored: {proc.stderr if proc.stderr else 'N/A'}") except Exception as e_check_exist: con.print_warning(f"Could not check existence for '{item_name}': {e_check_exist}. Will attempt command.") app_logger.warning(f"Exception checking plugin existence for '{item_name}': {e_check_exist}", exc_info=True) if should_skip_due_to_existence: continue try: system_utils.run_command( command_str, run_as_user=target_user, shell=True, capture_output=True, check=True, print_fn_info=con.print_info, print_fn_error=con.print_error, print_fn_sub_step=con.print_sub_step, logger=app_logger ) con.print_success(f"Applied: {item_name}") app_logger.info(f"Applied enhancement: {item_name}") except subprocess.CalledProcessError as e: if is_git_clone_cmd and e.returncode == 128 and e.stderr and "already exists" in e.stderr.lower(): con.print_info(f"Git clone for '{item_name}' reported destination exists. Skipped.") app_logger.info(f"Git clone for '{item_name}' skipped, dir likely exists.") else: con.print_error(f"Failed to apply: {item_name}. Check logs.") app_logger.error(f"Failed applying enhancement '{item_name}'.", exc_info=False) overall_success = False except Exception as e_cmd: con.print_error(f"Unexpected error applying '{item_name}': {e_cmd}") app_logger.error(f"Unexpected error for '{item_name}': {e_cmd}", exc_info=True) overall_success = False # Copy Dotfiles if target_user_home: project_root = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent if user_wants_to_proceed_with_enhancements: if not _copy_config_file_to_user_home(".zshrc", "zsh", target_user, target_user_home, project_root): con.print_warning("Failed to copy .zshrc.") if omz_install_command or (omz_exists_proc and omz_exists_proc.returncode == 0): overall_success = False if not _copy_config_file_to_user_home(".nanorc", "nano", target_user, target_user_home, project_root): con.print_warning("Failed to copy .nanorc.") else: app_logger.warning("Skipping dotfile copy: target_user_home not determined.") if overall_success: con.print_success("Phase 3: Terminal Enhancement completed.") app_logger.info("Phase 3 completed successfully.") else: con.print_error("Phase 3: Terminal Enhancement completed with errors. Please review the output.") app_logger.error("Phase 3 completed with errors.") return overall_success ``` Basically I got the pkgs to install from this json: ```json "phase3_terminal_enhancement": { "autin": "cargo install atuin", "eza": "cargo install eza", "fd-find": "cargo install fd-find", "zoxide": "curl -sSfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ajeetdsouza/zoxide/main/install.sh | sh", "omz": "sh -c '$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ohmyzsh/ohmyzsh/master/tools/install.sh)'", "zsh-autosuggestions": "git clone https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-autosuggestions ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-$HOME/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/zsh-autosuggestions", "zsh-syntax-highlighting": "git clone https://github.com/zsh-users/zsh-syntax-highlighting.git ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-$HOME/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/zsh-syntax-highlighting", "you-should-use": "git clone https://github.com/MichaelAquilina/zsh-you-should-use.git ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-$HOME/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/you-should-use", "zsh-eza": "git clone https://github.com/z-shell/zsh-eza.git ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-$HOME/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/zsh-eza", "fzf-tab": "git clone https://github.com/Aloxaf/fzf-tab ${ZSH_CUSTOM:-$HOME/.oh-my-zsh/custom}/plugins/fzf-tab" }, ``` for more code you can refer to this project: https://github.com/Salvodif/Fedora-AutoEnv-Setup

author: nasso
body: my understanding is: - by default `git_prompt_{status,info}` does something similar to what `_prompt_{status,info}_async` does in your theme - `_prompt_{status,info}` are being registered as asynchronous handlers and any code inside is already being run asynchronously (including calls to `git_prompt_{status,info}`) - for some reason, "nesting" asynchronous operations like that doesn't work - `zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt no` tells `git_prompt_{status,info}` to always operate synchronously - because `_prompt_{status,info}` are asynchronous already, the call to the now synchronous `git_prompt_{status,info}` won't block/slow down the prompt! the whole point of doing these asynchronous things is to allow the prompt to be shown immediately while the slower things are being computed in the background, and then update the prompt once they are ready. that's why you don't need `git_prompt_{status,info}` to do its own asynchronous magic: you're already doing that in the theme (so that `jj` doesn't slow down your prompt). `zstyle ':omz:alpha:lib:git' async-prompt no` tells `git_prompt_{status,info}` "don't worry about being slow, just print your final output". so if you call other things inside of `_prompt_{status,info}`, make sure they operate synchronously. alternatively (and depending on what you want to show in your prompt), you may create a different function (outside of `_prompt_{status,info}`) and directly call it from `PROMPT="..."`. then, it can have its own async handler without clashing with `_prompt_{status,info}`. i hope that makes things more clear!
discussion_title: jj plugin's prompt and falling back to git prompt

body: Excellent, that's very clear and it makes total sense indeed :) thank you again!

author: gitboyzcf
body: 下面是**在启用 `isolatedDeclarations` 的前提下，优雅生成 d.ts** 的常用做法与注意点（以 Vue + TS 为例），尽量不手写冗长类型： --- ## ✅ 核心思路 `isolatedDeclarations` 要求**每个文件都能独立产出声明**，因此禁止依赖“类型推断链过长/跨文件推断/复杂条件类型展开”。解决方案是： 1. **显式导出类型** 2. **把推断结果“固定住”** 3. **避免运行时值推断出复杂类型** --- ## ✅ 推荐做法 1：显式导出 Props/Emits 类型 ```ts export interface FadeInExpandTransitionProps { group?: boolean appear?: boolean width?: boolean mode?: 'default' | 'in-out' | 'out-in' onLeave?: () => void onAfterLeave?: () => void onAfterEnter?: () => void } ``` 然后： ```ts export default defineComponent({ props: { // ...使用 PropType 显式标注 } as PropType<FadeInExpandTransitionProps>, }) ``` **好处：** d.ts 能稳定生成，且 IDE 友好。 --- ## ✅ 推荐做法 2：`satisfies` 固定推断结果（TS 4.9+） ```ts const props = { group: Boolean, appear: Boolean, width: Boolean, mode: String as PropType<'default' | 'in-out' | 'out-in'>, onLeave: Function, onAfterLeave: Function, onAfterEnter: Function, } satisfies ComponentPropsOptions<FadeInExpandTransitionProps> ``` **好处：** 既保留推断，又能避免“类型漂移”。 --- ## ✅ 推荐做法 3：`defineProps<T>()` + 显式导出 在 `<script setup>` 中： ```ts export interface Props { group?: boolean appear?: boolean width?: boolean mode?: 'default' | 'in-out' | 'out-in' } const props = defineProps<Props>() ``` --- ## ✅ 推荐做法 4：拆分类型文件 在 `types.ts` 中导出： ```ts export interface FadeInExpandTransitionProps { ... } ``` 组件里导入使用。 **好处：**声明文件生成稳定，避免复杂推断链。 --- ## ⚠ 常见问题与规避 | 问题 | 规避 | |------|------| | 通过 `PropType<typeof something>` 推断链很长 | 改用显式 interface | | 条件类型 + infer 组合 | 拆成明确类型别名 | | 导出 default defineComponent 直接推断 | 用 `const X = defineComponent(...); export type Props = ...; export default X` | --- ## ✅ 结论（给你一个最实用的答案） **想“优雅声明组件”，而不是手写长类型：** ✅ 把 props/emits 类型显式抽到 interface ✅ 用 `satisfies` 或 `defineProps<T>()` 固定推断 ✅ 让每个文件声明可独立生成
discussion_title: How to use isolatedDeclarations with defineComponent in typescript

author: tobeyger
title: Focusing text field after navigation, why does it work in Vue but not React or Svelte?
body: I'm scratching my head over a thing that I've noticed when trying to programatically focus an input text field in Vue, React and Svelte. The way I want to set it up is to have a /search page. Whenever the page is mounted or activated, the field should be focused. That is simple, but the problem is that iOS Safari prevents fields from being focused (truly focused with the keyboard showing) without a user interaction triggering it. The funny but also annoying thing is that this works in Vue without issues. I have a `<RouterLink>` that goes to the `/search` page. I then use a ref to the field and focus on that with `this.$refs.field.focus()` in `mounted()` with the Options API. And it just works, the field gets focused and the keyboard shows. But recreating the same logic in React or Svelte, the field never gets the keyboard to show. I can see that the CSS focus state is applied to the field, but no keyboard appears. So I've aksed ChatGPT and Claude about this, and after many failed suggestions and assumptions I think I've reached the conclusion that this is either the fact that Vue Router is synchrounous (?) and that the click of the RouterLink is evaluated and accepted as a user interaction that can be chained to focus the field. Or that the RouterLink itself is constructed in a way that it either through a click or touch event manages to follow through until the new page is mounted and the focus is then accepted there. Does anyone know what kind of quirk in Vue that makes this work there? And is it intended or a happy accident?
discussion_title: Focusing text field after navigation, why does it work in Vue but not React or Svelte?

author: Tiimie1
body: yeah so you've basically figured it out already. vue's reactivity and vue router work synchronously — when you tap a RouterLink, the route change, component mount, and your focus call all happen in the same call stack as the original click event. ios safari sees that whole chain as still being part of the user interaction so it allows the keyboard to open. react breaks this chain. react's useEffect/useLayoutEffect runs after async state updates and reconciliation. by that point safari no longer considers it a trusted user gesture so it suppresses the keyboard. you can see the css focus styles because the element IS focused in the dom sense, safari just won't show the keyboard without that trust chain. its mostly a **happy accident** from vue's design. they chose synchronous reactivity and dom patching for predictability, not specifically for ios safari compat, but it happens to satisfy safari's requirements. the common workaround for react is to focus a temporary hidden input synchronously inside the click handler (which opens the keyboard while still in trusted context), then transfer focus to your real input after the page mounts: ```jsx function SearchLink() { const handleClick = () => { const tmp = document.createElement('input'); tmp.style.position = 'fixed'; tmp.style.opacity = '0'; document.body.appendChild(tmp); tmp.focus(); // keyboard opens — still in trusted context requestAnimationFrame(() => { const real = document.querySelector('#search-input'); if (real) real.focus(); tmp.remove(); }); }; return <Link to="/search" onClick={handleClick}>Search</Link>; } ``` ugly but it works because safari keeps the keyboard open once its already showing.
discussion_title: Focusing text field after navigation, why does it work in Vue but not React or Svelte?

author: gitboyzcf
title: Why does the reactive of vue not allow access to the private properties of the proxy class?
body: When I access private properties in a class and use getter to obtain them, I report an error. I wonder? >TypeError: Cannot read private member #name from an object whose class did not declare it at Proxy.getName
discussion_title: Why does the reactive of vue not allow access to the private properties of the proxy class?

author: skirtles-code
body: Private properties aren't supported with reactive proxies. This isn't something that can be fixed in Vue, JavaScript doesn't allow private properties to be accessed via a proxy. More generally, it is recommended to avoid using classes for reactive data. There are several GitHub issues relating to this. Two specific examples: - https://github.com/vuejs/core/issues/7240 - https://github.com/vuejs/core/issues/8149#issuecomment-1521009456
discussion_title: Why does the reactive of vue not allow access to the private properties of the proxy class?

author: jianxingli
title: @vue-generic 显式指定组件泛型类型，但是不生效 "vue": "^3.5.21"
body: 自定义组件在 setup中声明泛型 组件在使用的情况 期望在组件使用@vue-generic 声明的类型 能正确推导
discussion_title: @vue-generic 显式指定组件泛型类型，但是不生效 "vue": "^3.5.21"

author: TargiX
body: 你这个点基本不在 Vue runtime 本体，而是在 TS/IDE 语言服务（Volar / @vue/language-tools）这条链路上：<script setup generic="..."> 和 <!-- @vue-generic {...} --> 都是类型系统/编辑器在解析，不是运行时特性。官方文档里也明确给了 @vue-generic 的用法示例。 下面给你几个最常见的“看起来写对了但不生效”的原因 + 直接可用的修法。 1) DataSourceType 在 template 里不一定可见：用 import() 写法最稳 你现在写的是： ```typescript <!-- @vue-generic {DataSourceType} --> <ProTable ... /> ``` 这要求 DataSourceType 在当前 SFC 的 template 类型环境里“可解析”。最不踩坑的写法是照官方示例用 import()： ```typescript <template> <!-- @vue-generic {import('@/types').DataSourceType} --> <ProTable :columns="columns" :request="request" /> </template> ``` 官方文档示例就是这种 import('...').Type 形式。 备选方案是：在当前组件的 <script setup> 里显式 import type： ```typescript <script setup lang="ts"> import type { DataSourceType } from '@/types' </script> ``` 2) 你期待的“推导”可能是 反向推导（从模板用法推回 columns 变量）——TS 做不到 @vue-generic 主要影响的是：该组件标签上的 props / slots / emits 的类型检查与补全。 但 TypeScript 不会因为你在 template 里这样用： ```typescript <ProTable :columns="columns" /> ``` 就“反向推导”出 columns 变量应该是什么类型。 所以如果你想让 columns 在你定义它的时候就有 keyof DataSourceType 那种补全，你需要给 columns 自己一个类型上下文，比如 satisfies： ```typescript import type { ProTableColumnsType } from './tableType' import type { DataSourceType } from '@/types' const columns = [ // 在这里写 dataIndex / field 时才会按 DataSourceType 补全 ] satisfies ProTableColumnsType<DataSourceType> ``` 或者用一个小工厂函数制造上下文： ```typescript const defineColumns = <T>() => <C extends ProTableColumnsType<T>>(c: C) => c const columns = defineColumns<DataSourceType>()([ // ... ]) ``` 3) 你引入的 ProTable 可能被“包装”过，导致泛型签名丢了 如果你不是直接 import ProTable from './index.vue'，而是从 index.ts 里导出（比如 withInstall(ProTable)、Object.assign、插件包装），很容易把组件类型变成普通 DefineComponent，泛型构造签名被抹平，那 @vue-generic 就没法套进去。 解决：在需要泛型的地方直接 import 那个 .vue SFC（你截图里 ProTable 自己是 .vue，父组件也尽量这么引）。 4) 版本链路：确认你用的是 @vue/language-tools / Vue - Official 最新（这是关键） 你提到的讨论贴本身就是 Vue org 里的提问（但本质属于语言工具范畴）。 @vue-generic 是否生效，强依赖： VSCode 扩展：Vue - Official 依赖：vue-tsc / @vue/language-tools / typescript 如果这些还是老版本，就会出现“写了注释但完全没反应”。 一套你可以直接试的“最小修复版” ```typescript <script setup lang="ts"> import ProTable from './pro-table/index.vue' import type { DataSourceType } from '@/types' import type { ProTableColumnsType } from './pro-table/tableType' const columns = [ // ... ] satisfies ProTableColumnsType<DataSourceType> </script> <template> <!-- @vue-generic {import('@/types').DataSourceType} --> <ProTable :columns="columns" /> </template> ``` 如果你照上面改完： @vue-generic 依然“完全没任何类型效果”（props 不报错、不补全）——那几乎可以断定是 语言工具链没吃到这个指令（版本/IDE/插件冲突）。 但如果 props 的类型检查开始变严格，而你想要的是 columns 里的字段补全——那就是第 2 点：给 columns 自己加 satisfies/工厂函数上下文就行。
discussion_title: @vue-generic 显式指定组件泛型类型，但是不生效 "vue": "^3.5.21"

author: Fuzzyma
title: attributes that are not defined as props are not converted from camelCase to kebap-case
body: I came across this today and wonder if this is really intended. Every attribute name that is not defined as prop is just converted to lowercase when applied to the component. So things like `ariaControls` become `ariacontrols` instead of `aria-controls` However this does not happen, if the attrubute falls through and hits another component that does define it as prop. I guess this happens because setAttribute ultimately just lowercases the attribute when it hits an html element but otherwise just passes it though as i. So here are some examples: ```html ``` ```vue ``` Every thing you pass to `` is converted to camelCase. So `scope` only contains `dataTestid` and `ariaControls`. But when I now v-bind it to the button, it breaks the html attributes. I suspect not a lot of people come across this since attributes with hyphens are rare and mostly aria or data. So my big question is: is this intended? It feels very wrong to me at least. And if it is intended, why? And could there be an exception for data and aria attributes?
discussion_title: attributes that are not defined as props are not converted from camelCase to kebap-case

author: sunnypatell
body: @Fuzzyma this is a real bug in how `<slot>` attributes interact with `v-bind` on HTML elements. the root cause is in [`packages/compiler-core/src/transforms/transformSlotOutlet.ts`](https://github.com/vuejs/core/blob/main/packages/compiler-core/src/transforms/transformSlotOutlet.ts). when processing `<slot>` attributes, Vue's compiler explicitly camelizes every attribute name: ```ts p.name = camelize(p.name) ``` so `data-testid` becomes `dataTestid` and `aria-controls` becomes `ariaControls` in the scope object. the problem is in the runtime: [`packages/runtime-dom/src/modules/attrs.ts`](https://github.com/vuejs/core/blob/main/packages/runtime-dom/src/modules/attrs.ts) calls `el.setAttribute(key, value)` with the camelized key as-is. the DOM lowercases it to `datatestid` instead of converting back to `data-testid`. Vue has a `hyphenate()` utility in `packages/shared/src/general.ts` but it's never called in the `patchAttr` path. **workaround for now:** destructure the scope and bind explicitly: ```vue <template #trigger="scope"> <button :data-testid="scope.dataTestid" :aria-controls="scope.ariaControls"> Trigger </button> </template> ``` or use a helper: `v-bind="Object.fromEntries(Object.entries(scope).map(([k,v]) => [k.replace(/\B([A-Z])/g, '-$1').toLowerCase(), v]))"`. ugly but works. this should probably be filed as a bug. the compiler camelizes but the runtime never hyphenates back for HTML attributes.
discussion_title: attributes that are not defined as props are not converted from camelCase to kebap-case

author: yooouuri
title: defineModel default value is NOT reactive, buts that's odd
body: So I have a component, where I need to use the v-model in a scenario and one I don't. ```typescript const state = defineModel<CustomerLookupState>('lookup', { default: () =>({ phone_number: '', birthday: null, location_id: null, }), }); ``` And then in my component, I bind the `state` to some form component. In scenario 1, where I bind the v-model to my component, everything is working fine. In scenario 2, I don't need to bind the v-model, I want to use the defineModel and need the default value. As per https://github.com/vuejs/core/issues/10009, it states I should wrap it in `reactive`. That works. This feels weird, because of the use-case that you can use defineModel as a "standalone" ref, but if I define a default value, the defineModel is not reactive anymore...
discussion_title: defineModel default value is NOT reactive, buts that's odd

author: sunnypatell
body: @yooouuri this is by design. `defineModel` compiles to `useModel()` in [`packages/runtime-core/src/helpers/useModel.ts`](https://github.com/vuejs/core/blob/main/packages/runtime-core/src/helpers/useModel.ts), which uses `customRef` internally. the default value gets stored in a plain `localValue` variable, not a reactive proxy. when you do `state.value.phone_number = 'x'`, you're mutating a property on a plain object. the `customRef`'s `set()` is never called (you're not replacing `state.value`, just mutating a nested property), so no reactivity triggers. the fix is to wrap the default in `reactive()`: ```ts const state = defineModel<{ phone_number: string }>('lookup', { default: () => reactive({ phone_number: '' }) }) ``` this makes the inner object a reactive proxy, so property mutations are tracked. the factory function is required (same as standard Vue props). this was confirmed as expected behavior in [vuejs/core#10009](https://github.com/vuejs/core/issues/10009). `defineModel`'s default behaves identically to a prop default, it's not meant to be a standalone reactive store. if you don't need two-way parent binding and just want local reactive state, `ref()` or `reactive()` directly is the simpler option.
discussion_title: defineModel default value is NOT reactive, buts that's odd

author: likeswinds
title: AI-Native Rendering Engine (@vue/compiler-json)
body: # RFC: AI-Native Rendering Engine (@vue/compiler-json) ## Summary Proposing `@vue/compiler-json`, a dedicated core package designed to treat normalized, flattened JSON schemas as high-performance rendering inputs. Unlike standard runtime JSON-to-VDOM mappers, this engine implements **Ahead-of-Time (AOT)** and **Just-in-Time (JIT)** optimizations—specifically Static Hoisting and Patch Flags—to bridge the gap between AI-generated data and Vue’s optimized virtual DOM. ## Motivation As the industry shifts toward **AI-Driven Development**, the primary consumer of UI frameworks is increasingly AI models rather than human developers. 1. **LLM Token Efficiency**: AI models are significantly more reliable and cost-effective when generating structured JSON compared to complex SFC (Single File Component) templates. 2. **Structural Determinism**: JSON schemas eliminate common AI "hallucinations" such as syntax errors, missing closing tags, or incorrect ESM imports. 3. **Streaming UI**: In modern AI workflows (e.g., v0, Bolt.new), UI must be streamed and rendered dynamically. A native JSON compiler allows Vue to render these streams with the same performance as pre-compiled templates, without a heavy build step. ## Detailed Design ### 1. The Normalized Flat Schema To optimize for AI generation and runtime lookup, the compiler utilizes a flat element map: ```json { "root": "vnode_1", "elements": { "vnode_1": { "type": "div", "props": { "class": "container" }, "children": ["vnode_2"] }, "vnode_2": { "type": "span", "props": { "content": "{{ user.name }}" } } } } ``` ### 2. Compiler Optimizations The engine will port core logic from `compiler-dom` to the JSON context: - **Static Hoisting**: Elements marked with `patchFlag: -1` (or detected as static) are hoisted out of the render function to avoid memory re-allocation during re-renders. - **Patch Flags**: The compiler pre-identifies dynamic parts (Text, Class, Props). The runtime uses these bitwise flags to skip diffing for static attributes. - **JIT Render Function**: The compiler generates a highly optimized function body string and instantiates it via `new Function()`, ensuring execution speed is indistinguishable from standard Vue components. ### 3. Proposed API ```typescript import { compileJSON } from '@vue/compiler-json'; // 1. Compile the JSON schema into an optimized render function const renderFn = compileJSON(mySchema); // 2. Use within a Vue component export default { setup(props) { return () => renderFn({ ...props.context }); } } ``` ## Drawbacks - **Security**: The use of JIT compilation (`new Function`) requires a strict security policy and potentially a "no-eval" version for restricted environments. - **Bundle Size**: Adding compiler logic to the runtime build increases the initial library footprint. ## Adoption Strategy This should be introduced as an experimental, standalone package (`@vue/compiler-json`). If the community adopts it as the standard for AI-generated UI or Server-Driven UI (SDUI), it can be integrated deeper into the Vue core. ## Unresolved questions - How to handle Scoped Slots and complex Component Events within the JSON schema? - Integration with Vue DevTools for debugging compiled JSON structures. 抱歉，英文比较差，只能求助AI了，可以看下这个,感觉用vue底层实现优势更大 https://json-render.dev/
discussion_title: AI-Native Rendering Engine (@vue/compiler-json)

author: inzayi
title: Composable global store as data fetching pattern in Vue ⇆ performance and memory impact
body: I've frequently encountered this data fetching pattern in Vue applications and found it convenient initially, but now I view it as an anti-pattern. I'd like to confirm my concerns. If data like products is used only in one specific page or a smart component there's no need for a global store in the first place. However that's not my primary concern here. In a large application with 10–50 such composables that each fetch and manage their own data throughout the app, what is the impact on memory usage? I guess this data is not garbage collected when the user navigates away from a page or component unmounts potentially causing the browser's memory usage to creep up over time. ```typescript // composable/useProducts.ts const products = ref([]) const isLoading = ref(false) const useProducts = () => { const fetchProducts = async () => { try { isLoading.value = true const response = await fetch('https://api.example.com/products') // parsing response and extracting data products.value = data } catch (error) { // generic error handling } finally { isLoading.value = false } } return { products, isLoading, fetchProducts } } ```
discussion_title: Composable global store as data fetching pattern in Vue ⇆ performance and memory impact

author: gimler
title: Access ref from slot in my component
body: I have a component that defines a slot. ``` // MyComponent.vue <template> <form ref="form"> <slot></slot> </form </template> <script setup> import { onMounted, ref, useTemplateRef } from "vue"; const child = ref(null); const form = ref(null); const childRef = useTemplateRef('child'); onMounted(() => { console.log(child.value); console.log(childRef.value); }) </script> ``` When i use this component i define a ref on a child component in the default slot ``` <my-component> <my-child ref="child"> <my-btn ref="btn"> </my-component> ``` i always getting `null`. The form ref works. I found this hack ``` form.value.__vnode.children[0].dynamicChildren[0] ``` ## Why I need to call a method from `my-child` (use defineExpose) when clicking on `my-btn`. ## Question: Is there a better way to access the ref in the slot?
discussion_title: Access ref from slot in my component

author: Mookiepiece
body: Use `slot props` or `provide/inject` instead of `ref` solves your problem. --- Incomplete code snippet using `provide/inject`. ```vue // MyComponent.vue <script setup> const child = ref(), btn = ref(); provide('M',reactive({ child, btn })); </script> ``` ```vue // MyBtn.vue <script setup> const onClick = () => { } const M = inject('M') || {}; watchEffect(()=>{ M.btn = { onClick } }) </script> ``` --- You can even use HTML `id` and `querySelectorAll` to select child elements and dispatch custom events on them.
discussion_title: Access ref from slot in my component

author: rosyatrandom
title: [Proposal] Shorthand for setting classes and inline styles using argments
body: Simply because setting conditional classes and individual inline styles are both slightly annoying, I propose a completely unnecessary shorthand: ```vue // Existing (note -- I am going to disregard kebab/camel casing in the examples <AnyElement style="some-prop: some-val:" /> <AnyElement :style="{ 'some-prop': some-val }" /> // Proposed <AnyElement style:some-prop="some-val" /> // Existing <AnyElement :class="{ 'some-class': some-predicate: }" /> // Proposed <AnyElement class:some-class="some-predicate" /> ``` You're asking 'BUT WHY?' The answer is purely: I just prefer it slightly. Sorry. And if we were to take this all seriously, for some reason, a shorthand for `class:foo` and `style:bar` would be nice. Looking at readily available symbols, let's just say `^foo` and `$bar` for the example: ```vue <AnyElement $some-prop="some-val" ^some-class="some-predicate"/> ```

author: asdlem
title: [Proposal] Official "AI System Prompt" / Skills Standard for Vue ecosystem
body: **Description** I am proposing that the Vue team publishes an official **"AI Skills" / System Prompt definition** file (e.g., `ai-best-practices.md` or similar). **Context & Motivation** Recently, Vercel released an official repository [`agent-skills`](https://github.com/vercel-labs/agent-skills), which defines best practices for AI Agents (like ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor) when writing React/Next.js code. Currently, when developers ask AI to write Vue code, the results are often inconsistent: 1. **Legacy Mix:** AI often mixes Vue 2 Options API with Vue 3 Composition API. 2. **Outdated Syntax:** It may not strictly use `<script setup>` or standard TypeScript patterns unless heavily prompted. 3. **Lack of Standardization:** Every developer has to write their own "System Prompt" to correct the AI. **The Proposal** I suggest adding a dedicated section or a raw Markdown file in the official documentation repo that serves as a **"Single Source of Truth" for AI Context**. This file should explicitly state guidelines for LLMs, such as: * "Always use Composition API with `<script setup lang='ts'>`." * "Use `ref` over `reactive` by default (if that is the recommended stance)." * "Prefer Pinia for state management." * "Use standard naming conventions..." **Benefits** * **Vendor Integration:** Tools like Cursor, Copilot, or V0 can pull this official definition to ensure they generate idiomatic Vue code. * **Community:** Developers can simply point their AI agents to this official URL to get the best results. * **Modernization:** It forces the AI ecosystem to align with the latest Vue 3.x standards. Reference (React example): [Vercel React Best Practices Skill](https://github.com/vercel-labs/agent-skills/blob/main/skills/react-best-practices/SKILL.md)
discussion_title: [Proposal] Official "AI System Prompt" / Skills Standard for Vue ecosystem

author: APGPerson
title: About props
body: When I was learning how to use a ref object as a prop,I couldn't understand why ```js const { foo } = defineProps(["foo"]) watch(foo,()=>{}) // Not work watchEffect(()=>{ foo ... }) //Work well ``` I know it will compile to ```js const prop = defineProps(["foo"]) watch(prop.foo,()=>{}) // Not work watchEffect(()=>{ prop.foo ... }) //Work well ``` But I can't understand why prop.foo can't detected by watch() AI told me it will auto unpack ref data,so prop.foo is just normal object But I have learnt watchEffect() detect releative data by call it's getter or setter,if prop.foo is just normal object,it doesn't have getter or setter,so what causes this Because it's difficult to read all source code about this part for me,so I came to there for help Thanks
discussion_title: About props

author: ismaildasci
body: Hey, I had the same confusion before so let me explain what I learned. When you destructure props like this: ```js const { foo } = defineProps(["foo"]) ``` you basically extract the value at that moment. Its not reactive anymore, just a plain value. Thats why watch() cant detect changes. But watchEffect works different - it tracks whatever you access inside it while running. So when you use `foo` inside watchEffect, Vue still catches it. **What you can do:** Use getter function in watch: ```js const { foo } = defineProps(["foo"]) watch(() => foo, (newVal) => { console.log(newVal) }) ``` Or dont destructure and use props directly: ```js const props = defineProps(["foo"]) watch(() => props.foo, (newVal) => { console.log(newVal) }) ``` Or use toRefs if you really want destructuring: ```js const props = defineProps(["foo"]) const { foo } = toRefs(props) watch(foo, () => { ... }) // works now ``` Basically destructuring = copying value = losing reactivity. Hope this clears it up!
discussion_title: About props

author: APGPerson
body: But according to the [document](https://vuejs.org/guide/components/props.html#reactive-props-destructure) **In version 3.4 and below, foo is an actual constant and will never change. In version 3.5 and above, Vue's compiler automatically prepends props. when code in the same <script setup> block accesses variables destructured from defineProps. Therefore the code above becomes equivalent to the following:** ```js const props = defineProps(['foo']) watchEffect(() => { // `foo` transformed to `props.foo` by the compiler console.log(props.foo) }) ``` `props.foo` doesn't destructuring props If I am wrong, please point it out. Thanks for your answer
discussion_title: About props

author: skirtles-code
body: Props destructuring is a distraction here. You're essentially correct, that it compiles `watch(foo, ...` to `watch(props.foo, ...`, so the real question is why doesn't `watch(props.foo, ...` do what you want? To be a bit more explicit, consider this code: ```js const props = defineProps(['foo']) watch(props.foo, () => { console.log('changed') }) ``` As you noted, this won't work. Here's a Playground showing that: - [Playground](https://play.vuejs.org/#) As well as not triggering the watcher, you may also notice that Vue logs a warning. When we write `watch(props.foo, () => {...})`, we aren't passing the expression `props.foo` to `watch`, we're passing its current value. In my Playground that value is `1`. So we're essentially calling `watch(1, () => {...})`. That's why we get the warning, because `watch` receives a source value of `1` and doesn't know how to watch `1`. Contrast that with what happens when you pass a ref. Say we have something like this: ```js const r = ref(1) watch(r, () => {...}) ``` In this case, the 'expression' for the first argument is just `r`. Again, `watch` won't know about that expression, it will just receive the current value of that expression (this is a normal JavaScript function call, no special Vue magic involved). The current value of `r` is a ref object, and `watch` knows how to watch that: it needs to watch the `value` property of that object. All is well, as that's what we wanted it to do. Now consider this case, similar to the earlier example but with a function wrapper around `props.foo`: ```js const props = defineProps(['foo']) watch(() => props.foo, () => { console.log('changed') }) ``` This will work correctly: - [Playground](https://play.vuejs.org/#) The key thing here is that we're passing the wrapper function to `watch`, not the current value of `props.foo`. When `watch` receives a function it knows how to watch that: it starts tracking and calls the function, very similar to what it does for `watchEffect`. This allows it to track the property access and record it as a dependency of the watcher. In general, Vue's dependency tracking relies on where properties are read. You can think of this as a dot operator, `.`. So when we write `props.foo`, that is reading a property. It needs to be read somewhere that Vue can track it (a more technical description of this is 'it needs to be read inside a reactive effect'). In practice, that means it needs to be inside a function. Being inside a function allows Vue to start tracking and then call the function. Dependencies are then recorded while the function is running. If those dependencies subsequently change then Vue can re-run the function and re-gather those dependencies based on their new values. Going back to the earlier example with a ref called `r`, you can think of `watch(r, ...)` as being a shorthand for `watch(() => r.value, ...)`. When `watch` receives a ref for its source it just wraps it with a function like this. You can see this part in the Vue source code here: https://github.com/vuejs/core/blob/aa9af1deeda38457e33b595a7c68389cf7cf03fd/packages/reactivity/src/watch.ts#L153-L154 While this can sometimes be difficult to follow at first, one 'simple rule' that many of us use to spot mistakes quickly is 'dots should be inside functions'. This is only a guide, it sometimes won't apply, but it comes from the idea that reading properties needs to happen inside reactive effects. So consider these examples: ```js watch(a, ...) // 1 watch(() => a, ...) // 2 watch(a.b, ...) // 3 watch(() => a.b, ...) // 4 ``` 1. The first example is just using `a`. No dot, no function... all is well. 2. The second example has no dot, so it probably shouldn't be using the extra function. No property access occurs inside that function, so nothing will be tracked. 3. The third example does have a dot in `a.b`. This is probably wrong because it isn't inside a function. 4. The last example has both a dot and a function, so that's fine. Props destructuring is an awkward edge case in this regard, but so long as you know that `foo` really means `props.foo` it essentially works the same way, dots inside functions. Likewise, even if you aren't using props destructuring, you get something similar when you write something like `{{ foo }}` in your template. The 'function' in this case is the component's render function, which is generated from the compiled template. The dot is not immediately obvious, but templates have special handling for props and `{{ foo }}` is reading from `props.foo`, which gives us our dot.
discussion_title: About props

author: APGPerson
body: By [sfcplayground](https://play.vuejs.org/#) I have some guess # Why `props` can't use in `watch()` but can use in `watchEffect()` ## First the `props` object is like ```js props = Proxy{ Proxy({foo: 3}, MutableReactiveHandler), // Just like: reactive({foo: 3}) ReadonlyReactiveHandler ) // Shallow and Readonly ``` ## Second The `isRef()` function ```js function isRef(r) { return r ? r["__v_isRef"] === true : false; } ``` ## Third The `isReactive()` function ```js function isReactive(value: unknown): boolean { if (isReadonly(value)) { return isReactive((value as Target)[ReactiveFlags.RAW]) } return !!(value && (value as Target)[ReactiveFlags.IS_REACTIVE]) } ``` ## Fourth `watch()` parse `reactive` data ```js const reactiveGetter = (source: object) => { // traverse will happen in wrapped getter below if (deep) return source // for `deep: false | 0` or shallow reactive, only traverse root-level properties if (isShallow(source) || deep === false || deep === 0) return traverse(source, 1) // for `deep: undefined` on a reactive object, deeply traverse all properties return traverse(source) } ``` --- ## Why it can't directly use in `watch()` ```js console.log(isRef(props)) // Show false console.log(isReactive(props)) // Show true console.log(isShallow(props)) // Show true ``` ### The `watch()` will use `reactive` method to use it,but it is **SHALLOW** so `watch()` can only detect the inner proxy,and it will never change --- ## Why it can directly use in `watchEffect()` ### Because when we try to use `props.foo` it will firstly call `ReadonlyReactiveHandler`'s `get` method,it returns the inner proxy like `reactive` object, and it's `get` method also called, and it call `track` method so `watchEffect()` can know it's a dependence If my guess is wrong,please point it out Thanks

author: tkalmar
title: DevTools guard for jsdom could be improved
body: i recently stumbled upon https://github.com/vuejs/core/blame/623bfb29a23c36bf935e7faefa64d1baa69d465c/packages/runtime-core/src/devtools.ts#L67 after switching my vitest environment from jsdom to happy-dom. Wouldn't it be better if devtools get disabled if NODE_ENV=== 'test'? Only testing for jsdom there is very specific and what happens if jsdom replaces the user-agent? or someone mocks the user-agent with ohter strings? I personally find this very fragile.
discussion_title: DevTools guard for jsdom could be improved

author: krishkhinchi
body: Hi @tkalmar, Good catch. You’re right that relying on a jsdom-specific user-agent check is quite fragile and environment-dependent. Disabling devtools based on `NODE_ENV === "test"` would be a more robust and explicit signal, especially now that alternatives like `happy-dom` are common and user-agent strings can be spoofed or changed. A possible middle ground could be: * Prefer `NODE_ENV === "test"` (or a similar explicit flag) to disable devtools by default in test environments. * Keep the jsdom check only as a fallback for legacy cases. This would make the behavior more predictable and less coupled to a specific DOM implementation. --- If this addresses your concern, please mark this as answered. If not, let me know what part you’d like to dig into further.
discussion_title: DevTools guard for jsdom could be improved

author: tkalmar
body: Thanks for the feedaback! Workaround which worked for me was: ``` beforeEach(() => { // disable hook registration of vue devtools as it would add an setTimeoutSpy call (globalThis as any).__VUE_DEVTOOLS_GLOBAL_HOOK__ = { emit: vi.fn(), }; }); ```
discussion_title: DevTools guard for jsdom could be improved

author: xuyimingwork
title: Should .vue file need access modifiers? like public/protected/private in Java
body: https://github.com/vuejs/rfcs/issues/96 https://github.com/vuejs/rfcs/discussions/517 VueUse now has createReusableTemplate function to create sub component in a file. But I think break large component into sub component is a real requirement, the key problem is: - sub component is only used by large component, if I define sub component in another .vue, it exposed to the outside of the large component, which is not what I need. - if I keep sub component in large component file, making large component file even larger, which is not what I need either. So, my idea is: this is a problem of js missing access modifiers, unlike Java, we don't have public protected private access modifiers. A component in Vue is like a class in Java, a .vue file in Vue is like a .java file in Java. Why not just introduce the best practice of access modifiers in Java, which examined by millions of developer, to solve the problem we facing? Just using tools like eslint, add a new rule and config .vue's access modifiers by comment, maybe enough.
discussion_title: Should .vue file need access modifiers? like public/protected/private in Java

author: xuyimingwork
body: ## **Draft Specification: Vue File-Level Access Control** ### **1. The Modifier Matrix** | Level | Syntax | Scope | | :---- | :---- | :---- | | **Private** | `/** @access private(File.vue) **/` | Accessible **only** by the specific parent file. | | **Strict Protected** | `/** @access protected(Folder) strict **/` | Accessible **only** by files in the Folder/ root. | | **Protected** | `/** @access protected(Folder) **/` | Accessible by Folder/ and **all** its sub-directories. | | **Public** | `/** @access public **/` | Accessible by any file in the project. | ### **2. Project Example & Enforcement** Imagine a **User Management** feature which has a `UserList.vue` component. We want to break it down without leaking internal parts: #### **A. The Private** UserListItem.vue is a small part of the UserList.vue ```html <script setup> /** @access private(UserList.vue) **/ </script> ``` * **Allowed:** UserList.vue * **Blocked:** Any other component #### **B. The Strict Protected (Same Folder)** UserTheme.vue defines the look for the whole feature. ```html <script setup> /** @access protected(User) strict **/ </script> ``` * **Allowed:** components under User folder, like User/UserDashboard.vue * **Blocked:** any other components not directly under User, like: User/Reports/WeeklyReport.vue or Other/OtherReport.vue #### **C. The Protected** UserIcon.vue is used by all components under the User/ folder. ```html <script setup> /** @access protected(User) **/ </script> ``` * **Allowed:** User/UserDashboard.vue **AND** User/Reports/WeeklyReport.vue. * **Blocked:** any other components not under User, like: Other/OtherReport.vue #### **. The Public** a component can import by any component in project. ```html <script setup> /** @access public **/ </script> ``` or no comment(default behavior). * **Allowed:** all component can import * **Blocked:** None ### **3. ESLint Implementation Logic** To enforce this, a custom ESLint rule would perform these three checks whenever an import is detected: 1. **Tag Discovery:** Read the first 10 lines of the *imported* file to find the @access comment. 2. **Path Resolution:** Resolve the (Name) parameter to a physical directory path. 3. **Boundary Check:** * **If Private:** Is the importer's filename exactly Name? * **If Protected:** Does the importer's path start with Name's path? * **If Strict:** Does the importer's path start with Name's path **and** have the same folder depth? ### **4. Why this works for the Vue Ecosystem** * **No Runtime Cost:** The browser never sees these comments; it only affects the development/build phase. * **Incremental Adoption:** You can add this to just *one* folder in your project without needing to update the whole app. * **Zero Boilerplate:** It replaces the need for complex index.js export files or "Barrel" files which often slow down build times.
discussion_title: Should .vue file need access modifiers? like public/protected/private in Java

title: Alternatives to Suspense and top-await components
body: This is slightly related to: https://github.com/vuejs/core/issues/9995 Currently, Vue supports the Suspense-based pattern where the idea is that multiple async or top-await components are made so that they are all loaded together. But I prefer in fact a different UX approach where each component is responsible for its own way how they show "loading" state. The reason is that the component can control the loading look (like gray shapes which match expected look after loading). I find it better if that is done inside the same component because it is easier to maintain both loading look and final look together. So I wonder, are there any other patterns which would enable top-await enabled component which would allow them to show loading while await is being run?

author: akhansari
title: fetch, {#await}, and its result's reactivity.
body: A common pattern is to fetch data (usually based on a reactive id), then edit the data fields, and finally save. As far as I understand, it's not a best practice to fetch data in $effect and it's better to use {#await} blocks. But the issue is that the result's fields are not reactive... What recommendations are there for this use case?
discussion_title: fetch, {#await}, and its result's reactivity.

author: brunnerh
body: `#await` should be mostly superseded by [async Svelte components](https://svelte.dev/docs/svelte/await-expressions), this is still experimental, though. (_Edit_: `#await` might also be deprecated [in the next release](https://github.com/sveltejs/svelte/issues/17687#issuecomment-3901847651).) Fetching data in an `$effect` is fine - a request happening due to a change can be considered a valid side effect. With the await expressions feature you can await in `$derived` which still has issues around deep reactivity, see #17308. As mentioned in the issue you can however always make objects reactive by using a little function like this: ```js export function reactive(obj) { const state = $state(obj); return state; } ``` This can also be used with `#await` if you really want to. ```svelte {#await promise then valueRaw} {@const value = reactive(valueRaw)} ... {/await} ``` #16490 would enable this pattern without the helper function and just `$state`. Another approach would be to extract the contents of the `#await` into a new component, which gives you a new `<script>` scope you can manage state in; this is not very nice to use, adding a lot of indirection. Would not definitively recommend any approach, but I am not a huge fan of `#await` because it creates its own little scope that cannot be accessed from the `<script>`, so you tend to pass the value around a lot or end up extracting the contents to a separate component anyway.
discussion_title: fetch, {#await}, and its result's reactivity.

author: jrmoynihan
title: Attached Transitions/Animations
body: Transitions and animations are great out-of-the-box tools from Svelte, but as Attachments improved greatly on the flexibility of Actions, I think we need a similar update to these directives. We could implement some attachment to run a WAAPI animation on a child component/node and spread it down through props with `createAttachmentKey()`, but one feature is missing: **the ability to synchronize the cleanup function with the lifecycle of the component.** This is a poor, hacky attempt show what I mean: ```svelte <script> // imports... let promise = $state(new Promise<boolean>((resolve) => resolve(true))); const input_attributes: HTMLInputAttributes = { [createAttachmentKey()]: (node) => { // Normal reactivity to state... return () => { node.style.background = 'red'; setTimeout(() => { promise = new Promise(() => false); }, 1000); }; } }; </script> {#await promise} Waiting.... {:then value} {#if value} <Child {...input_attributes} /> {/if} {:catch error} {error} {/await} ``` Does anyone have a better way of doing this? Or do you think it is the purview of the framework to provide an elegant way to synchronize these primitives?
discussion_title: Attached Transitions/Animations

author: karchung0930
title: Passing Data Back to Parent with Scoped Slots
body: Is it possible to pass data into a component and have the component expose that data back for custom rendering? ``` // ComponentA.svelte <script> let { rows } = $props(); </script> {#each rows as row (row.id)} {#if row.dataType === 'custom'} // How to render this? {/if} {/each} ``` ``` // Usage <script> const rows = [ { id: 1, dataType: 'one', }, { id: 2, dataType: 'two', }, { id: 3, dataType: 'custom', }, ]; </script> <ComponentA {rows}> {#Custom (data)} // How to pass this and use the data? {/Custom} </ComponentA> ```
discussion_title: Passing Data Back to Parent with Scoped Slots

author: brunnerh
body: This can be done via [snippets](https://svelte.dev/docs/svelte/snippet), e.g. ```svelte <!-- ComponentA.svelte --> <script> let { rows, customRowTemplate } = $props(); </script> {#each rows as row (row.id)} {#if row.dataType === 'custom'} {@render customRowTemplate(row)} {/if} {/each} ``` ```svelte <ComponentA {rows}> {#snippet customRowTemplate(row)} Custom: {row.id} {/snippet} </ComponentA> ```
discussion_title: Passing Data Back to Parent with Scoped Slots

author: karchung0930
body: @brunnerh Thanks for responding. This is working but it's hard coded? The intention is that when using `ComponentA`, the name of the snippet is not fixed, and it could be also more than one. ``` <script> const rows = [ { id: 1, dataType: 'one', data: 'A' }, { id: 2, dataType: 'custom', dataTypeName: 'TypeA', data: 'B' }, { id: 3, dataType: 'custom', dataTypeName: 'TypeB', data: 'C' }, ] </script> <ComponentA {rows}> {#snippet)} Custom: {row.data} {/snippet} </ComponentA> ``` Here the snippet name should be the `dataTypeName`, this can be anything and not fixed. May I know is it possible to handle this case?
discussion_title: Passing Data Back to Parent with Scoped Slots

author: brunnerh
body: There are different ways of approach this. E.g. create a dictionary for the snippets. ```svelte <script> import ComponentA from './ComponentA.svelte'; const rows = [ { id: 1, dataType: 'one', data: 'A' }, { id: 2, dataType: 'custom', dataTypeName: 'TypeA', data: 'B' }, { id: 3, dataType: 'custom', dataTypeName: 'TypeB', data: 'C' }, ]; </script> {#snippet one(row)} One: {row.data} {/snippet} {#snippet custom(row)} Custom: {row.data} {/snippet} <ComponentA {rows} snippets={{ one, custom }} /> ``` ```svelte <!-- ComponentA.svelte --> <script> let { rows, snippets } = $props(); </script> {#each rows as row (row.id)} {@render snippets[row.dataType](row)} {/each} ```
discussion_title: Passing Data Back to Parent with Scoped Slots

author: brunnerh
body: You could also assign snippet instances to properties on your row objects, if the snippet can exist at the top level. ```svelte <script> import ComponentA from './ComponentA.svelte'; const rows = [ { id: 1, dataType: 'one', data: 'A', template: one, }, { id: 2, dataType: 'custom', dataTypeName: 'TypeA', data: 'B', template: custom, }, { id: 3, dataType: 'custom', dataTypeName: 'TypeB', data: 'C', template: custom, }, ]; </script> {#snippet one(row)} One: {row.data} {/snippet} {#snippet custom(row)} Custom: {row.data} {/snippet} <ComponentA {rows} /> ``` ```svelte <!-- ComponentA.svelte --> <script> let { rows } = $props(); </script> {#each rows as row (row.id)} {@render row.template(row)} {/each} ```
discussion_title: Passing Data Back to Parent with Scoped Slots
