author: code-crush0
title: How to share reactive state across multiple components in Svelte 5?
body: Hi everyone 👋 I'm migrating from Svelte 4 to Svelte 5 and I'm struggling with sharing reactive state across multiple components. In Svelte 4, I used writable stores, but I'd like to use the new runes approach. What I'm trying to do: I have a counter state that needs to be accessed and updated from two sibling components. What I tried: // counter.svelte.js let count = $state(0); export function getCount() { return count; } export function increment() { count++; } But when I import and use getCount() in my components, it doesn't update reactively. Environment: - Svelte 5 - SvelteKit 2 - Node 20 Any guidance would be appreciated. Thanks!
discussion_title: How to share reactive state across multiple components in Svelte 5?

author: forgecrafts
body: The issue is that `getCount()` returns the *value* of `count` at call time, not a reactive reference. In Svelte 5, you should use a getter to preserve reactivity: ```js // counter.svelte.js let count = $state(0); export function getCount() { return count; } export function increment() { count++; } ``` Then in your component, use it inside a **reactive expression** like `$derived`: ```svelte <script> import { getCount, increment } from './counter.svelte.js'; const count = $derived(getCount()); </script> <button onclick={increment}> Clicks: {count} </button> ``` Alternatively, you can export an **object with a getter**, which is the more idiomatic Svelte 5 pattern: ```js // counter.svelte.js let count = $state(0); export const counter = { get count() { return count; }, increment() { count++; } }; ``` ```svelte <script> import { counter } from './counter.svelte.js'; </script> <button onclick={counter.increment}> Clicks: {counter.count} </button> ``` The key takeaway: in Svelte 5, reactivity is preserved through **property access** (getters), not through direct value passing. The object getter pattern keeps the reference reactive without needing `$derived`.
discussion_title: How to share reactive state across multiple components in Svelte 5?

author: GoodBoyNinja
body: Reactivity is lost across modules for plain values, export an object instead and it will work ```ts //counter.svelte.js export let count = $state({ value: 0 }) ``` `$state()` in Svelte 5 proxies objects and their values which makes reactivity work across modules
discussion_title: How to share reactive state across multiple components in Svelte 5?

author: kingpig0117
title: How to properly handle cleanup in Svelte 5 with $effect?
body: Hi everyone! 👋 I'm migrating my project from Svelte 4 to Svelte 5 and I'm a bit confused about how to handle cleanup functions with the new `$effect` rune. In Svelte 4, I used `onDestroy`: ```svelte <script> import { onDestroy } from 'svelte'; let interval = setInterval(() => { console.log('tick'); }, 1000); onDestroy(() => { clearInterval(interval); }); </script> ``` How should I write this in Svelte 5 using `$effect`? I tried this but I'm not sure if it's the correct approach: ```svelte <script> $effect(() => { const interval = setInterval(() => { console.log('tick'); }, 1000); return () => { clearInterval(interval); }; }); </script> ``` Is this the right way? Are there any best practices I should follow? Thanks in advance! 🙏
discussion_title: How to properly handle cleanup in Svelte 5 with $effect?

author: mvpcraft
body: Yes, your approach is correct! 🎉 In Svelte 5, `$effect` supports returning a cleanup function that runs when: - The component is destroyed - The effect re-runs (dependencies change) Your code is the right way to do it: ```svelte <script> $effect(() => { const interval = setInterval(() => { console.log('tick'); }, 1000); // Cleanup function return () => { clearInterval(interval); }; }); </script> ``` ### Key points: 1. **Return a function** from `$effect` for cleanup — this replaces `onDestroy` for most cases 2. **The cleanup runs automatically** when the component unmounts or before the effect re-runs 3. **If you need reactive dependencies**, they're tracked automatically: ```svelte <script> let count = $state(0); $effect(() => { console.log(`Count is now: ${count}`); return () => { console.log(`Cleaning up for count: ${count}`); }; }); </script> ``` ### When to still use `onDestroy`: - When you need cleanup that's NOT tied to reactive dependencies - When working with stores or external subscriptions that don't need to re-run Here's the official docs for reference: https://svelte.dev/docs/svelte/$effect Hope this helps! 😊
discussion_title: How to properly handle cleanup in Svelte 5 with $effect?

author: kingpig0117
body: Thanks, @mvpcraft . Thanks, Channel.
discussion_title: How to properly handle cleanup in Svelte 5 with $effect?

author: GoodBoyNinja
body: Generally effects return "teardown functions" which are called before an effect reruns *and* when the component gets destroyed on the client side. Since your effect contains no states it shouldn't rerun, so in your case the teardown function should work just fine. If your effect had a state in it, the cleanup function would cancel the interval every time that state would change, and then the effect would set up a new interval. https://svelte.dev/docs/svelte/$effect#Understanding-lifecycle Btw, using onDestroy is totally valid in Svelte 5: https://svelte.dev/docs/svelte/lifecycle-hooks#onDestroy If you're using SvelteKit and SSR using effects in your case is correct since it makes the interval a client side code only.
discussion_title: How to properly handle cleanup in Svelte 5 with $effect?

author: brunnerh
body: Using `onMount` for client-side-only teardown is also possible. ```js onMount(() => () => { ... }); ```
discussion_title: How to properly handle cleanup in Svelte 5 with $effect?

author: Asguho
title: How to build a JWT-based session system when you can’t set cookies in a query function?
body: I have a JWT session system using long-lived and short-lived tokens. I’m making a request to a query function, but the short-lived token has expired. My logic is to use the long-lived token to verify the user in the DB, return the requested data, and ideally send back a new short-lived token as a cookie to save a DB trip on the next few requests. However, I’m getting an error: Error: Cannot set cookies in query or prerender functions. Since this is my first time implementing JWT sessions from scratch, am I doing something wrong, or is there a workaround? Also, why does this limitation exist? It feels arbitrary, but I assume it’s for cacheability, though I wouldn't want these requests cached anyway, as they’d return stale data.
discussion_title: How to build a JWT-based session system when you can’t set cookies in a query function?

author: th0rgall
title: Props type inference in Svelte 5: infer most, specify some?
body: Hello, I'm doing a sizeable Svelte 4 -> 5 migration, and I've encountered some components which have many props with default values. In Svelte 4, Svelte/TS could infer the types of the props using their default values. For those props that did not have a default value, it was possible to explicitly specify their type _while keeping inference for the rest_. I haven't found a way to do this in Svelte 5 without explicitly specifying the type for each property. Am I missing something? Here's an excerpt from the codebase with a few default values: Svelte 4: ```ts export let accept: string | string[]; // <-- notice this is the only prop with a specific type definition export let disabled = false; export let maxSize = Infinity; export let minSize = 0; export let multiple = true; ``` In Svelte 5, two changes are needed, as far as I could find: 1. Every property has to be explicitly specified in a separate type definition. 2. Every property which has a default ("fallback") now needs to be made optional, otherwise the component user still has to provide it. ```ts type Props = { accept: string | string[]; disabled: boolean; maxSize?: number; minSize?: number; multiple?: boolean; }; let { accept, disabled = false, maxSize = Infinity, minSize = 0, multiple = true, }: Props = $props(); ``` What doesn't work: ```ts // Svelte 5: if we try to specify a type only for `accept`, we get errors like: // > Property '.......' does not exist on type '$$ComponentProps'. let { .... props with default values.... }: { accept: string | string[] } = $props(); ``` Is the conclusion that Svelte 5 is just unavoidably more verbose in this situation?
discussion_title: Props type inference in Svelte 5: infer most, specify some?

author: krishkhinchi
body: Hii @th0rgall, In Svelte 5, to mix inferred defaults with explicit types, use `Partial` or optional props: ```ts interface ExplicitProps { accept: string | string[] } let { accept, disabled = false, maxSize = Infinity, minSize = 0, multiple = true, preventDropOnDocument = true, noClick = false, noKeyboard = false, noDrag = false, noDragEventsBubbling = false, containerClasses = '', containerStyles = '', disableDefaultStyles = false, name = '' }: Partial<ExplicitProps> & typeof $props() = $props(); ``` * `Partial<ExplicitProps>` → types only required props (`accept`). * `typeof $props()` → keeps inference for defaults. ✅ Rule of thumb: use explicit types for required props, defaults handle inference for the rest. --- If this answer solved all your doubts, please mark it as solved. If not, please tell me your doubt.
discussion_title: Props type inference in Svelte 5: infer most, specify some?

author: th0rgall
body: Thanks for trying to help, but did you check this answer in practice? This doesn't have any effect. `Partial` makes props optional, and `accept` is the only one we _don't_ want to have optional. Moreover, `typeof $props()` isn't even valid TypeScript, let alone that it would do some weird recursion if it worked. [`typeof` docs](https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/2/typeof-types.html#limitations) state you can only use `typeof` on identifiers, not on function call expressions. Honestly, this smells like an AI answer. You have to verify those, always.
discussion_title: Props type inference in Svelte 5: infer most, specify some?

author: sunnypatell
body: @th0rgall yes, svelte 5 is unavoidably more verbose here. this is a known trade-off of the runes design and it's working as intended. **why it works this way**: in svelte 4, each `export let` was its own declaration so typescript could infer types per-variable. in svelte 5, `$props()` merges everything into a single destructuring assignment, and typescript has no syntax for "infer some properties, explicitly type others" in a destructuring pattern. this is actually a [long-standing typescript limitation](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/29526) (156+ comments, still open). when you write `}: { accept: string | string[] } = $props()`, typescript treats that as the **complete** type of the object, so `disabled`, `maxSize` etc. don't exist on it. a few things that were considered and rejected: - **`$props<T>()` generic syntax** existed briefly but was [removed in next.79](https://github.com/sveltejs/svelte/issues/10812) because defaults created bad unions (`$props<{a: number}>()` with `a = ''` gave `string | number`) - **singular `$prop` rune** was [proposed](https://github.com/sveltejs/svelte/issues/9241) (140 upvotes) but closed as not planned since it couldn't handle rest props, `$bindable()`, or reserved keywords like `class` the cleanest pattern for your case: ```ts interface Props { accept: string | string[]; disabled?: boolean; maxSize?: number; minSize?: number; multiple?: boolean; } let { accept, disabled = false, maxSize = Infinity, minSize = 0, multiple = true, }: Props = $props(); ``` for components with many optional props that share patterns, you can use intersection types to reduce repetition: ```ts type Validatable = { maxSize?: number; minSize?: number }; type Props = Validatable & { accept: string | string[]; disabled?: boolean; multiple?: boolean; }; ``` ref: [svelte $props docs](https://svelte.dev/docs/svelte/$props) | [discussion #10690](https://github.com/sveltejs/svelte/discussions/10690) (brunnerh's explanation) | [TS #29526](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/29526) (partial destructuring types)
discussion_title: Props type inference in Svelte 5: infer most, specify some?

author: frederikhors
title: Big function in parent or in children component?
body: I have a component parent (Team.svelte) and a component children (Player.svelte). The question: can I create a big function like the one below in the child components or should I create it in the parent and pass down it as prop to al children? I mean for best performances what is the correct way? Or should I create it in a `module=context` of the children component (but maybe is not possible because I need some variables)? I'm worried to create it in the children (Player.svelte) component because I'm afraid it gets created for each player at runtime instead of one time only in the parent (Team.svelte). What is the idiomatic way in Svelte 5? ```ts const bigFunction = createQuery(() => ({ queryKey: [someKey], queryFn: someFunc, enabled: check, })); ```
discussion_title: Big function in parent or in children component?

author: ismaildasci
body: Good question! The answer really depends on **what data you need** and **how you want to use it**. ### If all players share the same query: Put `createQuery` in the parent (`Team.svelte`) and pass the result down as a prop. This way you only make one request and all children share the same cached data. This is typically what you want for "team-level" data. ### If each player needs their own query: Put `createQuery` in the child (`Player.svelte`). Yes, it creates a query instance per player, but that is intentional - each player might need different data (e.g., `queryKey: ["player", player.id]`). TanStack Query handles caching smartly, so identical queries get deduplicated anyway. ### Performance-wise: Don't worry too much about function creation overhead. JavaScript engines are incredibly fast at creating functions, and Svelte 5's reactivity system is optimized for this. The real performance consideration is **network requests**, not function instances. ### The `context="module"` approach: You're right that it won't work here since you need reactive variables. Module-level code runs once at import time and can't access component state. **TL;DR:** If every player needs the same data → parent. If each player needs player-specific data → child. Let the data requirements drive your architecture, not premature optimization concerns.
discussion_title: Big function in parent or in children component?

author: brunnerh
body: If you declare types, you also have to declare `children`. Not every component accepts this property, so it has to be added manually for those that do. ```ts import type { Snippet } from 'svelte'; interface Props { children: Snippet; // ... } let { children, /*...*/ }: Props = $props(); ``` (If you are wrapping an element, you can make the props extend an interface from `'svelte/elements'`, those already include the `children` property.)
discussion_title: Type Safety in Implicit #snippet props

author: peterreeves
title: Potential `$effect.server` rune?
body: I've had a few projects recently using `adapter-node` where I'm using either streaming requests or websockets, and when state changes I need to push updates to connected clients. I get all excited to use runes, before remembering that `$effect` and `$effect.root` don't run on the server 🥲. I'm currently using old subscriber functions instead, and it's a much worse experience (It really shows how nice runes are by comparison!). I've been wondering if it would be possible (or even a good idea?) to create an `$effect.server` rune. I imagine it using it like this: ```svelte.ts class MyServerState { count = $state(0); connectedClients = new Set(); disposeEffect = null; constructor () { this.disposeEffect = $effect.server(() => { for (const conn of this.connectedClients) { conn.sendData(this.count); } }); } dispose = () => { this.disposeEffect.?(); for (const conn of this.connectedClients) { conn.disconnect(); } connectedClients.clear(); } } const serverState = new MyServerState(); // Elsewhere ... serverState.connectedClients.add(clientConnection); serverState.count += 1; ``` It would ONLY run on the server, and it returns a dispose function just like `$effect.root`, so it would not need to be wrapped in a root like `$effect` does. This probably wouldn't be a good idea for platforms that aren't `adapter-node`, so I guess in those cases it might get transformed into a no-op? Still not sure if it's a great idea. `$effect` is already a footgun so adding another effect rune shouldn't be done carelessly, but it would be _really_ nice to be able to have server-side reactivity with runes 😅
discussion_title: Potential `$effect.server` rune?

author: Ocean-OS
body: Currently, there is no reactivity on the server; all `$state` variables are turned into regular variables and `$derived` variables are reduced to their expression (`$derived(count * 2)` -> `count * 2`), since components on the server can't re-render when state changes occur. Adding an `$effect` rune to the server wouldn't make too much sense, as it'd be inconsistent if components maintained reactivity in `$effect`s but not the template. If you need an effect to run (once) on the server, you can use the (deprecated) [`run`](https://svelte.dev/docs/svelte/svelte-legacy#run) function available in the `svelte/legacy` module. If you're using SvelteKit and need something to **only** run on the server, you can use the [`browser`](https://svelte.dev/docs/kit/$app-environment#browser) export from `$app/environment`.
discussion_title: Potential `$effect.server` rune?

author: peterreeves
body: Yeah that all makes sense, fair enough. `run` won't work as I want things to update multiple times. I've just had the thought that maybe I could try using a third party signal library for my reactive server stuff. That would also provide a nice distinction between browser reactivity (using runes) and server reactivity (using another lib).
discussion_title: Potential `$effect.server` rune?

author: vjpr
title: Easily create derived state
body: When using external stores, you can't just pass scalar state and use it reactively. But if its an object state then you can pass it and use `$derived` to read it. If you want to pass scalar state you have to use a getter function...or you have to create a store. It would be nice to have an easy way to create a store whose value is derived from some state in a component. ``` <script> let value = $state() // a. Won't be reactive when passed to component. let item1 = $derived({value}) // b. Current approach let item2 = $state({value: null}) $effect(() => item2.value = value) // c. Proposal. let item3 = $derivedState({value}) </script> <input bind:value={value} /> <Component {item}/> ``` It's easy to mistake `$derived({value})` thinking it creates reactive state but it does not. This rune would make it easy to pass reactive scalar state to a store...making it easier to extract functionality.
discussion_title: Easily create derived state

author: brunnerh
discussion_title: Easily create derived state

author: vjpr
title: There should be a different rune for creating state with object arg: `$proxy({value: 1})`
body: When state is given an object it compiles to a proxy object which can be passed to an external store which can read a property reactively. When given a scalar, it cannot be passed to a store unless it is in a getter. This is very confusing. Is there a reason why I shouldn't just use objects for all my state so that if I need to pass them to module stores I can?

author: brunnerh
body: > Is there a reason why I shouldn't just use objects for all my state so that if I need to pass them to module stores I can? It is more clunky to use and passing state around should be more the exception rather than the rule, so doing that when you do not really need to does not make much sense. I also do not think introducing a new rune improves the situation in any significant way. If you want to opt out of proxy creation, there is `$state.raw`.

author: arif456
title: Allow reactive state to be declared closer to its usage in templates
body:
## Problem Statement Svelte requires that all reactive state must be declared in the `
{#if modalOpen} modalOpen = false} /> {/if}
{#if tooltipVisible} {/if}
``` You end up constantly scrolling between the script block and template to understand what state controls which part of the UI. ## Desired Outcome **Enable scoped (preferably, but not required), localized state declarations within the template itself**, keeping state close to where it's actually used. This would allow developers to: - Declare state inline, near its usage - Scope state to specific sections of the template - Avoid polluting the script block with UI-local toggles - Make components more self-documenting and easier to refactor ## Possible Implementation (Example) One approach could be something like `{#let}` blocks: ```svelte
{#let modalOpen = $state(false)} {#if modalOpen} modalOpen = false} /> {/if} {/let}
{#let tooltipVisible = $state(false)} tooltipVisible = true}>Hover {#if tooltipVisible} {/if} {/let}
``` **But this is just one possible syntax.** The core request is for *some way* to declare reactive state closer to its usage point in the template, preferably with appropriate scoping. ## Use Cases - Modal/dialog open/close states - Tooltip visibility toggles - Accordion/dropdown expanded states - Form field-specific validation state - Local loading/error states for UI sections - Any UI state that's only relevant to a specific part of the template ## Additional Notes This isn't about replacing script-level state for shared/component-wide concerns—those should absolutely stay in `
discussion_title: Allow reactive state to be declared closer to its usage in templates

author: yeupanhmaj
body: How about separate your logic into a separate file?
discussion_title: Allow reactive state to be declared closer to its usage in templates

author: webJose
body: I don't hate the idea, but I'll play the devil's advocate here. 1. The obvious one: Why do you make such large components? Ideally, break large components into smaller ones. 2. IDE's help. For example, VS Code can do split panes by pressing `Ctrl+|`. Then one pane is for the template, and the other pane is for the script. 3. Is scoping the variable a must? Because I am thinking/guessing the concept of scoping it to a piece of markup is more difficult to achieve. 4. If those reactive values are so scoped, can't you naturally break those scoped pieces into components or snippets? (Do snippets allow state declaration? If not, maybe it is more feasible to ask for variable declaration in snippets than this). Overall, not a bad thing, in my untrained eye. Interested to hear other opinions.
discussion_title: Allow reactive state to be declared closer to its usage in templates

author: arif456
body: All of those you mentioned are unnecessary and complicated workarounds for something that can be achieved MUCH MUCH simpler. Large components in a single file can make code more readable and make it easier to understand how data is flowing. VSCode pane takes up monitor space unnecessarily. Snippets in this case are totally uncalled for and boilerplatey. The compiler already does the parsing and reconstruction of svelte code into js. It's just about taking a piece of declaration from the template area and putting it in the script block during the reconstruction. Simple as that. As for scoping, I mentioned already, "preferable, but not required". Even without scoping, such a feature would DRASTICALLY improve the DX for mundane completely local states such showing a toolip or modal. Thank you for your thoughts anyway.
discussion_title: Allow reactive state to be declared closer to its usage in templates

body: Already addressed by this: - #16490

body: Thanks. This really is it.

author: KieranP
title: How to trigger a function when a $state changes
body: I have a settings.svelte.ts file. Inside, there are dozens of different settings. When one of them changes, I want to be able to trigger a function, e.g. ```ts const ignoredReposSet = fetchAsSet('ignoredPrereleases') // fetchAsSet loads stringified json array, parses into JS Set export const ignoredRepos = $state(ignoredReposSet) ``` ```ts import { ignoredRepos } from 'settings.svelte.ts' function ignoreRepo(repo) { ignoredRepos.add(repo) localStorage.setItem(key, JSON.stringify([...ignoredRepos])) } ``` The issue is that I need to add that localStorage line each time I change ignoredRepos. I'm looking for way to do something like this: ```ts const ignoredReposSet = fetchAsSet('ignoredPrereleases') export const ignoredRepos = $state(ignoredReposSet, (value) => { localStorage.setItem(key, JSON.stringify([...value])) }) ``` ```ts import { ignoredRepos } from 'settings.svelte.ts' function ignoreRepo(repo) { ignoredRepos.add(repo) } ``` That way, if ignoredRepos has one added/removed, the localStorage code will always be run, and it is tied to the $state rune. But obviously this doesn't exist. There is $effect, but I would need to define 15 $effect blocks for 15 settings, because one effect block would call a bunch of local storage sets if just one changes. Any ideas for something cleaner?
discussion_title: How to trigger a function when a $state changes

author: brunnerh
body: > But obviously this doesn't exist. Well, it could exist, but [the PR for it](https://github.com/sveltejs/svelte/pull/15069) is stuck in limbo. Meanwhile, you would usually use an [`$effect`](https://svelte.dev/docs/svelte/$effect) for that. ```js $effect(() => { localStorage.setItem(key, JSON.stringify([...ignoredRepos])); }); ``` As long as you read the object deeply (which `JSON.stringify` does), the effect will trigger on any change to the object. (Note that the `$effect` will trigger at least once on component mount. So if e.g. the `ignoredRepos` state is invalid at that point, you might have to guard against that. Note that the guard condition has to be stateful as well, otherwise the `$effect` will not rerun once the condition changes.)
discussion_title: How to trigger a function when a $state changes

author: KieranP
body: The issue with $effect is that with 15 settings, I'd need 15 $effect blocks, which isn't great. And if I put them all in one $effect block, changing one setting would cause the whole set to run, which isn't great either. So either way, not a very clean pattern. Shame that #15069 is stalled, it would have been exactly what I needed.
discussion_title: How to trigger a function when a $state changes

author: webJose
body: Just use a persisted state implementation, like the `svelte-persisted-state` NPM package. The `runed` package also has an implementation.
discussion_title: How to trigger a function when a $state changes

author: brunnerh
body: Yes, the `$state` + `$effect` can be extracted to a function or class, though usually at the cost of having to access the value via a property instead of directly. But that is probably still better than duplicating `$effect` everywhere.
discussion_title: How to trigger a function when a $state changes

title: What is the recommended way to load environment variables securely in Deno for production?

author: KhalilBensaha
body: Deno recommends using native environment variables via Deno.env.get() and controlling access with the --allow-env permission. For local development, you can use a .env file with --env-file, but in production, environment variables should be injected by the deployment platform (Docker, systemd, CI/CD, or cloud provider), not loaded from files at runtime. I hope this answer helps you, good luck

author: ismaildasci
body: Hey! The issue is with how you're configuring the `script` and `interpreter` fields - they're conflicting with each other. When you set both `script: "deno"` and `interpreter: "deno"`, PM2 gets confused because it's essentially trying to run `deno deno run start ...` which doesn't work. Here's what should work: ```js module.exports = { apps: [{ name: 'blog', script: 'npm', args: 'run start', cwd: './' , instances: 1, exec_mode: 'fork', // use fork mode, cluster doesn't work well with Deno autorestart: true, restart_delay: 3000, max_restarts: 10, watch: false, // recommend disabling in production max_memory_restart: '800M', log_date_format: 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss', error_file: './logs/error.log', out_file: './logs/output.log', env: { NODE_ENV: 'production', PORT: 3000, HOST: '0.0.0.0', NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED: '1' } }] }; ``` A few things I changed: 1. **Removed the interpreter stuff** - just let PM2 call `npm run start` directly (or `deno task start` if you have that set up) 2. **Changed to fork mode** - cluster mode doesn't play nice with Deno since it uses Node's cluster module internally 3. **Disabled watch** - generally better for production; you can enable it for dev if needed Alternatively, if you want to call deno directly: ```js script: 'deno', args: 'task start', interpreter: 'none', ``` The `interpreter: 'none'` is key here - it tells PM2 not to wrap the command with another interpreter. Also heads up: the `--compat` flag was removed in Deno 2.x, so you don't need that anymore. Let me know if this works!
discussion_title: how can make the `pm2` config file works in the `deno` for the `nextjs` project?

author: charles-dyfis-net
title: Is building a binary-reproducible vendor directory feasible?
body: Howdy! I'm working with the [Nix](https://nixos.org/nix/) build system; one of its characteristics is that for security reasons, only build steps that know up-front what they expect their outputs to be (provided as a hash) are allowed network access. Unfortunately, `vendor/` directories include `meta.json` files that include information about which library versions were available at download time, _including those not requested by the lockfile_. This means that building a vendor directory out of a lockfile has unstable output, expected to change over time, so using the vendor directory's hash is expected to break whenever the set of library versions available for download is updated. This specific problem I could work around by just deleting every entry from `"versions"` that isn't actively in use, and pointing `"latest"` only at the selected item. That said -- has anyone else gone down this path, to know if there are more gotchas waiting? Alternatively, is there an intended path to build a completely binary-reproducible vendor directory from a lockfile?
discussion_title: Is building a binary-reproducible vendor directory feasible?

author: anntnzrb
body: Binary-reproducible vendor directories aren't currently feasible because JSR's meta.json includes ALL available versions at download time, not just the lockfile version. Your suggested workaround is correct: post-process the vendor directory to remove extra version entries from meta.json and point 'latest' only to the used version. Alternatively, use `deno compile` which properly bundles all metadata for offline use.
discussion_title: Is building a binary-reproducible vendor directory feasible?

author: acrodrig
title: Deno Open Telemetry data to a simple file log
body: I have a project where having a grafana server is overkill, but I still would like to have all the OTEL data collected go to a file (per day for example). Is this possible without writing (much) code? How would I go about? I googled, I asked the LLM powers that be, and I have not been able to formulate an answer for myself. Thanks.
discussion_title: Deno Open Telemetry data to a simple file log

author: anntnzrb
body: Deno's OTEL only exports to OTLP endpoints, not files directly. Create a minimal HTTP server (~15 lines) that receives OTLP JSON and writes to files: ```typescript const data = { spans: [], logs: [] }; Deno.serve({ port: 4318 }, async (req) => { const body = await req.json(); body.resourceLogs?.forEach(r => r.scopeLogs.forEach(s => data.logs.push(...s.logRecords))); body.resourceSpans?.forEach(r => r.scopeSpans.forEach(s => data.spans.push(...s.spans))); Deno.writeTextFileSync(`otel-${new Date().toISOString().split('T')[0]}.json`, JSON.stringify(data)); return Response.json({ partialSuccess: {} }); }); ``` Then set `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:4318` and `OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL=http/json`.
discussion_title: Deno Open Telemetry data to a simple file log

author: acrodrig
body: This is very nice, thanks for taking the time to write it
discussion_title: Deno Open Telemetry data to a simple file log

author: luckyman0026
body: You could try using a simple file exporter in OpenTelemetry. Most OTEL SDKs support exporting traces or metrics to a local file instead of sending them to a full server. For example, in Deno, you could write a small script to collect OTEL data and append it to a file per day. Even a basic implementation that writes JSON lines to a .log file can work. This way, you avoid setting up Grafana but still capture all the data for later analysis.
discussion_title: Deno Open Telemetry data to a simple file log

author: softshipper
title: What's the recommended way to implement gRPC in Deno?
body: Hi Deno community! 👋 I'm looking to implement gRPC in a Deno project and wanted to understand the current best practices. **Questions:** - What's the officially recommended approach for using gRPC with Deno? - Are there specific Deno-native libraries that are preferred over community ports? - Are there any known limitations or considerations when using gRPC with Deno compared to Node.js? - Any examples or starter templates available? I've seen various community packages but would appreciate guidance on the most stable and maintainable approach. Thanks in advance!

author: anntnzrb
body: Use `npm:@grpc/grpc-js` - officially supported since Deno 1.44 (May 2024). ```ts import * as grpc from 'npm:@grpc/grpc-js'; import * as protoLoader from 'npm:@grpc/proto-loader'; const packageDef = protoLoader.loadSync('./service.proto'); const proto = grpc.loadPackageDefinition(packageDef); const client = new proto.MyService( 'localhost:50051', grpc.credentials.createInsecure() ); ``` No Deno-native library needed - the standard Node.js gRPC client works directly via npm compatibility.

author: kuboon
title: Allow Processing Continuation with 'Not Found' Status Instead of Throwing `NotCapable`
body: Deno's fine-grained permissions are fantastic. However, many existing packages (especially npm packages) require read access to many environment variables and files that are not practically necessary for their operation. This forces us to use `--allow-read `(all) or `-A`, which essentially ruins Deno's permission model. I feel that the situation would be much improved if there were an option to simply continue processing with a "not found" status instead of throwing `NotCapable` when read or env permissions are denied. In the context of the rapidly increasing use of the stdio MCP server, Deno can prohibit unauthorized access by properly restricting permissions. Wouldn't this be a significant selling point?

author: foremtehan
title: Docker alpine issue: sh: deno: not found
body: Need some help for solving the error: `sh: deno: not found` my main Dockerfile is similar to: ``` FROM denoland/deno:bin-2.5.6 AS deno-stage FROM alpine:3.19 COPY --from=deno-stage /deno /usr/local/bin/deno ``` then inside docker container: ``` $ docker run -it test sh / # which deno /usr/local/bin/deno / # deno sh: deno: not found / # deno --version sh: deno: not found ``` why?
discussion_title: Docker alpine issue: sh: deno: not found

author: anntnzrb
body: The `denoland/deno:bin-*` images contain glibc-compiled binaries incompatible with Alpine's musl. Either use the official Alpine image `FROM denoland/deno:alpine-2.5.6`, or copy glibc libs from distroless: ```dockerfile FROM denoland/deno:bin-2.5.6 AS deno-bin FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc AS cc FROM alpine:3.19 COPY --from=cc /lib/*-linux-gnu/* /usr/local/lib/ COPY --from=cc /lib/ld-linux-* /lib/ RUN mkdir /lib64 && ln -s /usr/local/lib/ld-linux-* /lib64/ ENV LD_LIBRARY_PATH="/usr/local/lib" COPY --from=deno-bin /deno /usr/local/bin/deno ```
discussion_title: Docker alpine issue: sh: deno: not found

author: Aziz-Dhiab
title: Generic new constraint instead of DynamicallyAccessedMembers
body: I was working with ```Activator.CreateInstance<T>()``` and was looking for examples of how it is used. I found a lot of public generic methods that uses it and decorate the generic parameter with `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers(DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicParameterlessConstructor)]` This got me curious. Is there a reason for not adding `where T : new()` so that calling such method cause a compilation error instead of a runtime error. Example: ```cs public sealed class MyObject { public MyObject(int id) { } } MyObjecto = null; _ = LazyInitializer.EnsureInitialized<MyObject>(ref o); ```
discussion_title: Generic new constraint instead of DynamicallyAccessedMembers

author: MichalStrehovsky
body: > Is there a reason for not adding `where T : new()` so that calling such method cause a compilation error instead of a runtime error. You mean a reason we didn't add `where T : new()` to `Activator.CreateInstance<T>`? It would be a breaking change at this point. It's very unfortunate the API wasn't decorated with `T : new()` from the beginning, but that ship has sailed. We didn't want to break everyone just because of trimming (it would be breaking for everyone, whether they use trimming or not). For trimming analysis, `new()` and `DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicParameterlessConstructor` are interchangeable (the `T` that is constrained with `new()` will meet `DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicParameterlessConstructor` requirement so one can pass such `typeof(T)` to APIs that are marked with DynamicallyAccessedMembers without having to also put `DynamicallyAccessedMemberTypes.PublicParameterlessConstructor` on the T itself).
discussion_title: Generic new constraint instead of DynamicallyAccessedMembers

author: Aziz-Dhiab
body: Thank you for you explanation, so just to clarify the breaking change is the compile time error that would have been a runtime error anyways or is it a binary breaking because adding `where T : new()` will produce a different IL
discussion_title: Generic new constraint instead of DynamicallyAccessedMembers

author: MichalStrehovsky
body: Yes, compile time error for something that might not actually be reachable at runtime. E.g. ```csharp public object Activate<T>() => CanUseDefaultConstructor<T>() ? Activator.CreateInstance<T>() : CustomFactory<T>(); ```
discussion_title: Generic new constraint instead of DynamicallyAccessedMembers

author: 0x90CH
title: Does AOT still expose lots of .NET metadata?
body: I think that fewer people are interested in AOT in order to make reverse engineering their program more difficult (prevent usage of dnSpy etc), and I just discovered this blog post: https://blog.washi.dev/posts/recovering-nativeaot-metadata/, which states that AOT exposes a lot of .NET metadata. Is this still the case, or has it been fixed? With AOT, you assume you have a fully-fledged native program and not necessarily a ReadyToRun mix. At least that's the impression given in the docs for people who don't necessarily know exactly what's behind it.
discussion_title: Does AOT still expose lots of .NET metadata?

author: huoyaoyuan
body: They are not "exposed". They are just present to organize the OOP type system to work. > I think that fewer people are interested in AOT in order to make reverse engineering their program more difficult This is a side effect and never a goal. > Is this still the case, or has it been fixed? There's nothing to fix. AOT is not designed as an obfuscator. > With AOT, you assume you have a fully-fledged native program and not necessarily a ReadyToRun mix. The post is incorrect here. ReadyToRun was created from NativeAOT, although it went production earlier. The initial commit was https://github.com/dotnet/corert/pull/5943 . Because of the same origin, NativeAOT and ReadyToRun shares some auxiliary data structures.
discussion_title: Does AOT still expose lots of .NET metadata?

author: 0x90CH
body: If the goal wasn't to have a fully-fledged native program. What was the goal then?
discussion_title: Does AOT still expose lots of .NET metadata?

author: huoyaoyuan
body: It is a fully-fledged native program. What do you mean here?
discussion_title: Does AOT still expose lots of .NET metadata?

author: 0x90CH
body: The blog assumes that it leaks .NET metadata. Data which wouldn't exist in a native program written in a native language. Is this a misunderstanding or am I missing something?
discussion_title: Does AOT still expose lots of .NET metadata?

author: huoyaoyuan
body: > The blog assumes that it leaks .NET metadata. Data which wouldn't exist in a native program written in a native language. Such data exists for any type system present in runtime, including C++ vtable and RTTI information. Again, it's never a "leak". They are unavoidable to make things work.
discussion_title: Does AOT still expose lots of .NET metadata?

author: 0x90CH
body: Thank you for taking the time to explain this to me
discussion_title: Does AOT still expose lots of .NET metadata?

author: coding-red-panda
title: Blazor pages from a Plugin
body: Hello, I've had a previous discussion here related to the `IServiceProvider` not loading a registered class correctly, and this was caused by DNNE and the ACL that Kestrel uses. Now I am running into what I believe to be a similar issue, and I was wondering if someone encountered this before, or has a better solution to what I am doing. ## The Setup The main application is written in C++. We use DNNE to load C# .NET 10 ClassLibraries as plugins to that application. Each ClassLibary has a specific entrypoint that we call (Usually our `AppService`) class to "start". One of these plugins starts a Kestrel webserver and is supposed to server Blazor pages. ## The Issue The out of the box setup for Kestrel manages to load the `MudBlazor` components and pages, but static assets such as the CSS and JS files from both MudBlazor and Blazor itself are not loaded/served by Kestrel. ## The Workaround I need to do the following to make this all work: ```csharp namespace My.PlugIn.PluginName { public class AppService : ServiceWithSettings { public override void StartService() { _logger.LogInformation("Starting SiteGateway Configuration Service..."); _cts = new CancellationTokenSource(); // get the data path from the DataStore. var dataStore = DataStore.ExecuteInTx(tx => tx.GetKeySubtree("services.Platform")); var dataPath = dataStore["services.Platform.dataPath"]; // Create and configure the Kestrel webserver. // This will also act as our main dependency injection container for the plugin. _webHost = Host.CreateDefaultBuilder() .ConfigureWebHostDefaults(webBuilder => { var pluginPath = Path.GetDirectoryName(typeof(AppService).Assembly.Location)!; webBuilder // Set the content root to the base directory of the plugin. wwwroot will be next to it. .UseContentRoot(pluginPath) .UseWebRoot(Path.Combine(pluginPath, "wwwroot")) // Configure logging for the Kestrel webserver. .ConfigureLogging(logging => { logging.ClearProviders(); logging.AddConsole(); logging.AddPlatformLogger(PluginAPI.Log); logging.SetMinimumLevel(LogLevel.Debug); }) // Configure the services for our plugin .ConfigureServices((_, services) => { ConfigureContainer(services, dataPath.ToObject<string>()!); _logger.LogInformation("SiteGateway Configuration Plugin Services configured"); }) // Configure the Kestrel webserver itself. .Configure(ConfigureKestrelApplication) // Configure the Kestrel webserver behavior .ConfigureKestrel((_, options) => { var myQOptions = options .ApplicationServices .GetRequiredService<IOptionsMonitor<MyQServerOptions>>() .Get(MyQOptions.SiteGateway); // Register Kestrel to listen on the configured port. // This uses only HTTP traffic, but this is internal communication only. options.ListenLocalhost(myQOptions.ClientPort); _logger.LogInformation("SiteGateway Configuration Plugin Kestrel configured"); }); }) .Build(); // Start the web host in the background try { _logger.LogInformation("SiteGateway Configuration Plugin Kestrel Started"); _webHost.Start(); } catch (OperationCanceledException) { _logger.LogInformation("Kestrel webserver was cancelled."); } catch (Exception ex) { _logger.LogError(ex, "Kestrel encountered an error"); } base.StartService(); } private void ConfigureContainer(IServiceCollection serviceCollection, string dataPath) { serviceCollection // Register all services here // Register the controllers in Kestrel so they can be mapped. .AddControllers() // Wipe out the Assembly Registration and do it manually .ConfigureApplicationPartManager(apm => { // Clear any parts that ASP.NET Core registers on its own. // This prevents the "Default ACL" from seeing your controllers. apm.ApplicationParts.Clear(); // Add the assembly from the current (Isolated) context. var assembly = typeof(AppService).Assembly; apm.ApplicationParts.Add(new AssemblyPart(assembly)); }) // Register Newtonsoft JSON support as the default provider. .AddNewtonsoftJson(options => { // Optional but commonly needed options.SerializerSettings.NullValueHandling = NullValueHandling.Ignore; options.SerializerSettings.ReferenceLoopHandling = ReferenceLoopHandling.Ignore; }); // Register Blazor and MudBlazor serviceCollection.AddServerSideBlazor(); serviceCollection .AddRazorComponents() .AddInteractiveServerComponents(); serviceCollection.AddMudServices(); } private static void ConfigureKestrelApplication(IApplicationBuilder app) { // Add a developer exception page for detailed errors (local debugging only) app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage(); // Enable Static files middleware for Blazor app.UseStaticFiles(); // Serve _framework files from the plugin directory var pluginPath = Path.GetDirectoryName(typeof(AppService).Assembly.Location)!; var frameworkPath = Path.Combine(pluginPath, "_framework"); if (Directory.Exists(frameworkPath)) { app.UseStaticFiles(new StaticFileOptions { FileProvider = new PhysicalFileProvider(frameworkPath), RequestPath = "/_framework" }); } // Add the standard middlewares app.UseRouting(); app.UseAntiforgery(); app.UseEndpoints(endpoints => { // Add our Blazor pages endpoints.MapRazorComponents<App>() .AddInteractiveServerRenderMode(); // Add our controllers endpoints.MapControllers(); // Add the Blazor Hub endpoint for server interactivity endpoints.MapBlazorHub(); }); } } } ``` The above allows me to actually work with MudBlazor, but it still fails to load the core Blazor functionality such as `blazor.web.js` To make the latter work, I need to manually copy the assets after the build to include them in our package installer: ```csharp <Target Name="PostBuild" AfterTargets="PostBuildEvent"> <PropertyGroup> <SharedFolder>$(ProjectDir)..\bin\$(Configuration)\shared.net</SharedFolder> <OutputFolder>$(ProjectDir)..\bin\$(Configuration)</OutputFolder> </PropertyGroup> <Exec Command="dotnet publish "$(ProjectPath)" -o "$(SharedFolder)" -c $(Configuration) --no-build" /> <ItemGroup> <DeleteFiles Include="$(SharedFolder)\*.deps.json;$(SharedFolder)\*.lib;$(SharedFolder)\*.h" /> </ItemGroup> <Delete Files="@(DeleteFiles)" /> <ItemGroup> <TargetFiles Include="$(SharedFolder)\$(TargetName).*;$(SharedFolder)\$(DnneNativeBinaryName).*" /> </ItemGroup> <Move SourceFiles="@(TargetFiles)" DestinationFolder="$(SharedFolder)\.." /> <!-- Copy wwwroot folder --> <ItemGroup> <WwwRootFiles Include="$(SharedFolder)\wwwroot\**\*.*" /> </ItemGroup> <Copy SourceFiles="@(WwwRootFiles)" DestinationFiles="@(WwwRootFiles->'$(ProjectDir)..\bin\$(Configuration)\wwwroot\%(RecursiveDir)%(Filename)%(Extension)')" SkipUnchangedFiles="true" /> <!-- Find and copy blazor.web.js from NuGet packages --> <ItemGroup> <BlazorWebJsFiles Include="$(NuGetPackageRoot)**\blazor.web.js" /> </ItemGroup> <MakeDir Directories="$(OutputFolder)\_framework" /> <Copy SourceFiles="@(BlazorWebJsFiles)" DestinationFolder="$(OutputFolder)\_framework" Condition="'@(BlazorWebJsFiles)' != ''" /> <Copy SourceFiles="..\..\plugin-common-cs\Plugin.runtimeconfig.json" DestinationFiles="$(ProjectDir)..\bin\$(Configuration)\$(TargetName).runtimeconfig.json" UseHardlinksIfPossible="true" /> </Target> ``` The above copies the output files to a shared folder, including all DLL dependencies etc. I need to manually extract and copy the Blazor web JS file, otherwise Kestrel returns 404. Can...someone explain why I need to do it this way, and Kestrel can't serve `blazor.web.js` directly? Isn't this file supposed to be a core thing of the Blazor package?
discussion_title: Blazor pages from a Plugin

author: TheRealQuantam
title: Binary Buffer Library API Review
body: I work with binary file formats a lot and have many projects that all need to read or write binary files with varying endians. While the tools to do this already exist in the .NET API, they are verbose and I'm notoriously stingy; if I can write a reusable class to allow me to do a task with less code I will. I've been using a binary buffer class I wrote for a while now, but I'm currently looking at refactoring and polishing it to release it on NuGet, as it fits a niche where nothing else I could find exists. This discussion is to critique the API design, since I won't be able to change it anymore once it's on NuGet. The refactored library contains two main types: a FixedBuffer ref struct that wraps a user-supplied `Span<byte>` and is intended for reading/writing fixed-size structures, and a DynamicBuffer class that dynamically allocates memory as needed as the buffer grows. Both have very similar APIs, and resemble BinaryReader/BinaryWriter; they use stream semantics as that is the easiest way to read/write a list of different fields one after another. The key difference from the Reader/Writer classes is that most read/write functions have LE and BE variants. Additionally, all read/write functions take a parameter which specifies whether to advance the position, allowing read functions to serve as both read and peek. As they are backed by memory rather than streams, they also have some functions of lists. Apart from the BinaryReader/BinaryWriter-like functions they have: Length, Position, AsSpan, Seek, and WriteFill. DynamicBuffer also has Capacity, EnsureCapacity, TrimExcess, and Clear. In addition to individual values, sequence reads can be made into `IList<T>` or `Span<T>`. Writes can be made from `IEnumerator<T>`, `IEnumerable<T>`, `IReadOnlyList<T>`, and `ReadOnlySpan<T>`. Additionally, sequences of a data type can be read via functions to get an `IEnumerable<T>` starting at the current position (separate position variable from the buffer object), allowing the use of LINQ functions for reading arrays. There is a different such function for each data type and endian. In addition to general feedback of the API design, I have a couple questions: - Should it support explicit indexing for bytes? If so what would the semantics of this be? Would indices be absolute, or relative to the current position? - Do the write functions need the advance parameter (currently they have it), or is that only useful for read functions?
discussion_title: Binary Buffer Library API Review

author: 0UserName
title: Dynamically change array length
body: Out of curiosity. ```csharp internal readonly ref struct UnsafeResizeScope(object[] array) : IDisposable { /// <summary> /// See: <see href="https://github.com/dotnet/coreclr/blob/master/src/System.Private.CoreLib/src/System/Runtime/CompilerServices/RuntimeHelpers.CoreCLR.cs#L297">RuntimeHelpers.RawArrayData</see> /// </summary> private class RawArrayData { public uint Length; // Array._numComponents padded to IntPtr //#if TARGET_64BIT public uint Padding; //#endif public byte Data; } private readonly int _length = array.Length; public void Resize(int length) { Unsafe.As<RawArrayData>(array).Length = (uint)length; } /// <inheritdoc/> public void Dispose() { Resize(_length); } public UnsafeResizeScope(object[] array, int length) : this(array) { Resize(length); } } ``` This is a wrapper that allows dynamic resizing of an array. For example, we have an array, change its length, perform some operation on it (such as copying data elsewhere), and then restore its original length. While this approach appears to work, after approximately 100000 iterations, an `ExecutionEngineException` is thrown. Why does this happen? I suspect the issue is related to the `GC`, but it’s still unclear exactly how.
discussion_title: Dynamically change array length

author: tannergooding
body: Because you've corrupted GC data and potentially made memory accessible that is not meant to be accessible. Possibly overwriting other data, particularly if the GC decides to copy or move the now incorrectly sized array. Do unsafe things, bad things can happen.
discussion_title: Dynamically change array length

author: 0UserName
body: > GC decides to copy or move the now incorrectly sized array Is there a way to avoid this? By specifying GC to not touch the array for the duration of the operation. > and potentially made memory accessible that is not meant to be accessible Do you mean data beyond the set length? Are memory areas simply becoming writable and causing access collisions?
discussion_title: Dynamically change array length

author: tannergooding
body: > Is there a way to avoid this? By specifying GC to not touch the array for the duration of the operation. No. This is fundamentally unsafe, there is no way to make it safe. You are completely in undefined behavior territory when you resize an array, because now you're improperly tracking the backing allocation. In the case you undersize the array (tracking less than was actually allocated), then anything looking at that will be missing data. If the GC decides to copy it, then you will now be missing data and have corrupted the copy. Additionally, this should've just been handled with span and creating a sliced view over the data, so it is safe. In the case you oversize the array (tracking more than was actually allocate), then you risk things touching memory they shouldn't. This could be the GC copying the data, it could be something else reading an element it now thinks is valid. Such memory could be illegal to access (page isn't mapped), it could belong to another object, etc This is fundamentally incorrect code, it should never be written. > Do you mean data beyond the set length? Yes. > Are memory areas simply becoming writable and causing access collisions? All memory is always possible to "attempt" to write. Such memory may actually be writeable which leads to corruption or may be readonly/unmapped and lead to some kind of access fault.
discussion_title: Dynamically change array length

author: huoyaoyuan
body: A closer explanation why it breaks: Objects are stored compactly on GC heap. GC scans objects one by one on the heap, and computes the location of next object simply by `pPrevious + pPrevious->Length + objectHeaderSize`. If you modify the length field, GC will loose the track of next object, and crash when attempting to manipulate subsequent object. In the luckiest case, the length can happen to cover the next object exactly and direct GC to the second next object. This is totally unreliable since GC can place objects in arbitrary order onto the heap.
discussion_title: Dynamically change array length

author: tannergooding
body: This is also an implementation detail of the current CoreCLR GC implementation. It is not a guarantee, it is subject to change at *any time*, and may differ for other runtimes or GC implementations (Mono, Unity, etc). Every bit of the code in the original example is relying on implementation detail and undefined behavior, it is simply code that users should **never** write.
discussion_title: Dynamically change array length

author: jkotas
body: ` Unsafe.As<RawArrayData>(array).Length = (uint)length;` will *always* produce intermittent crashes, data corruptions and hangs on current CoreCLR. There is no way to make it reliable. I understand that having ability to resize an array would be convenient. You can find number of discussions about that in this repo. Unfortunately, there is no way to implement a reliable API like that without severe performance and security tradeoffs. A reliable API like that would be either very slow and/or we would have to add overhead to perf critical parts of the runtime to make that API reliable.
discussion_title: Dynamically change array length

author: Clockwork-Muse
body: As a side note; virtually no computer language allows you to resize an array, because similarly to the GC there are practical realities for tracking available memory, and leaving space "off the end" would most often be wasting it.
discussion_title: Dynamically change array length

author: eacp
title: Consider pooling cryptographically secure randomness for `Guid.NewGuid()` instead of calling the OS for each Guid for increased throughput.
body: Currently, each call to `Guid.NewGuid()` make a call to the OS to get 16 cryptographically secure random bytes. This can be expensive and can be optimized by using a thread-static pool of random bytes or `Guid`s. I wrote a proof of concept with a benchmark: ```cs using System.Buffers.Binary; using System.Runtime.CompilerServices; using System.Runtime.InteropServices; using System.Security.Cryptography; namespace Eacp.FasterGuid; internal sealed class RandomGuidPool { private readonly Guid[] pooledGuids; private int current; private static readonly int versionByte = BitConverter.IsLittleEndian ? 7 : 6; private const int variantByte = 8; private RandomGuidPool(int poolSize = 128) { this.pooledGuids = new Guid[poolSize]; // Force fill on first use current = poolSize; } [MethodImpl(MethodImplOptions.AggressiveInlining)] private Guid NextGuid() { // Fill the pool if used all if (current >= this.pooledGuids.Length) { FillGuids(this.pooledGuids); current = 0; } // Get the next guid Guid g = this.pooledGuids[current]; // Clear so it can be leaked this.pooledGuids[current] = default; // Advance the pointer current++; // Normalize by editing the raw bytes // TODO: Asign the _c and _d fields directly // when nested in the Guid class unsafe { // Get a view to the in memory bytes // by using the raw pointer to the guid in the local stack var gBytes = new Span<byte>(&g, sizeof(Guid)); // Version 4 gBytes[versionByte] = (byte)((gBytes[versionByte] & 0x0f) | 0x40); // Variant 10 gBytes[variantByte] = (byte)((gBytes[variantByte] & 0x0f) | 0xa0); } return g; } // TODO: Convert to unsafe interop [MethodImpl(MethodImplOptions.AggressiveInlining)] private static void FillGuids(Span<Guid> guids) { Span<byte> guidsBytes = MemoryMarshal.AsBytes(guids); RandomNumberGenerator.Fill(guidsBytes); // TODO: Replace with Interop.Get... and use an unmanaged pointer } #region Thread static helper [ThreadStatic] private static RandomGuidPool? threadInstance; internal static Guid NewRandom() { threadInstance ??= new RandomGuidPool(); return threadInstance.NextGuid(); } #endregion } ``` This can be a nested class within https://github.com/dotnet/dotnet/blob/release/10.0.103/src/runtime/src/libraries/System.Private.CoreLib/src/System/Guid.Unix.cs or https://github.com/dotnet/dotnet/blob/release/10.0.103/src/runtime/src/libraries/System.Private.CoreLib/src/System/Guid.cs if we also want to pool Windows. Here are the benchmark results: ``` BenchmarkDotNet v0.13.8, macOS 26.2 (25C56) [Darwin 25.2.0] Apple M4, 1 CPU, 10 logical and 10 physical cores .NET SDK 10.0.102 [Host] : .NET 10.0.2 (10.0.225.61305), Arm64 RyuJIT AdvSIMD DefaultJob : .NET 10.0.2 (10.0.225.61305), Arm64 RyuJIT AdvSIMD | Method | Mean | Error | StdDev | Ratio | RatioSD | Allocated | Alloc Ratio | |------------- |-----------:|----------:|----------:|------:|--------:|----------:|------------:| | GuidNewGuid | 185.544 ns | 0.6783 ns | 0.5296 ns | 32.95 | 0.17 | - | NA | | PooledRandom | 5.631 ns | 0.0239 ns | 0.0223 ns | 1.00 | 0.00 | - | NA | ``` Benchmark code: ```cs [Benchmark] public Guid GuidNewGuid() { return Guid.NewGuid(); } [Benchmark(Baseline = true)] public Guid PooledRandom() { return RandomGuidPool.NewRandom(); } ``` It looks like for Windows there is a specific system call for Guids (COM?), but for Unix it is just a plain call to the OS for random bytes, which can be pooled for performance. The thread static approach is similar to `Random.Shared` but uses a generator that pools OS provided randomness.
discussion_title: Consider pooling cryptographically secure randomness for `Guid.NewGuid()` instead of calling the OS for each Guid for increased throughput.

author: tannergooding
body: This doesn't really make the call 5ns, it just amortizes the remaining cost up front. When the buffer empties, you'd then incur more cost for that call to populate the buffer. The benchmark, particularly given the error/stddev shown, is most likely not doing enough queries to ensure that this cost is also included as part of the measurement I believe there's also potential security and other concerns from buffering what are intended to be cryptographically secure bytes, particularly long term for the process. CC. @GrabYourPitchforks, @bartonjs
discussion_title: Consider pooling cryptographically secure randomness for `Guid.NewGuid()` instead of calling the OS for each Guid for increased throughput.

author: jkotas
body: https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/123540 is switching NewGuid to use getrandom API in glibc when it is available. Linux kernel and glibc optimized getrandom API recently by having a stash of random bytes in user mode that is equivalent to the optimization you are suggesting: https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/123540#discussion_r2724271323
discussion_title: Consider pooling cryptographically secure randomness for `Guid.NewGuid()` instead of calling the OS for each Guid for increased throughput.

author: eacp
body: Interesting multiple people arrived at the same 'pre cache' idea. I am curious as to why it is considered insecure to store them in a private field and delete them as it is used? Is it possible for attackers to access arbitrary memory? Is't the result of `Guid.NewGuid()` going to be stored somewhere anyways?
discussion_title: Consider pooling cryptographically secure randomness for `Guid.NewGuid()` instead of calling the OS for each Guid for increased throughput.

author: tannergooding
body: The OS can do various things like ensuring pages are protected or isolated that can help mitigate certain attack vectors. It is much harder to do that with general application memory, especially user controlled memory. And while there is some aspect of "you may already be compromised if the user can access process memory", there remain many potential avenues that could put you at risk for user memory. The attack vector is a bit bigger when you have a "pool" of anything, particularly a pool of cryptographic bytes. Its less problematic for individual values. I would defer to one of the security experts I tagged above to go into detail and whether or not those are actual concerns, however.
discussion_title: Consider pooling cryptographically secure randomness for `Guid.NewGuid()` instead of calling the OS for each Guid for increased throughput.

discussion_title: Can one compute `TupleHash` (SHA-3 derived function) in .NET using just .NET API?

author: teo-tsirpanis
body: Implementing TupleHash using BCL APIs is not currently possible, because it requires cSHAKE, which is not available. Supporting cSHAKE in .NET would require platform support in at least two of Windows, OpenSSL, Apple, or Android crypto APIs. It is available on Windows, but not yet on other platforms (see openssl/openssl#28358).

author: Vincent-X-Zhang
title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.
body: Recently, I have been using `PipeReader` to parse large files. There is a `ReadOnlySequence` with a length greater than 800 * 1024 * 1024 that needs to be converted to an array using `ToArray`. Even after properly calling `Advance` and `Complete` on the reader, I still found a large amount of Gen2 memory fragmentation. The following issue was discovered in the [source code](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/main/src/libraries/System.IO.Pipelines/src/System/IO/Pipelines/StreamPipeReader.cs): ```csharp private BufferSegment AllocateSegment(int? minimumSize = null) { BufferSegment nextSegment = CreateSegmentUnsynchronized(); var bufferSize = minimumSize ?? BufferSize; int maxSize = !_options.IsDefaultSharedMemoryPool ? _options.Pool.MaxBufferSize : -1; if (bufferSize <= maxSize) { // Use the specified pool as it fits. int sizeToRequest = GetSegmentSize(bufferSize, maxSize); nextSegment.SetOwnedMemory(_options.Pool.Rent(sizeToRequest)); } else { // Use the array pool int sizeToRequest = GetSegmentSize(bufferSize, MaxBufferSize); nextSegment.SetOwnedMemory(ArrayPool<byte>.Shared.Rent(sizeToRequest)); } return nextSegment; } ``` `nextSegment.SetOwnedMemory(ArrayPool<byte>.Shared.Rent(sizeToRequest));` rent the array from `ArrayPool<byte>`. But i cannot found when and where it return back to the pool. I mean it may lead to memory leak.
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: EgorBo
body: > I mean it may lead to memory leak. Not returning a rented array to its pool doesn't cause memory leaks alone, just makes it useless (compared to normal gc allocation). The array seems to be returning to the pool inside `BufferSegment.ResetMemory()`
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: Vincent-X-Zhang
body: > The array seems to be returning to the pool inside BufferSegment.ResetMemory() It does! The following code shows that memory may not be freed due to the timing of GC operations and object references. ```csharp using System.Buffers; var buffers = Enumerable.Range(1, 200_000).Select(x => new Buffer()).ToArray(); await Task.Delay(5000); Console.WriteLine("Begin to return"); foreach (var buffer in buffers) { buffer.Reset(); } Console.WriteLine("Returned"); // Adding this line of code will allow the memory to be properly reclaimed // GC.Collect(); Console.ReadLine(); public class Buffer { private byte[]? _bytes; public Buffer() { ArrayPool<byte> pool = ArrayPool<byte>.Shared; _bytes = pool.Rent(4 * 1024); } public void Reset() { ArrayPool<byte>.Shared.Return(_bytes!); _bytes = null; } } ```
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: Clockwork-Muse
body: Returning an array to the pool won't free it - it retains a reference to it so that it can be reused (up to a limit). That's why it's a pool. Objects without references aren't "immediately" collected - GC runs are expensive, so if the system isn't hurting for free memory it's just going to leave it until it thinks it needs more. You're allocating less than 1GB there, so probably the GC is just going to shrug (until you force the collection, at which point it releases all the unpooled arrays). If you actually need to manage an object lifetime, implement `IDisposable` and use it properly. > There is a `ReadOnlySequence` with a length greater than 8 * 1024 * 1024 that needs to be converted to an array using `ToArray` That's 8MB, which you can likely get as a contiguous array, but you probably shouldn't be. `ReadOnlySequence` and the pipe API areas are intended to be "no additional memory" APIs. Attempting to extract a contiguous array removes all the advantages, and you may as well just have read it as an array in the first place, without dealing with pipes. (now, depending on the data in question you may be stuck with it, but somehow I doubt it)
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: Vincent-X-Zhang
body: > GC runs are expensive, so if the system isn't hurting for free memory it's just going to leave it until it thinks it needs more. It seems to be a garbage collection strategy rather than a memory leak issue. > There is a ReadOnlySequence with a length greater than 8 * 1024 * 1024 that needs to be converted to an array using ToArray Sorry, I made a mistake. It should be 800 * 1024 * 1024 (800MB). I've corrected it now. Here's the situation: I attempted to deserialize a large binary file using `Pipe`, possibly exceeding 2GB. Simplified, each block follows this format: length + data. After writing the code, I tested performance with an approximately 800MB file (split into two blocks: the first was several dozen KB, the second was 800MB). I discovered that memory usage remained at 1.7GB, which was truly puzzling. So I analyzed the memory data with `Windbg` and found a massive `byte[]` on LOH (which is obviously correct). But at the same time, there were also numerous `BufferSegments` and `byte[]` on Gen2. Given `Pipe`'s excellent performance previously, this made me suspect it might be a memory leak.
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: Clockwork-Muse
body: Right, it probably that high if you've saved off a copy into a new array, because that's ~2x of 800MB. > Simplified, each block follows this format: length + data. How nice. Just because it's that format, though, doesn't mean that you need to create a copy of each block as an array. What you want to do is spin through the sequence, getting `Span<byte>` chunks, so that no additional copies are created. Hopefully this also means you don't need to buffer the entire file in memory as well. Total allocated memory could be less than the total size of the file, depending on what you're doing with the data. For best ease of use of working with your data you would probably want to `Slice` per block, which would then pass the sub-sequence to whatever block-parsing code.
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: Vincent-X-Zhang
body: You're right! I shouldn't try to put all the data into a single array. But can you give me some good advice on the data type for those chunks? I mean, `BufferSegment` is internal, and `List<byte[]>` is not very convenient to use. There's no existing type that can accurately and simply describe this type.
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: Clockwork-Muse
body: > But can you give me some good advice on the data type for those chunks? Ideally, nothing; if you can avoid it, don't pass the chunks around at all. > `List<byte[]>` is not very convenient to use I mean, using that would negate the entire point, since that would be copies of the data. No, the output of [`ReadOnlySequence.Slice(...)`](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.buffers.readonlysequence-1.slice?view=net-10.0) is... another `ReadOnlySequence`. Which is what you want, because the source is over some segmented chunks of memory (regardless of what it's backed by), and you don't want to have to account for that yourself (presuming that the data is longer than a single segment). So slice from after the length header for that number of bytes, then iterate through that subsequence parsing and processing it as normal. Ideally, you can just use `ReadOnlySpan`s from segments, but occasionally you may need to copy to a _temporary_ pooled array (if data spans two or more segments) - one that should be returned before advancing to the next token. `SequenceReader` may be helpful here. The entire point is to have no significant portion as a raw binary array copy.
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: Vincent-X-Zhang
body: I see! # class prototype ```csharp public class Block { public long Length {get;set;} public ReadOnlySequence<byte> Data { get; set; } } ``` # the way to use ```csharp var toUse = block.Data.Slice(0, 1000); await WriteAsync(toUse.ToArray()); ``` or use pool: ```csharp var toUse = ArrayPool<byte>.Share.Rent(1000); block.Data.Slice(0, 1000).CopyTo(toUse); try { await WriteAsync(toUse); } finally { ArrayPool<byte>.Return(toUse); } ```
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: Clockwork-Muse
body: What? No. You don't need the class (since the slice would already be length-limited), and whatever `WriteAsync(...)` is should be taking `ReadOnlyMemory<byte>` which you can get directly from (iterating through) the sequence, so there's no reason to get an array copy of the data either. Also, in _some_ cases sending an array to another method is supposed to relinquish ownership of it, so it may not be safe to return the array after passing it to another method. That said: 1. If all you're doing is writing the bytes somewhere, you'd just pass the stream through, or use `CopyToAsync(...)`, and not bother with anything at this point in the pipeline. Using pipes is mostly meant for de/serializing, and if you're not doing that you'd just skip chunking it. 2. What I would expect to write would have some signature similar to: ```csharp private bool TryParseDomainObject(ref ReadOnlySequence<byte> data, out TDomainObject? do) { // internals complicated } ``` ... and you keep doing backoff/lengthening the sequence until it's long enough to parse for whatever (portion of) the domain object you're working on. The entire point is to _avoid_ copies to other buffers (and definitely creation of other arrays), so if you're creating an array of bytes and sending or saving it somewhere you're probably not making the best use of pipelines.
discussion_title: `StreamPipeReader` may have a potential memory leak risk.

author: GeeLaw
title: Potential bug in byref-like generics (`allows ref struct`)
body: The runtime does not permit `TypedReference` as a generic argument, but the other two "old" byref-like types (`ArgIterator` and `RuntimeArgumentHandle`) are allowed. ```csharp // Windows 11 version 24H2 x64 .NET 10.0. using System; static class Program { private ref struct Wrapper<T> where T : allows ref struct { } public static void M<T>() where T : allows ref struct { } static void CreateType(Type t) { try { typeof(Wrapper<>).MakeGenericType(t); Console.WriteLine("CreateType succeeded."); } catch (Exception ex) { Console.WriteLine("CreateType failed: " + ex.GetType()); } } static void CreateMethod(Type t) { try { typeof(Program).GetMethod(nameof(M)).MakeGenericMethod(t); Console.WriteLine("CreateMethod succeeded."); } catch (Exception ex) { Console.WriteLine("CreateMethod failed: " + ex.GetType()); } } static void Main() { foreach (var t in new[] { typeof(ArgIterator), typeof(RuntimeArgumentHandle), typeof(TypedReference) }) { Console.WriteLine(t); CreateType(t); CreateMethod(t); // For TypedReference, we get TypeLoadException and BadImageFormatException. } } } ``` In [Ecma-335-Augments.md](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/main/docs/design/specs/Ecma-335-Augments.md): - "ByRefLike types in generics" does not prevent `TypedReference` from being used as a generic argument. - "Ref Fields" states "converting TypedReference [...] into following the normal rules of C# ref structs is desired." - "Ref Fields" has > Replace “Typed references have the same restrictions as byrefs.” With “Typed references are byref-like types and have the same restrictions as normal byref-like types.” All of those suggest that `TypedReference` should be usable as a generic type argument.
discussion_title: Potential bug in byref-like generics (`allows ref struct`)

At the moment, I have to generate IL code for incompatible methods to work around this limitation, in order to avoid breaking binary compatibility with .NET 8.0 and earlier versions. My decision to allow `ValPtr<T>` and `ReadOnlyValPtr<T>` to accept `ref struct` types is based on the fact that it is perfectly valid to have a managed reference to a `ref struct`.
