author: aloraman
title: Modern CRNG on Windows
body: The go-to implementation for cryptographic random number generator on .NET is `System.Security.Cryptography.RandomNumberGenerator`. On Windows, it is implemented via call to [BCryptGenRandom](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/api/bcrypt/nf-bcrypt-bcryptgenrandom) function from BCrypt.dll [here](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/b14b0908874ee12dc537003ad223c44cbcf21842/src/libraries/System.Security.Cryptography/src/System/Security/Cryptography/RandomNumberGeneratorImplementation.Windows.cs), which is a well-known approach, e.g. used in [SecurityDriven.Inferno's CryptoRandom](https://github.com/sdrapkin/SecurityDriven.Inferno/blob/e906d378ec1a64f9439e9f9b782081b4e4f45eda/CryptoRandom.cs#L444) and multiple other places. ```csharp Interop.BCrypt.BCryptGenRandom(IntPtr.Zero, pbBuffer, count, Interop.BCrypt.BCRYPT_USE_SYSTEM_PREFERRED_RNG) ``` I've known about this approach for about ten years, so recently I've started wondering if there is a faster and more modern API on Windows. Initially, I found a recommendation to prefer a [pseudo-handle](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/seccng/cng-algorithm-pseudo-handles#random-number-generator-algorithms) over flags like this: ```csharp Interop.BCrypt.BCryptGenRandom(Interop.BCrypt.BCRYPT_RNG_ALG_HANDLE, pbBuffer, count, 0) ``` I didn't find an actual source for this recommendation, just direct references to documentation, so I guess it was generated by some LLM. I did, however, found another API function by looking for what is used by other programming platforms. Which is [ProcessPrng](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/seccng/processprng) from BCryptPrimitives.dll. Indeed, [Golang](https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/536235), [Chromium](https://issues.chromium.org/issues/40277768), [BoringSSL](https://github.com/google/boringssl/blob/2db0eb3f96a5756298dcd7f9319e56a98585bd10/crypto/rand_extra/windows.c#L64) and [Rust](https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/pull/121337) have moved away from `BCryptGenRandom` and `RtlGenRandom` to `ProcessPrng`, to achieve better performance and stability in some environments. Though, there seems to be some problem with static linking to that function (that's not a problem for dotnet programs, is it?) I've cooked up some dirty benchmark to check this: ```csharp public unsafe class Benchmarks { [DllImport("BCrypt.dll")] internal static extern int BCryptGenRandom(IntPtr hAlgorithm, byte* pbBuffer, int cbBuffer, int dwFlags); [DllImport("BCryptPrimitives.dll")] internal static extern int ProcessPrng(byte* pdData, int cbData); public delegate void Rng(byte* ptr); public static long Cycle(Rng action) { byte* buffer = stackalloc byte[8]; long acc = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 10000; i++) { action(buffer); acc ^= Unsafe.ReadUnaligned<long>(buffer); } return acc; } [Benchmark] public long ProcessPrng() => Cycle(ptr => ProcessPrng(pdData: ptr, cbData: 8)); [Benchmark] public long BCryptGenRandomPseudoHandle() => Cycle(ptr => BCryptGenRandom(hAlgorithm: 0x81 /*BCRYPT_RNG_ALG_HANDLE*/, pbBuffer: ptr, cbBuffer: 8, dwFlags: 0)); [Benchmark(Baseline = true)] public long BCryptGenRandomFlags() => Cycle(ptr => BCryptGenRandom(hAlgorithm: 0, pbBuffer: ptr, cbBuffer: 8, dwFlags: 2 /*BCRYPT_USE_SYSTEM_PREFERRED_RNG*/)); } ``` Not the best benchmark possible, just small enough to paste here and to check if there is a difference. Judging from results: _BenchmarkDotNet v0.15.6, Windows 11 (10.0.26200.7462) 13th Gen Intel Core i9-13900 2.00GHz, 1 CPU, 32 logical and 24 physical cores [Host] : .NET 10.0.2 (10.0.2, 10.0.225.61305), X64 RyuJIT x86-64-v3_ | Method | Mean | Error | StdDev | Ratio | |---------------------------- |---------:|--------:|--------:|------:| | ProcessPrng | 257.8 us | 1.19 us | 1.11 us | 0.68 | | BCryptGenRandomPseudoHandle | 352.0 us | 4.10 us | 3.83 us | 0.93 | | BCryptGenRandomFlags | 379.7 us | 3.90 us | 3.65 us | 1.00 | the difference between Flags and PseudoHandle is neglible (and not very stable), but ProcessPrng is indeed noticeably faster. I've tried juggling it a bit, e.g. manually expanding Cycle delegate into benchmark methods, trying to fill up larger structs - the difference is still there. Considering all of the above, I have two questions: 1) Would it be a good idea to update BCL implementation (`RandomNumberGeneratorImplementation.Windows.cs`) to use ProcessPrng or BCryptGetRandom with pseudo-handle? 2) Are there any pitfalls in using ProcessPrng (or BCryptGenRandom with pseudo-handle) in another library, which targets older .NET version or even .NET Framework?
discussion_title: Modern CRNG on Windows

author: bartonjs
body: I had a chat with a compatriot on the OS crypto team. * Right now, our (internal) company guidance is to use BCRYPT_USE_SYSTEM_PREFERRED_RNG, but they say that's probably just that no one remembered to update it, so they're looking to get it changed. * BCRYPT_USE_SYSTEM_PREFERRED_RNG requires hitting the registry, so using the pseudo-handle is better. * Previously we needed the code to work on Windows 7, so this wasn't really available to us. * ProcessPrng is OK (from their perspective), but that it should go through the API-set DLL, not directly to BCryptPrimitives. I don't see the API-set DLL (theoretically `ext-ms-win-cng-rng-l1`) on my computer, so it's probably one of those "if you reference it you should carry it with you" things, or it's just magically handled by LoadLibrary. Given that the API-set is not being super-obvious, the recommendation I got was to use the pseudo-handle (if we want to make a change). "But it could be faster if we just jump to ProcessPrng anyways!" Sure... but the team that owns it asked us not to (at least not "yet"). And is the CSPRNG really the limiting factor on your hotpath? I would imagine most of that perf delta is the function call overhead, so for better bytes/second you'd want to bulk-fetch, not fetch 8 bytes at a time.
discussion_title: Modern CRNG on Windows

author: aloraman
body: Ah, API-set DLL. Guess it's the same story as with `_fpreset`, you can import from `msvcr.dll` without shipping dependencies - but it's frowned upon and not officially supported, you're expected to import from `msvcrXX.dll` and ship corresponding redistributable with your app. Too bad :) As for performance factors - it's a part of cost analysis. Optimization via bulk-fetching/batching is great, but large batches have their downsides too (see caching in https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/pull/123540 for example). So it's kinda balancing between "how fast is the call" and "how large is the batch". Thus, I'm testing the limits.
discussion_title: Modern CRNG on Windows

author: aloraman
body: A little update: It seems there's an API-set available, so the function can be imported as follows: ```csharp [DllImport("ext-ms-win-cng-rng-l1-1-1.dll")] internal static extern int ProcessPrng(byte* pdData, int cbData); ``` Well, at least it works on my machine. `ext-ms-win-cng-rng-l1-1-0.dll` also works. Indeed, it is a form of `LoadLibrary` magic. There's no such dll present on the machine though. After digging through the PEB's map of namespaces and hosts, it seems this is a namespace that leads to the same `BCryptPrimitives.dll` _Namespace: 'ext-ms-win-cng-rng-l1-1-1' (Hash 0x7B42EFC8) Host 1: 'bcryptprimitives.dll'_

author: tsw-github
title: .NET sandboxed app on macOS does not see certificate in keychain imported by third party sandboxed app
body: Hello, we have a dotnet application (.NET 10, C#14) that uses the SescureBlackbox libraries (v24.0.9470) to access the macOS (v26.2, M3) keychain. The application runs within the macOS platform in a sandbox, because we need it to communicate with the Safari web browser extension via App Groups, the application is not distributed via the App Store. We have currently noticed a problem that when a third-party application eToken (https://www.602.cz/e-token), which also runs in a sandbox and does not share an App Group or Keychain group with our application, issues a certificate and imports it into the keychain, our application does not see it. When we disable the sandbox for our application, we see the certificate fine. When we create a simple test application via XCode/Swift, it always sees the certificate in the keychain, whether it has sandbox enabled or not. Do you know what the problem could be? Thank you.
discussion_title: .NET sandboxed app on macOS does not see certificate in keychain imported by third party sandboxed app

author: vst121
body: Hi, What you’re seeing is expected behavior due to macOS sandboxing. When your .NET app runs in a sandbox, it can only access keychain items that belong to its own App Group or Keychain group. Certificates imported by a third-party app (like eToken) that isn’t in the same group won’t be visible. When you disable the sandbox, your app can see all keychain items, which is why the certificate appears. The Swift/Xcode test app likely has broader entitlements or is not constrained in the same way, which is why it sees the certificate even in a sandbox. This isn’t a bug with SecureBlackbox. It’s the expected macOS sandbox behavior. To access the certificate in a sandboxed app, it must be imported into a shared App Group or Keychain group that your app can access.
discussion_title: .NET sandboxed app on macOS does not see certificate in keychain imported by third party sandboxed app

author: ha-ves
title: [NativeAOT] Referenced NuGet package build files are not evaluated when publishing NativeAOT ?
body: I'm doing some MSBuild & NuGet acrobatics by packaging native library binaries and build files to make the consuming project use those binaries. Until the static-linking part in NativeAOT. My idea was to add to `NativeLibrary` in a target, because I have to get the path from `$(Configuration)`, which only exists in-msbuild-process. Excerpt: ```csproj <!--- Package.targets file, which should be imported via generated '*.nuget.g.targets' --> ... <Target Name="NativeFilesToCopy" DependsOnTargets="PrepareNativeProps" BeforeTargets="BeforeBuild"> <ItemGroup> <DirectPInvoke Include="package" /> <NativeLibrary Include="$(NativeFilesPath)\package.lib" /> </ItemGroup> ... ``` The linker doesn't see the `NativeLibrary` inclusion and It's not finding the library file to link. It seems like the package's build files are not used, only the project file. Or am I hooking into the wrong target? I tried https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/discussions/108981#discussioncomment-10988946 (hooking after `Build` target) except added in the `Package.targets`, but it didn't work.
discussion_title: [NativeAOT] Referenced NuGet package build files are not evaluated when publishing NativeAOT ?

author: vst121
body: What you’re running into is a common timing/target ordering issue in MSBuild with NativeLibrary items. The key points: - NativeLibrary items are evaluated early by the SDK during the ResolveNativeAssets / Compile phase. Adding them in a BeforeBuild target is too late, so the linker never sees them. - NuGet-generated .targets are imported after SDK evaluation, but only certain targets propagate items into ResolveNativeAssets correctly. **Suggestion:** Moving NativeLibrary inclusion out of a Target and into a statically evaluated ItemGroup (or an earlier target) is what allows the linker to find your library.
discussion_title: [NativeAOT] Referenced NuGet package build files are not evaluated when publishing NativeAOT ?

author: ha-ves
body: It seems either `Publish` target and/or `dotnet publish` (which I know does `-restore` (not target)) will not evaluate currently restored packages for additional packages (will only restore from empty).
discussion_title: [NativeAOT] Referenced NuGet package build files are not evaluated when publishing NativeAOT ?

author: Danielku15
title: Make ArrayMemoryPoolBuffer a struct for less GC pressure.
body: Hey there. While troubleshooting the memory allocations in my networking code, I realized that the `MemoryPool<byte>.Shared` allocates new heap objects on every `Rent`. https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/83d4dcd1877caff231b144c9454aa481d64e3c1b/src/libraries/System.Memory/src/System/Buffers/ArrayMemoryPool.cs#L19 https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/83d4dcd1877caff231b144c9454aa481d64e3c1b/src/libraries/System.Memory/src/System/Buffers/ArrayMemoryPool.ArrayMemoryPoolBuffer.cs#L8 I wonder if this is really by-design, or just a historical oversight. When using `System.IO.Pipelines.PipeWriter` the underlying pipe will allocate objects from the configured MemoryPool triggering heap allocations on every write to the pipe. https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/83d4dcd1877caff231b144c9454aa481d64e3c1b/src/libraries/System.IO.Pipelines/src/System/IO/Pipelines/Pipe.cs#L229-L257 Even Kestrel is affected by this: https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/8d9545796d3a6e0a7a21610d234ff5b28bada6d9/src/Servers/Kestrel/shared/DefaultSimpleMemoryPoolFactory.cs#L9-L17 For my own sake I now created an own `MemoryPool<T>` which I pass into the related code areas and there I use a `struct ArrayMemoryPoolBuffer : IMemoryOwner<T>`.
discussion_title: Make ArrayMemoryPoolBuffer a struct for less GC pressure.

author: huoyaoyuan
body: It is passed in the `IMemoryOwner<T>` interface. Making it a struct has no effect because it will always be boxed.
discussion_title: Make ArrayMemoryPoolBuffer a struct for less GC pressure.

author: Danielku15
body: :man_facepalming: of course, you're right. That makes the whole situation trickier than I thought. I think for my project I'll use some `ObjectPool<PooledMemoryOwner>` approach to reuse the wrapped objects. Nevertheless I think my concern stays valid: there is room for improvement regarding these objects. Just looking at GC/Memory performance shifting to structs would eliminate some overhead here with a direct benefit for various components. Regarding the breaking changes and compatibility with some other use cases, there might be need for a different approach to this.
discussion_title: Make ArrayMemoryPoolBuffer a struct for less GC pressure.

author: huoyaoyuan
body: `MemoryPool<T>` is used less than `ArrayPool<T>`. If you don't need `IMemoryOwner`, then using `ArrayPool<T>` just works.
discussion_title: Make ArrayMemoryPoolBuffer a struct for less GC pressure.

author: Danielku15
body: Yes, in my own code I go for ArrayPool directly. But sometimes it is part of the default library behaviors like specifically System.IO.Pipelines uses the memory pool: 1. Pipe uses the memory pool [here](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/15d90be9726d3f43f4c7d54b3d3ed7ba59967c10/src/libraries/System.IO.Pipelines/src/System/IO/Pipelines/Pipe.cs#L229-L257). 2. The memory pool might come from [outside](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/15d90be9726d3f43f4c7d54b3d3ed7ba59967c10/src/libraries/System.IO.Pipelines/src/System/IO/Pipelines/PipeOptions.cs#L79-L80). 3. Kestrel also [loads](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/5e25f153d8e613642c33f4d3570947ae4976469a/src/Servers/Kestrel/Transport.Sockets/src/SocketTransportOptions.cs#L169) the memory pool as configured `https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/03d43ac61779148acb14ff0abd32a079a28ba42d/src/Servers/Kestrel/Transport.Sockets/src/WebHostBuilderSocketExtensions.cs#L36` The memorypool might be reconfigured through a variety of DI injections. Also kestrel itself seem to change it to PinnedBlockMemoryPoolFactory by default. In my memory profiles I could see a lot of objects created when many users are connected to the web application simultaneously and receiving data. Somehow the non-default MemoryPools make it through. It's easily happening that a different memory pool is not the default.
discussion_title: Make ArrayMemoryPoolBuffer a struct for less GC pressure.

author: huoyaoyuan
body: Speaking at higher level, the design of `MemoryPool<T>` itself introduces the boxing overhead because of using the `IMemoryOwner<T>` interface. The direct way to get rid of it is to not use `MemoryPool<T>`.
discussion_title: Make ArrayMemoryPoolBuffer a struct for less GC pressure.

author: Danielku15
body: > Speaking at higher level, the design of MemoryPool<T> itself introduces the boxing overhead because of using the IMemoryOwner<T> interface. Yes I understood that. My concern is that the MemoryPool<> is quasi automatically active in some performance critical paths when using Kestrel and System.IO.Pipelines due to the DI registrations. My wish would be that the out-of-the-box experience is causing less heap allocations. > The direct way to get rid of it is to not use MemoryPool<T>. For my own code I do that. But it gets tricky if Kestrel/ASP.net core register MemoryPool implementations in the DI container which then get picked up everywhere. Workarounds are possible until some extend, but especially in Kestrel many things are internal and you need to rely on these internal behaviors to achieve the goal of not having MemoryPools used in System.IO.Pipelines.

author: fern-support
title: Guidance for handling Null vs Undefined in .NET JSON
body: ## The Problem We've been building HTTP SDKs and OpenAPI clients in C#, and we've been running into this issue with representing three distinct states for a field: 1. **Undefined**: Field not provided (e.g., PATCH request - don't update this field) 2. **Null**: Field explicitly set to null (e.g., PATCH request - clear this field) 3. **Value**: Field set to a specific value (e.g., PATCH request - update to this value) The problem is that C#'s nullable types (`string?`, `int?`) can only represent two states: null or value. There's no way to distinguish between "not provided" and "explicitly set to null." ### Example Let's say you have a PATCH endpoint for updating a user profile: ```csharp PATCH /users/123 { "name": "Alice", // Update name to "Alice" "email": null, // Clear email (set to null) // age not present // Don't touch age (leave unchanged) } ``` With standard C# types, you can't express this: - `string? Email = null` - Does this mean "clear it" or "not provided"? - There's no way to tell if the user **omitted** a field vs **explicitly sent null** ## What I've Built So Far I ended up implementing an `Optional<T>` type that wraps nullable types to give us that third state: ```csharp public readonly struct Optional<T> : IOptional, IEquatable<Optional<T>> { public static Optional<T> Undefined { get; } public static Optional<T> Of(T value) { } public bool IsDefined { get; } public bool IsUndefined { get; } public T Value { get; } } ``` ### How It Works Here's what using it looks like: ```csharp public class UpdateUserRequest { // null => null, or value => value [Nullable] public string? Description { get; set; } // Optional, nullable - can be undefined => undefined, null => null, or value => null [Optional, Nullable] public Optional<string?> Email { get; set; } = Optional<string?>.Undefined; // null => undefined, value => value [Optional] public string? Name { get; set; } } // Usage var request = new UpdateUserRequest { Name = "Alice", // Update name Email = Optional<string?>.Of(null), // Clear email (explicit null) // Description omitted // Don't touch description }; ``` ### Serialization Behavior Quick reference for how different combinations serialize: | Property Type | Value | JSON Behavior | |--------------|-------|---------------| | `string? Field` | `null` | Omitted (JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingNull) | | `[Nullable] string? Field` | `null` | Included as `null` | | `[Nullable] string? Field` | `Of("text")` | Included as `"text"` | | `[Optional] string? Field` | `null` | Omitted | | `[Optional] string? Field` | `Of("text")` | Included as `"text"` | | `[Optional] Optional<string> Field` | `Undefined` | Omitted | | `[Optional] Optional<string> Field` | `Of("text")` | Included as `"text"` | | `[Optional, Nullable] Optional<string?> Field` | `Undefined` | Omitted | | `[Optional, Nullable] Optional<string?> Field` | `Of(null)` | Included as `null` | | `[Optional, Nullable] Optional<string?> Field` | `Of("text")` | Included as `"text"` | ## What's in the Repo The implementation includes: 1. **Core Type**: `Optional<T>` struct with IEquatable support 2. **JSON Serialization**: Custom `JsonConverter` and `JsonTypeInfoResolver` using reflection to check `[Optional]` and `[Nullable]` attributes 3. **Testing Support**: NUnit extensions for comparing `Optional<T>` values in assertions 4. **Complete Test Suite**: 20+ tests covering all serialization/deserialization scenarios Key files in this repository: - `Optional.cs` - Core implementation with IOptional interface - `OpenApiSerialization.cs` - JSON serialization with attribute-based modifiers - `OptionalAttribute.cs` & `NullableAttribute.cs` - Attribute markers - `OpenApiSerializationTests.cs` - Comprehensive test coverage - `OptionalComparerExtensions.cs` - NUnit test helpers ## Questions I'm looking for guidance on whether this is the right approach: **1. Is there an existing .NET pattern I should be using instead?** - Are there plans to add first-class support for "undefined vs null" in future C# versions? - Should I be using a different approach with System.Text.Json? - Are there existing BCL types or patterns that address this? **2. JSON serialization - am I doing this right?** - Is using `JsonTypeInfoResolver` with reflection on custom attributes reasonable? - Should I be leveraging different System.Text.Json features? - Any performance implications I should be aware of? **3. API design - the `Optional<T?>` question** I chose to separate concerns: - `Optional<T>` = "Is the field present in the request?" - `T?` = "Can the field value be null?" This leads to `Optional<T?>` for optional nullable fields. Is this the right approach, or should I build null handling into `Optional<T>` itself (like having an `Optional<T>.Null` state)? --- Full working implementation with tests is in [this repo](https://github.com/Swimburger/dotnet-test-explicit-null). All tests pass on .NET 8.0, .NET 10.0, and .NET Framework 4.6.2. Would really appreciate any guidance on the recommended .NET approach here!
discussion_title: Guidance for handling Null vs Undefined in .NET JSON

author: eiriktsarpalis
body: > Is there an existing .NET pattern I should be using instead? In my opinion, the best way to encode the presence or absence of a property within JSON is to use one of the JSON DOM types. Using a POCO is bound to lose some fidelity since there is no native concept of a property being absent. If I absolutely needed to I would probably go for a wrapper type like the one you described, although I think the `[Nullable]` and `[Optional]` attributes are adding more complexity than necessary and I would just got with a converter based approach like so: ```C# using System.Text.Json; using System.Text.Json.Serialization; string json = JsonSerializer.Serialize(new MyPoco() { Null = null, Defined = "defined" }); Console.WriteLine(json); var result = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<MyPoco>(json); Console.WriteLine(result.Undefined.HasValue); Console.WriteLine(result.Null.HasValue); public class MyPoco { [JsonIgnore(Condition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingDefault)] public Option<string?> Undefined { get; set; } [JsonIgnore(Condition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingDefault)] public Option<string?> Null { get; set; } [JsonIgnore(Condition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingDefault)] public Option<string?> Defined { get; set; } } [JsonConverter(typeof(OptionConverterFactory))] public readonly struct Option<T> { public bool HasValue { get; } public T Value { get; } public Option(T value) { HasValue = true; Value = value; } public static implicit operator Option<T>(T value) => new(value); } public sealed class OptionConverterFactory : JsonConverterFactory { public override bool CanConvert(Type typeToConvert) => typeToConvert.IsGenericType && typeToConvert.GetGenericTypeDefinition() == typeof(Option<>); public override JsonConverter? CreateConverter(Type typeToConvert, JsonSerializerOptions options) { Type valueType = typeToConvert.GetGenericArguments()[0]; Type converterType = typeof(OptionConverter<>).MakeGenericType(valueType); return (JsonConverter?)Activator.CreateInstance(converterType); } } public sealed class OptionConverter<T> : JsonConverter<Option<T>> { public override bool HandleNull => true; public override Option<T> Read(ref Utf8JsonReader reader, Type typeToConvert, JsonSerializerOptions options) { return new(JsonSerializer.Deserialize<T>(ref reader, options)!); } public override void Write(Utf8JsonWriter writer, Option<T> value, JsonSerializerOptions options) { if (!value.HasValue) { // If absolutely necessary, write undefined values as null although // this should be gated using JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingDefault writer.WriteNullValue(); return; } JsonSerializer.Serialize(writer, value.Value, options); } } ```
discussion_title: Guidance for handling Null vs Undefined in .NET JSON

author: Danielku15
body: We use a pattern similar to the OData deltas: https://github.com/OData/AspNetCoreOData/blob/main/src/Microsoft.AspNetCore.OData/Deltas/DeltaOfT.cs The specialized JSON serializer for the deltas store the the information in a key-value property bag. Then you can either obtain the values/nested values via the getters (by name, by expression, looping through them) or you apply the modifications directly to a given object. It's a bit tricky to get everything aligned (JSON serialization, OpenAPI docs with correct optional/required field declaratiosn etc.) but once you got the main things in place, the approach is really nice for strict RESTful services. You can then simply accept a `Delta<TResource>` on your PATCH methods.

title: Using Threadpool in managed code run from a Runtime started by nethost
body: Hello guys, I am writing a scripting system for an Unreal Engine 3 game. My native host is loaded into the game using DLL injection. Then, I use `hostfxr` to load in the .NET Core Runtime and run my managed entry point. However, it seems like some features of .NET aren't inited properly. Specifically, doing anything async like creating tasks seem to freeze the application. I have tried using the relatively new `System.Threading.ThreadPool.UseWindowsThreadPool` runtime option which did fix Tasks. However, that only appears to be a workaround as other things like `FileSystemWatchers` still freeze the game. [Here's a link to my native host](https://github.com/Samuil1337/BmSDK/blob/34fcd1490f5e149e813c16d6969ddfd8676963d9/src/BmSDK.Host/dllmain.cpp) (~100 lines without boilerplate code) and [here's a link to the function that it's running](https://github.com/Samuil1337/BmSDK/blob/34fcd1490f5e149e813c16d6969ddfd8676963d9/src/BmSDK/Loader.cs). I am using a modified version of the [tutorial host](https://github.com/dotnet/samples/blob/main/core/hosting/src/NativeHost/nativehost.cpp). I also tried switching to the newer independent `load_assembly_fn` and `get_function_pointer_fn` but it didn't work. I wanted to ask if this is a known issue or if I have something misconfigured. Are there any workarounds for this problem that I haven't tried yet? Thanks in advance. Samuil1337
discussion_title: Using Threadpool in managed code run from a Runtime started by nethost

author: AustinWise
body: You are calling `LoadLibrary` from `DllMain`, some that is explicitly not allowed. See [Dynamic-Link Library Best Practices](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows/win32/dlls/dynamic-link-library-best-practices). Using managed code is also on the list. Perhaps on `DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH` use `CreateThread` to call your `load_dll` function, rather than calling it directly? `hostfxr_close` potentially takes a lock and uses memory management functions ( see [here](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/5d525b730bc668ca693f83b47520258a5fd7254e/src/native/corehost/fxr/fx_muxer.cpp#L970-L972) ). Those are also not allowed in `DllMain`. And the host handle is not thread safe ( [see here](https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/blob/main/docs/design/features/native-hosting.md#initialize-host-context) ). So I'm not sure what is the right way to clean that up.

author: zigzag312
title: GC Isolates
body: ## Problem Modern C# makes it possible to write high-performance, low-latency code. This requires pre-allocating as much as possible during initialization and then making zero allocations on the heap by using techniques like object pooling, stackalloc, Span\<T\>, ref structs, etc, to avoid triggering the GC. The problem is that when carefully written zero-allocation code with low-latency requirements needs to be a part of a larger application, the no-allocation constraint propagates to the entire managed process. Zero-allocation techniques are feasible in individual components, but they become cost-prohibitive at application scale because allocations must be avoided even in modules that aren’t latency-sensitive. ## Proposal Add an ability to create GC-isolated threads that run on an isolated managed heap with its own GC, so GC pauses in the rest of the process don’t affect latency-sensitive work in the isolated thread. Any threads spawned from isolated thread inherit the same isolated heap and GC. Like Dart isolates, C# GC isolates would use separate heaps with no shared object references, but here the goal is isolating GC pauses for low-latency threads in the same process, ideally with zero-copy ownership transfer between isolates. ### Benefits - **Latency:** allocations in the “main” app can’t trigger GC STW pauses in the isolate. - **Same-process:** keep everything in one process (no IPC tax, easier to write), still get isolation. - **Same-language:** keep it all in C#, no need for interop with an unmanaged language. - **Less restrictive:** you don’t have to enforce a global no-allocation rule across the entire application. ### Practical example [OwnAudioSharp](https://github.com/ModernMube/OwnAudioSharp) audio framework had to switch to C++, even though it was initially developed as a fully managed C# version that didn’t allocate and performed well. Because currently, any application using a managed audio engine also has to avoid allocations across the entire application to prevent GC pauses from affecting the audio thread. ## Summary This proposal would enable us to put a latency-sensitive component into a GC isolate, insulating it from STW collections happening elsewhere in the app. Would something like this be technically feasible?
discussion_title: GC Isolates

author: huoyaoyuan
body: A NativeAOT module will have its own GC heap and isolated object references. It only pauses threads known to the module. > * **Same-process:** keep everything in one process (no IPC tax, easier to write), still get isolation. > * **Same-language:** keep it all in C#, no need for interop with an unmanaged language. This is not possible. A foreign GC heap is effectively a foreign native module. It can only be interact with (in-process) interop. Since the standalone module shouldn't be invoked on the same thread to avoid making it known by other heap, cross-thread IPC must be used.
discussion_title: GC Isolates

author: zigzag312
body: I had thought Dart can do it, but after taking a closer look at how Dart can pass objects from one isolate to another without copying, I've learned it can only do so between isolates in the same _isolate group_, and all isolates in a group share the same heap. So, one of my key assumptions is wrong. Is there no technique that would (with tight restrictions) allow passing of objects between isolated GCs without copying? Maybe something like a shared Pinned Object Heap used by otherwise independent GCs under an isolated-ownership model, where each pinned object has exactly one owning GC at any time and is reachable only from its owner? Could the GCs transfer ownership using move semantics here?
discussion_title: GC Isolates

author: huoyaoyuan
body: This is way too niche and unlikely to work well. GC takes ownership by segments. It doesn't really manage individual objects. `GCHandle` would be the correct way to access objects from different heaps, with manual reference tracking.
discussion_title: GC Isolates

author: Joy-less
title: Add no-allocation version of `new StringInfo(string).LengthInTextElements`
body: The [`new StringInfo(string).LengthInTextElements`](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.globalization.stringinfo.lengthintextelements) method is used to get the number of graphemes in a string. However, you have to allocate a `StringInfo`, which is not performant for a method that is an alternative to `string.Length`. If you don't want to allocate, you have to write a method like this: ```cs public static int GetLengthInTextElements(scoped ReadOnlySpan<char> Input) { int TextElementCount = 0; int Index = 0; while (true) { int Length = StringInfo.GetNextTextElementLength(Input[Index..]); if (Length <= 0) { break; } TextElementCount++; Index += Length; } return TextElementCount; } ``` Could this method be added to the standard library? EDIT: See my proposal: https://github.com/dotnet/runtime/issues/123092
discussion_title: Add no-allocation version of `new StringInfo(string).LengthInTextElements`

author: huoyaoyuan
body: > However, you have to allocate a `StringInfo`, which is not performant for a method that is an alternative to `string.Length`. `StringInfo` is a high cost API. It can never be a performance alternative to `string.Length`: ```csharp [Benchmark] [Arguments("qwertyuiopasdfghjklzxcvbnm")] public int StringInfoLength(string str) => new StringInfo(str).LengthInTextElements; [Benchmark] [Arguments("qwertyuiopasdfghjklzxcvbnm")] public int LengthFromTextElements(string str) { int TextElementCount = 0; int Index = 0; while (true) { int Length = StringInfo.GetNextTextElementLength(str.AsSpan(Index)); if (Length <= 0) { break; } TextElementCount++; Index += Length; } return TextElementCount; } ``` | Method | str | Mean | Error | StdDev | Gen0 | Allocated | |----------------------- |--------------------- |---------:|--------:|--------:|-------:|----------:| | StringInfoLength | qwert(...)cvbnm [26] | 397.5 ns | 1.30 ns | 1.15 ns | 0.0067 | 128 B | | LengthFromTextElements | qwert(...)cvbnm [26] | 395.7 ns | 0.73 ns | 0.65 ns | - | - | The overhead of allocation is negligible comparing to the cost of the algorithm. Moreover, your code has an issue that amplifies the allocation much more. `Input[Index..]` slices on the string and allocates.
discussion_title: Add no-allocation version of `new StringInfo(string).LengthInTextElements`

author: Joy-less
body: I ran a benchmark with 100,000 iterations: | Method | str | Mean | Error | StdDev | Gen0 | Allocated | |----------------------- |--------------------- |---------:|---------:|---------:|----------:|-----------:| | StringInfoLength | qwert(...)cvbnm [26] | 54.70 ms | 0.134 ms | 0.126 ms | 2500.0000 | 16000000 B | | LengthFromTextElements | qwert(...)cvbnm [26] | 51.82 ms | 0.087 ms | 0.077 ms | - | - | <details> <summary>Benchmark code</summary> ```cs using BenchmarkDotNet.Attributes; using BenchmarkDotNet.Running; using System.Globalization; namespace LengthInTextElementsBenchmark; public class Program { public static void Main() { BenchmarkRunner.Run<Benchmarks>(); } } [MemoryDiagnoser] public class Benchmarks { [Benchmark] [Arguments("qwertyuiopasdfghjklzxcvbnm")] public int StringInfoLength(string str) { int Counter = 0; for (int I = 0; I < 100_000; I++) { Counter += new StringInfo(str).LengthInTextElements; } return Counter; } [Benchmark] [Arguments("qwertyuiopasdfghjklzxcvbnm")] public int LengthFromTextElements(string str) { ReadOnlySpan<char> Span = str; int Counter = 0; for (int I = 0; I < 100_000; I++) { int TextElementCount = 0; int Index = 0; while (true) { int Length = StringInfo.GetNextTextElementLength(Span[Index..]); if (Length <= 0) { break; } TextElementCount++; Index += Length; } Counter += TextElementCount; } return Counter; } } ``` </details> The allocating version is slightly slower, but more importantly the allocation is 16 MB for 100,000 iterations. The `Error` is also ~1.5x higher, which implies certain runs are slower, which implies garbage collection spikes are present. Also, I don't know if the `StringInfo.GetNextTextElementLength` method is the most performant way to implement this method - there may be internal optimizations that can be done. > Moreover, your code has an issue that amplifies the allocation much more. Input[Index..] slices on the string and allocates. I think you've misread the original example - it takes a span not a string.
discussion_title: Add no-allocation version of `new StringInfo(string).LengthInTextElements`

author: huoyaoyuan
body: > I ran a benchmark with 100,000 iterations > The `Error` is also ~1.5x higher, which implies certain runs are slower, which implies garbage collection spikes are present. This is not the best practice, and will count the instability introduced by GC into the benchmark. But yes, it correctly shows the impact of GC. However, benchmarking `LengthInTextElements` may not match real-world workloads. It's very likely to enumerate the elements after acquiring the length. In this case, `StringInfo` will be optimal because it won't recalculate the offsets. > Also, I don't know if the `StringInfo.GetNextTextElementLength` method is the most performant way to implement this method - there may be internal optimizations that can be done. It's hardly. You can check the source code of `StringInfo`.
discussion_title: Add no-allocation version of `new StringInfo(string).LengthInTextElements`

author: Joy-less
body: For what it's worth, I looked at the `new StringInfo(string).LengthInTextElements` implementation and optimised the original example, now I benchmarked it at ~8% faster. ```cs public static int GetLengthInTextElements(scoped ReadOnlySpan<char> Input) { ReadOnlySpan<char> Remaining = Input; int TextElementCount = 0; while (!Remaining.IsEmpty) { TextElementCount++; Remaining = Remaining.Slice(StringInfo.GetNextTextElementLength(Remaining)); } return TextElementCount; } ``` After looking at the `StringInfo` implementation, it's complicated and not necessarily optimised for simple (ASCII only, single-rune characters only) examples. If anything, it may be good to add a `StringInfo.GetLengthInTextElements` method so that if it gets optimised in the future then code can take advantage of it.

author: bartonjs
body: 4216792 / 98212 is 42. I forget what the object overhead is, but feels like it's in the ballpark for a 16 byte buffer to hold the IV state, assuming you're using Windows with an ephemeral key, and thus that "..." is "BasicSymmetricCipherLiteBCrypt". All three of those things (well, all 97203 triplets, the other 1009 arrays presumably came from somewhere else) should be rather transitory and should get cleaned up during gen0 GC. Reducing the allocation any further would require some not-insignificant "plumbing" changes, and potentially no longer sharing code between the stateful and stateless encryption paths. So, it's "possible" to get it down lower, but it isn't (or, at minimum "hasn't been") the top of our areas-for-improvement pile. Is ~128 bytes gen0 per call really being a problem for you, or just a somewhat big-looking number on your profiler?

So it's not only an extra allocation, but also the overhead of the copying. I guess the allocation can be fixed quickly with ArrayPool, but the copying is a problem nonetheless. author: bartonjs
body: > I suspect that internally, it allocates a byte[], copies from my span to the byte[], invokes native, then copies from byte[] to my destination span. Just for the IV. The data is passed in without a copy, and the encrypted form is written back without a copy. Whether you encrypt one byte or a gigabyte, a single call to TryEncryptCbc is only going to allocate one 16 byte array (since it's AES, and thus a 16-byte IV), one SafeKeyHandle, and one BasicSymmetricCipherLiteBCrypt. The IV has to be a copy because we take it as input-only, but Windows takes it as in-out, and write the "next call" IV during the same call. With aforementioned plumbing changes we could eliminate that for the one-shots, but it'll still leave you at 2/3 of what you're seeing without even further plumbing changes. > all that was generated from only 10 HTTP requests That implies you're doing about 9700 calls to TryEncryptCbc for each request. That's... atypical? :smile: > int bodyLen = aes.GetCiphertextLengthCbc It wouldn't change the allocation results here, but it's weird to call GetCiphertextLength and TryEncrypt, as TryEncrypt itself is just GetCiphertextLength, early return, then do the work. If you want a custom exception you can use TryEncryptCbc without GetCiphertextLengthCbc. If you want CryptographicException skip them both and just use EncryptCbc (which is if-not-TryEncryptCbc-throw). GetCiphertextLengthCbc is mostly for "I don't want do to double-and-retry, so tell me the answer and I'll allocate a big enough array, or call whatever.EnsureCapacity, or whatever") > TryEncryptCbc(plainSpan, _staticIV, ... Encrypting with a static IV means that the same data (assuming the same key) will encrypt to the same output, which leaks "the data is unchanged" across multiple messages. Maybe it's right for what you're doing, but usually a static IV is an anti-pattern. Again, not relevant to the allocation story, but "someone (might be) is wrong on the Internet..."

author: Tacodiva
title: Is it possible to safely sandbox C# code by analyzing the produced assembly?
body: I would like to be able to compile and execute C# scripts at runtime in a sandbox. I would like to use a system like this: 1. Compile the scripts with `allowUnsafe` set to false. 2. Analyze the script's assembly to enforce a white-list of safe types, methods, and fields. If the CIL references anything not in the whitelist, refuse to continue. - The whitelist would have to prevent the usage of some language features, like `dynamic` (as it would block the reflection methods required for dynamic to work). 3. Analyze the script's assembly to look for unsafe CIL emitted by the [pseudo attributes](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/csharp/language-reference/attributes/pseudo-attributes). - Make sure all structs have a sequential memory layout. - Make sure no methods are `internalcall` or `pinvokeimpl`. 4. Load the assembly into the runtime and execute the scripts. Assuming the white-list is secure and enforced correctly, is this a safe way to sandbox scripts? Note that I am not worried about denial of service attacks with infinite loops or crashing, I only care that that code is not able to "infect" the broader system. I guess the real question is if there is a way to produce unsafe access or reflection without having the assembly reference any types/methods/fields which are not white-listed. As a hypothetical, the `Unsafe.Unbox` methods seems to always compile to the `call` CIL instruction, thus can be caught by the checker, but if it was able to compile directly into an `unbox` instruction, the whitelist would not be able to identify it as unsafe code.
discussion_title: Is it possible to safely sandbox C# code by analyzing the produced assembly?

author: huoyaoyuan
body: It's theoretically possible, but may not be practical. The current .NET libraries are designed for in-process isolation. There may be undiscovered holes that allows accessing wide global state. Limiting access to a narrow range like API of host system and common collections only may work. Besides `dynamic` and reflection, all the memory unsafe API should also be covered, including `Marshal`, `MemoryMarshal` and `Unsafe`. The [memory safety](https://github.com/dotnet/csharplang/blob/main/proposals/unsafe-evolution.md) proposal covers them. > * Make sure all structs have a sequential memory layout. Note that auto layout is also safe. > As a hypothetical, the `Unsafe.Unbox` methods seems to always compile to the `call` CIL instruction, thus can be caught by the checker, but if it was able to compile directly into an `unbox` instruction, the whitelist would not be able to identify it as unsafe code. It's covered by the verifiability of IL. However, some modern C# features aren't supported by verification yet.
discussion_title: Is it possible to safely sandbox C# code by analyzing the produced assembly?

author: rahulmohang
title: Potential license incompatibility between MPL and Apache
body: Hello, The folder, 'https://github.com/moby/moby/tree/master/vendor/github.com/hashicorp' contains source code licensed under the MPL v2.0. According to https://www.apache.org/legal/resolved.html#weak-copyleft-licenses, MPL v2.0 is a category B license. It is explicitly mentioned not to include Category B licensed works in source releases in an Apache v2.0 licensed project. Such software may only be included in binary form within an Apache product if we label the inclusion appropriately. Do you think that this could be a compatibility issue?
discussion_title: Potential license incompatibility between MPL and Apache

author: thaJeztah
body: I don't think applies here; that page (https://www.apache.org/legal/resolved.html#weak-copyleft-licenses) is for projects governed by the Apache Software Foundation (ASF); it's a guidance for projects in the ASF, NOT for projects using the Apache 2 _License_. Scope / purpose of that document is outlined at the top of that page; https://www.apache.org/legal/resolved.html#audience > This policy provides licensing guidance to Apache Software Foundation projects. It identifies the acceptable licenses for inclusion of third-party Open Source components in Apache Software Foundation products.
discussion_title: Potential license incompatibility between MPL and Apache

author: rahulmohang
body: Ok, thank you.
discussion_title: Potential license incompatibility between MPL and Apache

[moby/moby#51765](https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/51765) ### Rootless - Rootless: Consider `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/cdi` and `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/cdi` when looking for CDI devices. [moby/moby#51624](https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/51624) - Update RootlessKit to [v2.3.6](https://github.com/rootless-containers/rootlesskit/releases/tag/v2.3.6). [moby/moby#51757](https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/51757) ### API - Natively support gRPC on the listening socket. [moby/moby#50744](https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/50744) ### Go SDK - cli/command: add WithAPIClientOptions option. author: lw33
title: docker0 interface MTU reverts to 1500 after all container removal despite daemon.json mtu=1400, while docker network inspect still shows correct value
body: ### Description After configuring `mtu: 1400` in `/etc/docker/daemon.json`, the `docker0` bridge correctly shows MTU=1400 on Docker startup with no containers. However, after running and then removing a container, `docker0` MTU reverts to 1500. This contradicts the expected behavior introduced in PR #46849 ("Explicitly set MTU on bridge devices"). ### Question **Should users rely on `ip link`, `docker network inspect bridge`, or another method to verify the *effective* MTU for containers?** Currently, `docker network inspect bridge` correctly shows: ```json "com.docker.network.driver.mtu": "1400" ``` ### Reproduce 1. Install Docker 2. Create `/etc/docker/daemon.json`: ```json { "mtu": 1400 } ``` 3. Restart Docker: sudo systemctl restart docker 4. Check MTU: ip link show docker0 → shows mtu 1400 5. Run and remove a container: ```shell docker run --rm --entrypoint ls busybox / ``` 6. Check MTU again: ip link show docker0 → now shows mtu 1500 ### Expected Behavior docker0 MTU should remain 1400 at all times, as per PR #46849. author: htoyoda18
title: daemon: avoid unnecessary config.New() execution when running `dockerd --version`
body: ### Description The `config.New()` function performs unnecessary filesystem lookups when running `dockerd --version`, which only needs to display version information. **FIXME reference:** https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/master/daemon/command/docker.go#L19 ```go // FIXME(thaJeztah): config.New also looks up default binary-path, but this code is also executed when running "--version". cfg, err := config.New() ``` ### Current behavior When running dockerd --version, the following sequence occurs: 1. newDaemonCommand() is called 2. config.New() is executed 3. setPlatformDefaults() performs filesystem lookups 4. lookupBinPath("docker-proxy") searches multiple directories: - /usr/local/libexec/docker/ - usr/libexec/docker/ - /usr/local/lib/docker/ - /usr/lib/docker/ - Falls back to PATH lookup This happens even though `--version` only needs to print version information and exit immediately. ### Code reference: https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/master/daemon/config/config_linux.go#L158-L170 ### Impact - Performance overhead: ~50-100ms of unnecessary filesystem I/O (more on network filesystems) - Unnecessary system calls: Multiple stat() calls on paths that may not exist - Debug logs: In some cases, debug messages are logged (suppressed at debug level) - Code clarity: Makes it unclear why binary path lookup is needed for --version ### Proposed solutions I see a few possible approaches, and I'd like to get maintainer feedback on which direction is preferred: #### Option 1: Defer binary path lookup (Simple, recommended for short-term) Delay the lookupBinPath() call until the daemon actually starts: ```go func setPlatformDefaults(cfg *Config) error { // Don't lookup binary path here cfg.BridgeConfig.UserlandProxyPath = "" // Will be resolved later // ... } // Then in daemon startup: func (daemon *Daemon) initNetworkController() error { if daemon.config.BridgeConfig.UserlandProxyPath == "" { path, err := lookupBinPath(userlandProxyBinary) // handle error... daemon.config.BridgeConfig.UserlandProxyPath = path } // ... } ``` Pros: - Minimal code changes - No filesystem I/O for --version - Easy to implement and test Cons: - Delays error detection (binary path issues only detected at daemon start) #### Option 2: Split Config initialization (More architectural) Separate minimal config (for CLI purposes) from full config (for daemon): ```go // Lightweight config creation (no filesystem access) func NewMinimal() *Config { ... } // Full config with platform defaults func NewFull() (*Config, error) { cfg := NewMinimal() if err := setPlatformDefaults(cfg); err != nil { return nil, err } return cfg, nil } ``` Pros: - Clear separation of concerns - Can be applied to other similar cases Better long-term architecture Cons: - Larger refactoring required - Need to update all callers #### Option 3: Lazy command construction Only construct full daemon command when not running --version: ```go func newDaemonCommand() (*cobra.Command, error) { cmd := &cobra.Command{ // ... RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error { // Only now call config.New() cfg, err := config.New() // ... }, } // ... } ``` Pros: --version skips config.New() entirely - Clean conceptually Cons: - Flag setup might need restructuring - More changes to command initialization ### Questions for maintainers 1. Which approach aligns best with the project's direction? 2. Are there any other constraints or considerations I should be aware of? 3. Is this optimization worth pursuing, or are there higher priority items? 4. Would you accept a PR implementing one of these approaches? ### Additional context - This FIXME was added in: e8e20c0897e (2024-01-09) - The workaround currently is to log at debug level to avoid noise during --version - Similar patterns might exist in other CLI tools in the repository
discussion_title: daemon: avoid unnecessary config.New() execution when running `dockerd --version`

However, the two commits [50443](https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/50443/files) and [49728](https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/49728) I found involve branches that diverge significantly from branch 23.0.3. Many of the modified files cannot be found in 23.0.3. Could you please provide a patch for 23.0.3?

author: thaJeztah
body: The 23.x branch reached EOL and is no longer maintained; https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/fba74ac758b82b5503b666b2112cfd0b6649615b/project/BRANCHES-AND-TAGS.md#docker-engine-branches If you're trying to fix those versions yourself, you may be able to adjust the patches that were backported to [v25.0.13](https://github.com/moby/moby/releases/tag/v25.0.13), which may be closer to the v23.x branch (https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/50445), but as the 23.x branch is unmaintained, it may be missing other patches and/or security fixes.

## 29.1.3 For a full list of pull requests and changes in this release, refer to the relevant GitHub milestones: - [docker/cli, 29.1.3 milestone](https://github.com/docker/cli/issues?q=is%3Aclosed+milestone%3A29.1.3) - [moby/moby, 29.1.3 milestone](https://github.com/moby/moby/issues?q=is%3Aclosed+milestone%3A29.1.3) ### Bug fixes and enhancements - Add shell completion for `docker stack deploy --compose-file`. [docker/cli#6690](https://github.com/docker/cli/pull/6690) - containerd image store: Fix a bug causing `docker build` to ignore the explicitly set `unpack` image exporter option. [moby/moby#51514](https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/51514) - Fix `docker image ls` dangling image handling. [docker/cli#6704](https://github.com/docker/cli/pull/6704) - Fix a bug that could cause the Engine to leave containers with autoremove set in 'dead' state on shutdown, and never reclaim them. [moby/moby#51693](https://github.com/moby/moby/pull/51693) - Fix build on i386. author: GamePlayer-8
title: How to specify the IPv6 subnet in an overlay network?
body: Hello, How do I specify the IPv6 subnet in an overlay network for docker swarm nodes? I've tried to use this command: ```shell docker network create \ --driver=overlay --ipv6 \ --subnet="fd12:0000:$(< /dev/urandom tr -dc 'a-f0-9' | head -c 4):$(< /dev/urandom tr -dc 'a-f0-9' | head -c 4)::/64" \ --subnet="172.16.$(shuf -i 1-255 -n 1).0/24" \ --attachable examplenet ``` and after that I've attempted to deploy the `compose.yaml` onto the swarm with `docker stack deploy`, but the daemon log has returned such errors: ```go time="2025-11-21T14:36:12.686687118Z" level=error msg="Failed to release pool fd12:0:1234:5678::/64" error="address space does not contain specified address pool" time="2025-11-21T14:36:12.686755403Z" level=error msg="Failed allocation for network k5kmp839hcvi64jff92ehra8u" error="failed allocating pools and gateway IP for network k5kmp839hcvi64jff92ehra8u: cannot find address pool for poolID:fd12:0:1234:5678::/64/invalid Prefix" module=node node.id=yft7198ykwav1l0ol54ir89f4 time="2025-11-21T14:36:13.230375963Z" level=error msg="Failed allocation for service rmg1hjxqpjq5242avswyy0cuy" error="networkallocator: could not find local network state" module=node node.id=yft7198ykwav1l0ol54ir89f4 time="2025-11-21T14:36:13.280138982Z" level=error msg="task allocation failure" error="service rmg1hjxqpjq5242avswyy0cuy to which task 65zuv5j2at6atd7ci5adgiho2 belongs has pending allocations" module=node node.id=yft7198ykwav1l0ol54ir89f4 ``` Whenever I remove the line `--subnet="fd12:0000:$(< /dev/urandom tr -dc 'a-f0-9' | head -c 4):$(< /dev/urandom tr -dc 'a-f0-9' | head -c 4)::/64"` everything starts working again, even with `--ipv6` argument specified. The `compose.yaml` file is looking as follows: ```yaml # compose.yaml services: exampleservice: image: "[2034:5678:1:5::4]:5000/exampleservice" build: context: ./src dockerfile: Dockerfile tmpfs: - "/tmp:rw,exec,mode=1777" - "/var/run:rw,exec,mode=1777" - "/var/tmp:rw,exec,mode=1777" - "/var/log:rw,exec,mode=1777" networks: - examplenet deploy: replicas: 1 networks: examplenet: name: examplenet external: true ```
discussion_title: How to specify the IPv6 subnet in an overlay network?

author: ismaildasci
body: Hey! The issue here is that Docker Swarm's overlay network driver needs the IPv6 address pool to be pre-configured in the daemon before you can use custom subnets. For overlay networks with custom IPv6 subnets, you need to define an `ipv6 default-address-pool` in your `/etc/docker/daemon.json` on **all swarm nodes**: ```json { "ipv6": true, "fixed-cidr-v6": "fd00::/80", "default-address-pools": [ { "base": "172.16.0.0/12", "size": 24 }, { "base": "fd12::/48", "size": 64 } ] } ``` Then restart Docker on each node. After that, when you create the overlay network: ```shell docker network create --driver=overlay --ipv6 --attachable examplenet ``` Docker will automatically allocate subnets from those pools (both IPv4 and IPv6). The error `address space does not contain specified address pool` happens because you're trying to use a random `fd12:0:xxxx:xxxx::/64` subnet that wasn't registered in any address pool. The swarm network allocator doesn't know about it. If you really need a *specific* subnet rather than auto-allocation, make sure your `--subnet` value falls within the `base` range you defined in `default-address-pools`. Hope that helps!
discussion_title: How to specify the IPv6 subnet in an overlay network?

author: GamePlayer-8
body: I have added that IPv6 range to the default address pool, to all nodes in the swarm but there's still no IPv6 connectivity, even after recreating the whole swarm. Also still when adding the `--subnet="fd12:0000:$(< /dev/urandom tr -dc 'a-f0-9' | head -c 4):$(< /dev/urandom tr -dc 'a-f0-9' | head -c 4)::/64"` argument, the same error occurs: ``` time="2026-01-18T19:48:19.560681559Z" level=error msg="Failed to release pool fd12:0:fdd2:d18f::/64" error="address space does not contain specified address pool" time="2026-01-18T19:48:19.560736885Z" level=error msg="Failed allocation for network nyswzmqeefyzj1q2vmuddchvr" error="failed allocating pools and gateway IP for network nyswzmqeefyzj1q2vmuddchvr: cannot find address pool for poolID:fd12:0:fdd2:d18f::/64/invalid Prefix" module=node node.id=qclcuunq3fhii1aoa4iae45rx time="2026-01-18T19:48:19.847181958Z" level=error msg="Failed allocation for service 2bcw7aajotom9m6befueze1uo" error="networkallocator: could not find local network state" module=node node.id=qclcuunq3fhii1aoa4iae45rx time="2026-01-18T19:48:19.848232432Z" level=error msg="task allocation failure" error="service 2bcw7aajotom9m6befueze1uo to which task n28xifpn5hd01pbwqu83c2ifv belongs has pending allocations" module=node node.id=qclcuunq3fhii1aoa4iae45rx ``` Removing the argument and leaving only the `--ipv6` does nothing (the service starts without any IPv6 connectivity). although the IPv6 range changed for docker0 interface and veth interface is being created for every new service with link-local address.

author: alanpurple
title: dependency on @types/express-serve-static-core induced incompatibility
body: ## Environment information **Version**: express 5.2.1 @types/express 5.0.6 **Platform**: windows 11 x64 and ubuntu 24.04.3 **Node.js version**: 24.13.0 **Any other relevant information**: ## What steps will reproduce the bug? currently express typing have dependency "@types/express-serve-static-core": "^5.0.0", which installs @types/express-serve-static-core 5.1.1, which is incompatible works up to 5.1.0 so massive same errors like this ``` error TS2345: Argument of type 'string | string[]' is not assignable to parameter of type 'string'. Type 'string[]' is not assignable to type 'string'. 80 const id = parseInt(req.params.id); ``` generated and this makes the documentation on https://expressjs.com/en/5x/api.html useless, in that documentation, same "req.params.id" is used @types/express-serve-static-core should be fixed
discussion_title: dependency on @types/express-serve-static-core induced incompatibility

author: krzysdz
body: The TypeScript type definitions for Express are maintained as a part of the [DefinitelyTyped](https://github.com/DefinitelyTyped/DefinitelyTyped/) project and generally I'd suggest asking there about problems with `@types/` packages. The reason why your code broke is my recent PR https://github.com/DefinitelyTyped/DefinitelyTyped/pull/74161 that fixed the `req.params` type. Your problems are not caused by a package version mismatch. In Express 5, if you use a path with a wildcard, the parameter that you get is an array. This is documented in the [migration guide](https://expressjs.com/en/guide/migrating-5.html#req.params) and [`req.params` docs](https://expressjs.com/en/5x/api.html#req.params). If you use a specific path with parameters, the typings will try to parse it and set the correct `req.params` type - e.g.: ```ts app.get("/user/:id", (req, res) => { req.params.id // string } app.get("/file/*path", (req, res) => { req.params.path // string[] } ``` If you don't give any path, then the default type - `string | string[]`, which covers all possible types, will be used: ```ts const handler: RequestHandler = (req, res) => { req.params.id // string | string[] } ``` You can also declare the type of `req.params` yourself: ```ts const handler1: RequestHandler<{ id: string }> = (req, res) => { req.params.id // string } const handler2 = (req: Request<{ path: string[] }>, res: Response) => { req.params.path // string[] } ```
discussion_title: dependency on @types/express-serve-static-core induced incompatibility

author: pedro-w
title: connect-rid
body: In the docs, one of the middlewares mentioned is connect-rid. https://expressjs.com/en/resources/middleware/connect-rid.html But, connect-rid has not been updated since 2014 and seems to have been archived by its authors in May 2025. Is it a good idea to remove it from the middleware docs or replace it with something (I don't know what) that _is_ supported?
discussion_title: connect-rid

author: ismaildasci
body: Yeah, makes sense to remove it since it's archived. A few alternatives that could potentially replace it: - [`express-request-id`](https://github.com/floatdrop/express-request-id) - simple and straightforward, uses UUIDs - [`cls-rtracer`](https://github.com/puzpuzpuz/cls-rtracer) - more full-featured, uses AsyncLocalStorage so you can access the request ID anywhere in your call chain (great for logging) Both are reasonably maintained and have decent adoption. I'd lean toward `cls-rtracer` as a recommendation since it's the more modern approach and solves the "how do I get the request ID in my service layer" problem that people often run into.

author: SkypLabs
title: Middleware called for no reason
body: Hi, In a Node.js application using Express 4, I introduced a new custom middleware for handling user authentication. The middleware, called `authClient`, is only used in one single request handler and nowhere else: ```javascript router.get("/:clientId", authClient, async (req: Request, res: Response) => { ... } ``` However, when running integration tests, all tests related to testing the absence of path parameters, even in other request handlers attached to the same router, fail because they called `authClient`, which is not supposed to happen. Does anyone have any idea why? Thanks!
discussion_title: Middleware called for no reason

author: SkypLabs
body: Someone found the solution for me. Actually, the requests sent by the failing tests, even though they were supposed to test other request handlers, were already caught and processed by `router.get("/:clientId", ...` by mistake, even before I introduced the new `authClient` middleware. This was due to `router.get("/:clientId", ...` located at the beginning of the source code file, before the other router's request handlers. The solution was to move the `router.get("/:clientId", ...` request handler to the bottom of the source code file, and to turn mandatory path parameters into optional ones to make sure they still get caught by the right request handlers.
discussion_title: Middleware called for no reason

author: laggu
title: express-cargo — Simplify Request Data Handling in Express
body: Hello everyone, I’ve built a middleware for Express developers called express-cargo, designed to make request data handling effortless. Instead of manually dealing with req.body, req.query, and req.params, you can define a class and use decorators to map incoming request data automatically. It’s written in TypeScript and works seamlessly with decorators. **Features** - Declarative handling of body, query, params, and headers - Type-safe with full TypeScript support - Reduces boilerplate in route handlers - Built-in validation decorators for request data integrity **Installation** ``` npm install express-cargo reflect-metadata ``` **Example** ``` import express from 'express' import { body, bindingCargo, getCargo, min, header, params } from 'express-cargo' const app = express() app.use(express.json()) class RequestExample { @body() name!: string @body() @min(0) age!: number @params('id') id!: number @header() authorization!: string } app.post('/:id', bindingCargo(RequestExample), (req, res) => { const data = getCargo<RequestExample>(req) // write your code with bound data }) app.listen(3000) ``` **Link** - github : https://github.com/Beyond-Imagination/express-cargo - npm : https://www.npmjs.com/package/express-cargo

author: krzysdz
body: I don't think there is a good way to do it now. @bjohansebas is working on a PR that might help (pillarjs/router#174), but it is intended to return all paths of a router, so I'm not sure if it will be exactly what you want. If you don't mind some repetitions (and chance to make mistakes), you can add middleware functions that save (and restore) current prefix (that has to be specified manually...): ```js const express = require("express"); const app = express(); function trim(str, ch) { let start = 0; let end = str.length; while (start < str.length && str[start] === ch) ++start; while (end > start && str[end - 1] === ch) --end; return str.substring(start, end); } // This function must be called with a path and returns a middleware function // DON'T USE DIRECTLY AS MIDDLEWARE function pathPrefixSaver(path) { return (req, res, next) => { req.pathSoFar = req.pathSoFar || []; // Trim "/" for better path segment combining req.pathSoFar.push(trim(path, "/")); next(); } } // On the other hand this is just normal middleware, called when there was no handler for the given route // in a router that was called anyway due to a shared prefix. // If you design your code reasonably it might not be necessary. function pathPrefixPopper(req, res, next) { if (Array.isArray(req.pathSoFar)) req.pathSoFar.pop(); next(); } // Just join everything nicely. I'm not 100% sure if the `path` property of Route is a public API. function recoverFullPath(req) { return "/" + [...(req.pathSoFar || []), trim(req.route.path, "/")].join("/"); } const somethingRouter = express.Router(); somethingRouter.get('/my/:pathParam', (req, res) => { console.log(recoverFullPath(req)); // '/api/:something/my/:pathParam' res.send("ok"); }); // This one is here just to mount it under a shared prefix, to show why pathPrefixPopper may be necessary. const otherRouter = express.Router(); otherRouter.get('/asdf', (req, res) => { console.log(recoverFullPath(req)); res.send("ok"); }); app.use("/api", pathPrefixSaver("/api"), otherRouter, pathPrefixPopper); app.use("/api/:something", pathPrefixSaver("/api/:something"), somethingRouter, pathPrefixPopper); const server = app.listen(8080, async () => { await fetch("http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/param1/my/param2"); server.close(); }); ``` As noted in the code, I'm not sure if `req.route.path` is public API, since it is not documented in Express docs nor [`router`](https://github.com/pillarjs/router) README.
discussion_title: Get full route path

author: JLLeitschuh
title: Supporting HTTP/2: HTTP/1.1 must die
body: At BlackHat & DEF CON 33, James Kettle detailed that HTTP/1.1 contains fundamental desynchronization security vulnerabilities that researchers keep finding and will continue to find due to the poorly defined boundaries between requests. In that research, bug bounty hunters found $300k+ worth of bounties due to a novel HTTP Request Splitting vulnerability. This research is summarized here: https://http1mustdie.com/ James believes that there will continue to be new, and noverl HTTP request smuggling vulnerabilities due to the fundamental insecure nature of HTTP/1.1. His proposed mitigation is to encourage all backing servers to support HTTP/2. I know that a ticket on this topic #5462, however given that this research is new, and situationally relevant to express, it seems like it was appropriate to create a new issue.
discussion_title: Supporting HTTP/2: HTTP/1.1 must die

author: bjohansebas
body: I understand, but that still doesn’t fall under Express’ responsibilities. Express is just a wrapper to make it easier to create servers with Node.js, and we won’t stop supporting HTTP/1.1 (I’m not speaking on behalf of the team here). We haven’t achieved HTTP/2 yet, and QUIC is not yet available in Node.js. In the end, this would be more of a Node.js problem, but I don’t think they can do much about it either.
discussion_title: Supporting HTTP/2: HTTP/1.1 must die

author: bjohansebas
title: Stream support in res.send()
body: I think we should support sending streams as part of the res.send() API, allowing us to do the following: ```javascript const fs = require('node:fs') express.get('/streams', function (req, res) { const stream = fs.createReadStream('some-file', 'utf8') res.send(stream) }) ``` And along with that, support web streams so that it’s quite easy to use Blob, Response, and ReadableStream Could this be a breaking change? How much would it affect the middleware we maintain, for example, compression, which transforms the response? These are things that would need to be investigated, but I fully support this integration. Fastify has it, and so does Koa.

author: zeeshanmazhar
title: How can we implement a fully streaming-compatible middleware pipeline in Express without buffering the request/response?
body: I’m working on an app that needs to handle large file uploads and real-time transformations (e.g., video stream filters, compression) without buffering the entire payload in memory. Given that most Express middleware (e.g., body parsers, logging, error handling) tend to operate on fully-buffered request bodies, how can we build a fully streaming-compatible middleware pipeline in Express? Specific challenges: • Avoid memory spikes on large payloads • Keep compatibility with some Express features (e.g., next() handling, res.send) • Ensure backpressure is handled correctly Has anyone implemented or seen a pattern where middleware can work purely in streaming mode?
discussion_title: How can we implement a fully streaming-compatible middleware pipeline in Express without buffering the request/response?

author: Yasir-Rafique
body: Hey Zeeshan, I have run into this challenge before. Express by default (and most of its middleware) really aren't optimized for pure streaming scenarios. Following was what helped me solve it: - Avoid body parsers like express.json() or urlencoded(), since they buffer the whole payload. Instead, work directly with req as a readable stream. - Use libraries like busboy or multer (in stream mode) for uploads, or handle the stream yourself for full control. - For real-time transformations (like compression or video processing), pipe req through a series of stream.Transform instances. Hopefully Node.js streams naturally handle backpressure for you. - Middleware can be “streaming-friendly” if you design them to operate on the stream (not on a parsed body). You can even chain transforms with stream.pipeline() for better error and backpressure handling. - For error handling, make sure to catch stream errors so Express can handle them cleanly. - Logging middleware like morgan still works fine, since it just inspects headers, not the body. - As for next() and Express middleware pipeline: as long as you don’t consume or buffer the whole stream in an earlier middleware, downstream handlers can still operate on the stream. Hopefully this will help. It takes a bit of extra setup, but it’s totally doable!
discussion_title: How can we implement a fully streaming-compatible middleware pipeline in Express without buffering the request/response?

author: zeeshanmazhar
body: Thanks! Quick one — if I pipe req into a transform stream in one middleware, can another middleware after that still access req as a stream? Or once consumed, is it gone?
discussion_title: How can we implement a fully streaming-compatible middleware pipeline in Express without buffering the request/response?

author: Yasir-Rafique
body: Great question! In Node.js, request streams (req) are “consumable” once you pipe or read from them in a middleware, the data is gone and later middleware can’t re-read that stream. Basically, the stream doesn’t “reset” after it is been read. So if you need multiple transformations, you will want to chain them together in the same middleware (using something like stream.pipeline() or by piping through multiple Transform streams in one go), rather than trying to process req in separate, later middleware. If you must split logic across middleware, you would have to buffer the body yourself (which kinda defeats the purpose for large uploads). That is why for streaming use cases, it is best to set up all your transformations in a single pipeline early on. Hope that helps! Streams are powerful but definitely have their quirks.
discussion_title: How can we implement a fully streaming-compatible middleware pipeline in Express without buffering the request/response?

author: zeeshanmazhar
body: That makes perfect sense now — really appreciate the explanation. This clears up the stream consumption behavior and helps a lot with how I’ll structure my pipeline. Definitely helped with my use case. Thanks again!

author: ale-grosselle
title: req.query is now a getter and can't use as custom server in front of Next.js
body: When using Express as a custom server in front of Next.js, accessing req.query directly no longer returns a mutable plain object. Instead, it is now a getter, and attempts to modify it or use it as a plain object lead to unexpected behavior or errors. This change affects routes where the server modifies or depends on the mutability of req.query before passing it to Next.js, breaking compatibility with existing implementations. Next.js Issue: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/79158 --- ### Context We applied the following patch, but we do not believe it is the best solution: ```js // Express 5 makes the 'query' property read-only, but Next.js needs to write to it try { Object.defineProperty(req, 'query', descriptor); } catch (error) { // If property already exists and is configurable, delete and recreate it try { // eslint-disable-next-line @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any delete (req as any).query; Object.defineProperty(req, 'query', descriptor); logger.logInfo(`query property fixed ${error}`, requestID); } catch (error) { logger.logWarn( `Could not fix query property for Next.js compatibility: ${error}`, requestID ); } } ``` Is it possible to know why Express decided to change? This would help us understand where we need to make adjustments (on the Next.js side or the Express.js side).
discussion_title: req.query is now a getter and can't use as custom server in front of Next.js

author: bjohansebas
body: While I wasn't around at the time when that [commit](https://github.com/expressjs/express/commit/dcc4eaabe86a4309437db2a853c5ef788a854699) was made and possibly discussed, I found reasons (https://github.com/expressjs/express/issues/3472#issuecomment-341783875, https://github.com/expressjs/express/issues/2752#issuecomment-139111402) from the former maintainer for making this just a getter. The long-term solution is to use req.locals to store the query and modify it later, i'm not sure how this could work for Next.js, https://github.com/expressjs/express/issues/3472#issuecomment-341820984
discussion_title: req.query is now a getter and can't use as custom server in front of Next.js

author: raphaelweis
body: From what I understand, we shouldn't overwrite any of the properties of the request object, but only `req.query` is enforced. I'm ok with putting my validated data (header, path, body, query...) in `res.locals` but if I go look at the documentation for (`res.locals`)[https://expressjs.com/en/5x/api.html#res.locals] it doesn't say it's supposed to anything other that a place to put variables accessible to templates. Are we just not supposed to validate specifically `req.query` ?

author: Yasir-Rafique
body: You definitely can get automatic API docs with Express, though it works a bit differently than FastAPI, which has Swagger UI out of the box. In Express, there are two main approaches: JSDoc-style annotations: Packages like [swagger-jsdoc](https://www.npmjs.com/package/swagger-jsdoc) with [swagger-ui-express](https://www.npmjs.com/package/swagger-ui-express) let you add OpenAPI definitions right in your route comments. This way, you can keep docs close to your code, and the Swagger UI updates as you go. Downside: you do have to keep comments in sync with your code. Code scanning/generation: [swagger-autogen](https://www.npmjs.com/package/swagger-autogen) is great if you want to skip writing docs manually. It analyzes your code and generates the Swagger docs automatically. This is quick for prototypes, but you may need to tweak things for more complex validation or custom logic. If you just want to get up and running quickly, swagger-autogen is probably the fastest path. For bigger or longer-term projects, I personally find JSDoc comments with Swagger UI to be more robust, since it is easier for teams to maintain and review alongside the code. After adding either approach, you will get an interactive Swagger UI usually at /api-docs very similar to FastAPI’s experience where you can test endpoints right from the browser. So, Express does not bundle docs like FastAPI, but you can get a similar “try it out” experience with just a little setup using community packages.

body: Express does not support HTTP/2 (see #5462). The error caused by `http2-express-bridge` has been reported in the package's repository (https://github.com/rahulramesha/http2-express-bridge/issues/11), but it looks like that project has not been updated in a long time and is abandoned by its author.

author: Thyran
body: I think I found the cause of the error. The function `application.lazyrouter` was removed with version 5 which the http2 bridge used to hook prototype overwriting for Http2ServerRequest and -Response on the req and res objects before forwarding them to the router api. Since the `application.router` is now not accessible anymore before initialization and prototype overwriting happens in `application.handle` is there any other way to set Http2ServerRequest and -Response prototypes for req and res objects before `application.router.handle` is called?
